animism.name: Animism
animism.description: Animism is less a single religion than a way of being in the world: the conviction that spirit, agency, and personhood are not confined to human beings but extend to animals, plants, rivers, stones, weather, and sometimes to words and made objects. The term itself is an outsider's coinage. Edward Burnett Tylor introduced it in Primitive Culture (1871) to name what he took to be religion's earliest stratum, and he defined it as belief in innumerable spiritual beings who take an active interest in human affairs and can help or harm them. Tylor's evolutionary framing — animism as a first rung on a ladder climbing toward monotheism and then toward science — has been abandoned; no such developmental sequence has ever been demonstrated, and archaeologists now object that lumping the world's indigenous traditions under one label erases exactly the complexity that makes them religions at all. What survives is the descriptive core. Animist practice tends to be particularist rather than universal: spirits are local, plural, and independent of one another rather than arranged in a ruling pantheon, and the practical business of religion is avoiding trouble, securing food, healing illness, and negotiating safe passage. When trouble comes anyway, a diviner identifies the offended party. Recent anthropology has gone further, treating animism not as a mistaken belief about a disenchanted world but as a coherent ontology in its own right — a relational stance in which sentience is something one encounters and negotiates with, not something one attributes by error. Many communities described as animist have no word for either 'animism' or 'religion'; the worldview is embedded in kinship, land, healing, and ritual rather than set apart from them.

animism.godsNote: Animism is a category, not a pantheon. Its spirits are local and particular — a spirit of this river, this grove, this ancestor — and do not form a shared, named roster across cultures. Named deities appear in the traditions that descend from it (see yoruba, norse, aboriginal), not here.

polyEurope.name:
Europe
Polytheism
polyEurope.description: There is no single 'European polytheism' before the Indo-European traditions — only a long, thin trail of ritual behaviour inferred from what survives in the ground. Belief in the Lower and Middle Paleolithic is essentially unrecoverable; the archaeological record does not carry it, and honest accounts say so. From roughly 50,000 years ago the evidence thickens. The dead are buried in prepared holes with stone tools and animal parts, which is generally read as provisioning them for something after death; grave goods grow richer through the Upper Paleolithic and begin to track the status the dead held in life. Skulls in particular are singled out and deliberately placed, and the ritual treatment of them is confirmed for the Middle Paleolithic. Painted caves at Lascaux and Altamira give animals and geometric signs, often interpreted through a shamanic frame, though 'art for art's sake' and totemism have both been proposed and largely abandoned — the animals are shown wounded, which totems would not be. Bears recur as guardian or power figures from the Upper Paleolithic onward. What all this adds up to is a matter of argument, not settled fact, and reading it as an early rung on a ladder toward later religion is precisely the move modern scholarship warns against. Older claims of Paleolithic cannibalism as a marker of primitiveness have been abandoned outright.

polyEurope.godsNote: No named deities are recoverable. Any 'gods' assigned to Paleolithic Europe would be modern invention — the evidence consists of burials, bones, pigment, and painted animals, none of which carry names.

san.name:
San
Animism
san.description: The religious traditions of the San peoples of southern Africa turn on potency — a power that moves through animals, people, and the land, and that can be raised, felt, and directed. Its central rite is the healing or trance dance, in which women sit around a fire clapping and singing while the men dance, sometimes for hours, until the most experienced dancers cross into trance. In that state healers draw out sickness, drive off malevolent forces, influence weather, and travel to the spirit world; participants describe transforming into animals, an experience painted directly onto rock surfaces, where the stone itself was understood as a membrane between worlds and figures emerge from or vanish into its cracks. The eland is the most potent animal of all, and its fat and its imitation appear in boys' first-kill rites, girls' puberty rites, and marriage. The great figure of San story is /Kaggen, a trickster-creator most often associated with the praying mantis but not confined to it — he takes the form of an eland, a hare, a snake, a vulture, or an ordinary San man. He is by turns wise and foolish, generous and tiresome, and scholars disagree over whether he counts as a supreme god at all. Other San communities name a creator such as ǃXu and a rain power such as ǃKhwa, and speak of the spirits of the dead, vaguely identified and generally feared as bringers of sickness rather than venerated as ancestors. Practices vary considerably between San groups.

gods.name: /Kaggen
gods.description: The central figure of San story — creator, trickster, and shapeshifter, most often linked to the praying mantis but equally able to appear as an eland, hare, snake, or vulture, and to live as an ordinary San man when he is none of those. He is helpful and maddening by turns, and he is said to have given the people the dance itself. Whether he is a supreme god or simply the greatest of the story-beings is disputed.
gods.imageQuery: praying mantis San rock art southern Africa

gods.name: ǃXu
gods.description: A creator and supreme being named among several San groups, associated with the sky and with the making of the world and its creatures. Distant rather than intimate; the practical religious work happens in the dance, not in appeals to him.
gods.imageQuery: San Bushmen rock art figures Drakensberg

gods.name: ǃKhwa
gods.description: The rain power — in an arid land, the most consequential force there is. Rain-making was one of the healer's functions, and water and the rain-animal recur throughout San rock painting.
gods.imageQuery: San rock art rain animal eland

san.godsNote: San communities differ widely; these figures are not a unified pantheon and different groups name and rank them differently.

middleSham.name:
Middle East
Shamanism
middleSham.description: Before the temple cities of Sumer and Egypt, the ritual life of Southwest Asia is known through architecture, bones, and carved stone rather than texts. The Natufians of the Levant — semi-permanent hunter-gatherer settlements of roughly 12,500 to 9,500 BCE — demonstrate that people settled before they farmed, and it is against that background that Göbekli Tepe rises around 9,500 BCE in southeastern Anatolia: rings of monumental T-shaped pillars, carved with foxes, boars, snakes, vultures, and scorpions, and with human arms and clothing on the great central pairs, built by people who had not yet domesticated a crop. Tens of thousands of animal bones from the site indicate large-scale slaughter and feasting; who or what the offerings were for is unknown, though the anthropomorphic pillars have been read as ancestors, priests, or deities. The evidence overall points not to organized religion but to something closer to animism and shamanism — ecstatic practice, communication with spirits, and above all the veneration of ancestors, with skull cults well attested across the region. One influential and still-contested hypothesis inverts the usual story: that the need to feed the crowds who gathered for these rituals is what drove the first experiments in cultivating wild cereals, making religion a cause of agriculture rather than its product.

sources.title: The Göbekli Tepe Ruins and the Origins of Neolithic Religion
sources.mla: "The Göbekli Tepe Ruins and the Origins of Neolithic Religion." Biblical Archaeology Society, https://www.biblicalarchaeology.org/daily/ancient-cultures/ancient-near-eastern-world/the-gobekli-tepe-ruins-and-the-origins-of-neolithic-religion/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Göbekli Tepe: Discovering the World's Oldest Religious Site
sources.mla: "Göbekli Tepe: Discovering the World's Oldest Religious Site." Popular Archaeology, https://popular-archaeology.com/article/gobekli-tepe-discovering-the-worlds-oldest-religious-site/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

middleSham.godsNote: No names survive. The carved pillars of Göbekli Tepe may represent ancestors, priests, or deities — the site is pre-literate, and any name attached to them would be a guess.

centralAsian.name:
Central Asian
Shamanism
centralAsian.description: Across the steppe and the Siberian forest, ritual life has long been organized around a three-tiered cosmos — an upper world, the earth, and a world of darkness below — with a shaman (kam) able to travel between them and negotiate with the beings who live there. The worlds are not sealed off; they press constantly on one another. Above everything is Tengri, the Eternal Blue Sky, paired with the earth-mother Etügen, and the term tengri also covers a wider host of sky-spirits. Tengri-worship is attested early: the name shows up in Chinese chronicles as the sky god of the Xiongnu as far back as the fourth century BCE. It became the religion of the ruling class of the Turkic and Mongol steppe empires — Genghis Khan's mandate to rule was Tengri's will made visible in his victories — and the Huns and early Bulgars carried it into Europe. From the eighth century onward the steppe converted, to Manichaeism and Buddhism and then overwhelmingly to Islam, but conversion was rarely a clean replacement: ancestor veneration, sacred springs, offerings tied to trees, and juniper burned at graves persist inside a nominally Islamic frame. Shamanism was never entirely stamped out, and since the 1990s an organized Tengrist revival has spread through Buryatia, Sakha, Tuva, Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, and Tatarstan. Note that 'Tengrism' as a unified named religion is largely a modern coinage.

sources.title: Tengrism Is the Religion of Steppes and Nature
sources.mla: "Tengrism Is the Religion of Steppes and Nature." Central Asia Guide, https://central-asia.guide/tengrism/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Tengri
gods.description: The Eternal Blue Sky — sky father, creator of the visible and invisible world, and the source of a ruler's mandate. Khans conquered because Tengri had chosen them, and defeat meant the mandate had been withdrawn. The word also generalizes: there are said to be ninety-nine tengri, sky-spirits of the upper and lower worlds, with Tengri supreme among them.
gods.imageQuery: Tengri eternal blue sky Mongolia ovoo

gods.name: Etügen Eke
gods.description: Mother Earth, consort to the sky — fertile ground answering eternal sky. Her name is bound up with Siberian words for the shaman herself, and Umay in the Turkic world plays a comparable role as protector of mothers and children.

gods.name: Erlik
gods.description: Lord of the world of darkness beneath the earth, to whom the shaman must descend and bargain — the counterweight in the three-tiered cosmos, and the source of the sicknesses the shaman is called on to negotiate away.

eastSouth.name:
East / South Asian
Shamanism
eastSouth.description: 'East / South Asian shamanism' is an umbrella label rather than a religion — a modern grouping for the ritual traditions that preceded, and then continued underneath, the named religions of the region. What can be said with confidence is what its descendant traditions inherited. In China the wu appear in the Shang oracle-bone record, mediating with the powers that generate things: divining, exorcising, healing, and above all making rain. In Korea the mu — cognate to the Chinese wu — perform the same office, and the Korean and Chinese traditions share obvious ground with Japanese Shinto, Ainu religion, and the Siberian and Manchurian complex to the north. Common features run through the whole area: ancestors who remain members of the family and must be fed; spirits attached to particular mountains, rivers, and trees; a specialist who enters altered states to bargain on the community's behalf; and healing understood as the repair of a broken relationship rather than the treatment of a body. Scholars increasingly resist the word 'shamanism' as a single category here, since the practices differ sharply across the region and the term imports a Siberian model onto traditions that may not fit it.

sources.title: Shamanism Theory and the Early Chinese Wu
sources.author: Michael, Thomas.
sources.container: Journal of the American Academy of Religion, vol. 83, no. 3
sources.mla: Michael, Thomas. "Shamanism Theory and the Early Chinese Wu." Journal of the American Academy of Religion, vol. 83, no. 3, https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280953138_Shamanism_Theory_and_the_Early_Chinese_Wu. Accessed 14 July 2026.

eastSouth.godsNote: An umbrella category, not a tradition with a pantheon. Named deities belong to the specific descendant traditions — see chineseSham, muism, shinto, bon.

fosna.name:
Fosna
Shamanism
fosna.description: Fosna is an archaeological culture, not a documented religion. The Fosna–Hensbacka complex (c. 8300–7300 BCE) names the earliest coastal settlements of Scandinavia after the retreat of the ice — Fosna and Komsa along the Norwegian coast, Hensbacka along western Sweden — and the finds are almost entirely lithic: flake axes, lanceolates, tanged arrowheads, and the debris of making them. These were sea-facing hunter-gatherers who lived off seal hunting and fishing and almost certainly used hide-covered boats, and their settlements now sit sixty to seventy metres above the water because the land itself has risen. Of their beliefs, nothing is directly attested. What is available is inference from the wider Fennoscandian record — rock art at sites such as Alta, where engraved and painted figures include reindeer, geometric and abstract signs, and anthropomorphic figures whose attributes resemble later shamanic dress, and which some researchers now date earlier than previously assumed, possibly into the Mesolithic. Shamanic readings of that art offer a cosmological frame; they remain interpretations, not evidence, and this entry should be understood as a placeholder for a people whose religion we do not have.

sources.title: Rock Art in Northern Fennoscandia and Eurasia
sources.author: Helskog, Knut.
sources.mla: Helskog, Knut. "Rock Art in Northern Fennoscandia and Eurasia." Academia.edu, https://www.academia.edu/20000772/Rock_art_in_Northern_Fennoscandia_and_Eurasia_painted_and_engraved_geometric_abstract_and_antropomorphic_figures. Accessed 14 July 2026.

fosna.godsNote: None recoverable. Fosna is defined by stone tools; no religious content is directly attested for it.

paleoIndian.name:
Paleo-Indian
Shamanism
paleoIndian.description: The religion of the first peoples of the Americas is known almost entirely through one grave and a quantity of red ochre. The Anzick site in Montana holds the only Clovis burial ever found: a young child, radiocarbon dated to roughly 12,600 years ago, interred with more than a hundred stone and bone objects — biface fragments, projectile points, flake tools, blades, bone rods — and coated in red ochre. Ochre is the thread running through the whole period. It appears in caches, in kill sites, and on the finest points, and it was carried long distances; Powars II in Wyoming, the oldest known red ochre quarry in North America, was worked around 12,850 years ago. Clovis points themselves are worked with a symmetry and care that exceeds any functional requirement, and caches such as Fenn and East Wenatchee — ochre-coated, deliberately deposited — suggest they carried meaning beyond use. At Caradoc in Ontario, at least seventy-one artifacts were deliberately smashed, evidence of ritual destruction. The Anzick child's genome ties these people to Siberia, and to the 24,000-year-old Mal'ta boy in particular, placing them within the same broad northern Eurasian world whose later inheritors practised shamanism. Beyond burial, ochre, and the treatment of stone, their beliefs are not recoverable.

sources.title: The Clovis Culture of North America
sources.mla: "The Clovis Culture of North America." Evolve, https://www.evolve2.org/blog/everything-we-know-about-the-clovis-culture. Accessed 14 July 2026.

paleoIndian.godsNote: No named deities survive. Later Indigenous North American traditions have their own — see plains, pueblo, iroquois, inuit — but projecting those backward onto Clovis would be a fabrication.

aboriginal.name:
Aboriginal
Dreamtime
aboriginal.description: The Dreaming — Tjukurrpa in Arrernte and Pitjantjatjara, with different names in each of the hundreds of Aboriginal language groups — is not a time that ended. It is what one writer calls the Everywhen: a creation period with a beginning but no foreseeable end, in which ancestral beings emerged from the earth, the sea, and the sky and travelled across a featureless country, fighting, making love, and changing shape, and in doing so raised the mountains, cut the rivers, dug the waterholes, and brought into being every animal and plant species. When their work was done they went back into the land, the water, and the sky — but they did not leave. They are spiritually as alive now as they ever were, and the places where they acted or turned into something else became sacred, with ritual focused on them. The routes they walked are songlines, sung across enormous distances. The Dreaming supplies not only the origin of the landscape but the law: kinship, marriage, initiation, food distribution, death ritual, totemic identity, and the ecological duties a person owes their own country. The term 'Dreamtime' was coined by the anthropologist Francis Gillen around 1896, because Arrernte uses one word for both 'dream' and 'the creation of the world'; today 'the Dreaming' or the specific language-group term is preferred. Early Europeans, looking for churches and priests and finding none, concluded that Aboriginal people had no religion. They were wrong.

sources.title: The Dreaming
sources.author: Berndt, Ronald M.
sources.mla: Berndt, Ronald M. "The Dreaming." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/the-Dreaming-Australian-Aboriginal-mythology. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Australian Aboriginal Religion and Mythology
sources.mla: "Australian Aboriginal Religion and Mythology." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Australian_Aboriginal_religion_and_mythology. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: The Dreaming
sources.mla: "The Dreaming." Working with Indigenous Australians, http://www.workingwithindigenousaustralians.info/content/Culture_2_The_Dreaming.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Rainbow Serpent
gods.description: The most widely distributed of the ancestral beings — an enormous snake who carved rivers and gorges in its passage and who lives on in deep waterholes. It gives water and life, and takes them: disturb its places and it answers with flood, sickness, or drought. Names and stories differ by region, and it is not one being so much as a figure recognisable across many countries.
gods.imageQuery: Rainbow Serpent Aboriginal rock art Arnhem Land

gods.name: Baiame
gods.description: A sky-dwelling creator and law-giver of southeastern Australia — Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, Wonnarua country — who made the land and the people, gave the laws and the initiation rites, and then returned to the sky. He is painted in ochre in rock shelters such as Baiame Cave with arms stretched impossibly wide.
gods.imageQuery: Baiame Cave rock art New South Wales

gods.name: Wandjina
gods.description: Cloud and rain spirits of the Kimberley in northwestern Australia, painted on rock walls with great haloed heads, huge eyes, and no mouths. They are custodians of the country and bringers of the wet season, and their images must be periodically repainted by those with the right to do so.

gods.name: Bunjil
gods.description: The wedge-tailed eagle creator of the Kulin nations of what is now Victoria, who made the land, the animals, and the first people, and then went up into the sky. His brother Waa the crow forms the other half of the moiety system that orders Kulin kinship.

aboriginal.godsNote: There are hundreds of Aboriginal nations with distinct traditions. These ancestral beings belong to particular countries and language groups; none is universal across Australia, and 'god' is an imperfect translation for what they are.

newGuinea.name:
New-Guinea
Animism
newGuinea.description: New Guinea holds more than eight hundred living languages, and its traditional religions are correspondingly plural — not one system but hundreds of local cosmologies, each tied to a particular valley, river, and clan. What they share is a working assumption that the landscape is inhabited. Masalai — a Tok Pisin word taken from New Hanover — are spirits that own specific places: a deep waterhole, a bend in a river, a cliff, a bog, a stand of dark forest. Margaret Mead described them as holding limited jurisdiction over their own ground, guarding it and punishing those who enter unannounced or in violation of taboo. They shapeshift, often into snakes, crocodiles, lizards, or cassowaries, and they can help or ruin a hunt. In New Ireland they also function as clan totems, animal ancestors marking out the 'skin and blood' shared by a matrilineage. Alongside them stand the ancestors, who have not left: they watch, they eat the first portion of the feast, they bless a household that honours them and send sickness to one that does not. Ritual life ran through elaborate male initiations, ancestor figures — the Iatmul of the Middle Sepik built them around actual skulls, shells, and hair — and Malagan carvings made for funerary rites. Sorcery (sanguma, puripuri) is feared and remains a live social force. Missionary conversion from the 1870s, accelerating sharply after the Second World War, made the country overwhelmingly Christian, but syncretism is the norm rather than the exception; the masalai did not go anywhere.

sources.title: Religion in Papua New Guinea
sources.mla: "Religion in Papua New Guinea." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religion_in_Papua_New_Guinea. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Religious Beliefs in Papua New Guinea
sources.mla: "Religious Beliefs in Papua New Guinea." WorldAtlas, https://www.worldatlas.com/articles/religious-beliefs-in-papua-new-guinea.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Masalai
gods.description: Not gods but place-spirits, and the central category of Melanesian religious life. Each owns a particular feature — a waterhole, a rock, a stretch of bush — and enforces the rules of entry to it. They take animal form, most often snake, crocodile, lizard, or cassowary. Cross one and you get sickness, a lost soul, or worse; keep the protocol and you may get protection and power.
gods.imageQuery: Sepik river spirit carving Papua New Guinea

gods.name: Ancestral spirits
gods.description: The dead remain part of the community, watching and judging. They are fed the first portion at feasts and consulted through dreams and divination; when honoured they bring fertility and prosperity, and when neglected they bring illness. The Iatmul built ancestor figures incorporating real skulls, shells, and hair to house them.

gods.name: Imunu
gods.description: Among Papuan peoples, the power that pervades things — including ritual objects. Less a personality than a charge that objects and places carry, closer to Melanesian mana than to any named deity.

newGuinea.godsNote: With 800+ language groups there is no shared pantheon. These are categories of spirit power, not a unified list of gods, and any single 'New Guinea pantheon' would misrepresent the region.
imageQueries: Papua New Guinea Sepik spirit house
Papua New Guinea sing-sing ceremony masks

protoIndo.name:
Proto-Indo
Europe
Polytheism
protoIndo.description: The Proto-Indo-European religion is not attested — it is reconstructed. Its speakers were preliterate, so comparative mythologists work backward from the inherited similarities among the traditions that descend from them: Vedic, Roman, and Norse principally, with Greek, Celtic, Baltic, Slavic, Hittite, Armenian, and Albanian evidence in support. When the same god appears in Greek, Latin, and Sanskrit with a name that obeys the regular sound laws, the inference is that the name is inherited rather than borrowed. The most securely reconstructed figure by far is *Dyḗws Ph₂tḗr, Sky Father — the daylit sky conceived as a divine being and as the dwelling of the gods — surviving as Zeus Pater, Jupiter, and Dyaus Pitar. He was probably not the ruler of the pantheon and probably did not police justice, but he was 'all-seeing' and served as witness to oaths. Around him: the earth mother *Dʰéǵʰōm; the dawn goddess *H₂éusōs, who becomes Eos, Aurora, and Uṣas; the divine twins, often horse-associated; and the storm god *Perkʷunos, the Striker, an oak-and-thunder warrior recoverable from Perkūnas, Perun, Pērkons, and Parjanya. Reconstruction is contested at every level — Mallory and Adams reject deities that others accept — and alternative frameworks exist, most famously Georges Dumézil's trifunctional hypothesis, which reads the pantheon as sorted into priestly, warrior, and productive functions. Archaeology has proven very hard to align with any of it.

gods.name: *Dyḗws Ph₂tḗr
gods.domain: The daylit sky, oaths, the dwelling of the gods
gods.description: The best-attested reconstructed deity of the whole family — the bright sky itself, personified, and the gateway to the gods. His name descends directly into Zeus Pater, Jupiter, Dyaus Pitar, and Tyr. Described as all-seeing and wide-visioned. Notably, he was probably not the supreme ruler that Zeus and Jupiter later became, nor the guarantor of justice; he witnessed oaths rather than enforcing them.
gods.imageQuery: Zeus Jupiter sky father ancient sculpture

gods.name: *Perkʷunos
gods.description: The storm god, whose name means the Striker — a thunder-wielding warrior associated with oak trees and with the defence of order against chaos. Reconstructed from Lithuanian Perkūnas, Slavic Perun, Latvian Pērkons, Norse Fjǫrgyn (Thor's mother, with Thor taking over the function), and Vedic Parjanya, though that last connection is disputed.

gods.name: *H₂éusōs
gods.description: The dawn goddess, a youthful maiden who heralds the light each morning — daughter of Dyēws, and among the most confidently reconstructed figures in the pantheon. She survives as Greek Eos, Roman Aurora, Vedic Uṣas, and Baltic Aušrinė.
gods.imageQuery: Eos Aurora dawn goddess classical art

gods.name: *Dʰéǵʰōm
gods.description: The wide flat earth, consort of the Sky Father — the pairing of sky and earth, rain and furrow, is one of the oldest structures in the family. She surfaces as Vedic Pṛthivī, Greek Demeter (contested), Slavic Mati Zemlya, and Lithuanian Žemyna.
gods.imageQuery: earth mother goddess ancient figurine Europe

gods.name: The Divine Twins
gods.domain: Horses, rescue, the morning and evening star
gods.description: Twin sons of the Sky Father, closely associated with horses and with rescue at sea and in battle. They descend into the Vedic Aśvins, the Greek Dioscuri (Castor and Pollux), the Baltic Dieva dēli, and probably the founding twins of Rome.
gods.imageQuery: Dioscuri Castor Pollux twin horsemen relief

protoIndo.godsNote: Every name here is a linguistic reconstruction marked with an asterisk, not an attested deity. Scholars disagree over which figures are securely reconstructible; Mallory and Adams reject several that others accept.

berber.name:
Berber
Animism
berber.description: The traditional religions of the Amazigh (Berber) peoples of Northwest Africa — sometimes called Libyco-Berber religions — were plural and local rather than a single system, and they both absorbed and fed into the beliefs of everyone who passed through the Maghreb: Egyptian, Punic, Greek, and Roman. In a dry land, rain and water dominate. Springs, caves, and groves held spirits, and the practice of ancestor veneration is a defining feature: tombs were sacred, and women in particular slept in ancestral tombs to receive guidance in dreams — much of the tradition was carried through female knowledge and custom. Some of the gods later famous elsewhere may run the other way: Amun was honoured at the Siwa Oasis as a ram-headed god of hidden things and prophecy, and Amazigh chiefs claimed descent from him; Alexander the Great made the journey to consult his oracle. After Carthage, Phoenician influence brought Baal, who fused with Ammon into Baal-Hammon, and Tanit, who was taken up widely across Berber lands. Gurzil, a war god represented as a bull or bull-headed warrior and said to be the son of Amun and a cow, was carried into battle by the chiefs of the Laguatan against the Byzantines; his sanctuary was probably at Ghirza in Libya and was still in use in the sixth century. Islam arrived in the seventh century and, after roughly a century of resistance, prevailed — but pre-Islamic practice survived inside it: belief in djinn, the evil eye, saint veneration, and offerings at sacred springs. As late as the eleventh century al-Bakrī records Berber tribes worshipping a stone idol named Kurzah.

sources.title: Amazigh Religion in Libya
sources.mla: "Amazigh Religion in Libya." Libyan Heritage House, https://libyanheritagehouse.org/religion/amazigh-religion-in-libya. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Gurzil
gods.description: A war god depicted as a bull or a bull-headed warrior, said to be the son of Amun and a cow. The Laguatan carried his image into battle against the Byzantines and the Arabs, and his high priest was their chief; his sanctuary at Ghirza in Libya was still functioning in the sixth century. He became a symbol of Berber revolt against foreign rule.

gods.name: Anzar
gods.description: The rain-bringer, best known in Kabyle folklore — a sky lord who descends to earth so that crops grow and herds thrive. The story tells of his love for a mortal woman, and of the tears that became the first rain. In drought, women still sing to Anzar to coax the clouds.

gods.name: Amun
gods.description: Honoured at the Siwa Oasis as a ram-headed god of the hidden and of prophecy, long before — and arguably independently of — his ascent in Egypt. Amazigh chiefs claimed descent from him, and Alexander the Great crossed the desert to consult his oracle there.
gods.imageQuery: Siwa Oasis Oracle of Amun temple

gods.name: Tanit
gods.description: Chief goddess of Carthage and consort of Baal-Hammon, taken up widely by the Amazigh. A moon goddess and patron of fertility, shown with arms outstretched beneath a crescent, and associated with sacred springs and palm groves. Often equated with Astarte.

gods.name: Antaeus
gods.domain: The earth, strength, defence of territory
gods.description: Known to the interior Libyan Berbers as Anti, and to the Greeks as the giant Antaeus who drew his strength from contact with the earth. An earth-shaking guardian of the land, invoked in defence of territory against outsiders.

yoruba.name:
Yoruba
Animism
yoruba.description: The Yoruba tradition — called Ìṣẹ̀ṣẹ by its practitioners, 'the tradition of antiquity' — divides the cosmos into Ọ̀run, the invisible realm, and Ayé, the earth. Above everything is Olódùmarè, the supreme being, source of all creation and holder of àṣẹ, the divine authority and life-force that runs through everything living and inanimate alike and is best translated as the power to make things happen. Olódùmarè is not male or female, has no shrines and receives no direct prayer, and is too immense to be approached; instead, aspects of that immensity were released into the world as the òrìṣà, conventionally numbered 400+1 as a way of saying 'beyond counting'. Some òrìṣà are primordial; others are ancestral, kings and founders and warriors who did not die but 'disappeared' and joined the pantheon, which is why some are venerated only in their own towns and families. The moral centre of the tradition is ìwà — character — and the pursuit of ìwà pẹ̀lẹ́, gentle good character, is the route to àṣẹ. Its scripture is the Odù Ifá, 256 sections of oracular verse held in oral tradition by the babaláwo and iyanifa, and named by UNESCO in 2005 as a Masterpiece of the Oral and Intangible Heritage of Humanity. Carried across the Atlantic by the slave trade, the òrìṣà became the foundation of Santería in Cuba, Candomblé and Umbanda in Brazil, and Trinidad Orisha, where they were hidden behind the faces of Catholic saints so that they could survive.

sources.title: Orisha
sources.author: Mark, Joshua J.
sources.mla: Mark, Joshua J. "Orisha." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Orisha/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Yemonja
sources.author: Canson, Patricia E.
sources.mla: Canson, Patricia E. "Yemonja." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Yemonja. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Olódùmarè
gods.description: The supreme being and origin of everything that exists — òrìṣà, humans, animals, forces. Neither male nor female, and referred to as 'it' as often as 'he'. Olódùmarè gave èmí, the breath of life, and is the owner of all heads. Too vast to be worshipped directly: there are no shrines and no rituals addressed to Olódùmarè, and all approach is made through the òrìṣà.

gods.name: Ọbàtálá
gods.domain: Creation, purity, the shaping of humans
gods.description: Olódùmarè's right hand — the òrìṣà who created the solid earth and moulded the first human bodies from clay, into which Olódùmarè breathed life. Associated with white cloth, purity, calm, and the moral obligation owed to the disabled, whom tradition holds he shaped while drunk on palm wine.

gods.name: Ọ̀rúnmìlà
gods.description: The òrìṣà of wisdom and prophecy, creator of the Ifá divination system and witness to every person's destiny — praised as second in command to Olódùmarè. His priests, the babaláwo, read the Odù Ifá to find out what a situation truly is and what sacrifice will change it.
gods.imageQuery: Ifa divination board opon Ifa Yoruba

gods.name: Èṣù
gods.description: The messenger and the trickster — the one who carries offerings up and answers down, who stands at every crossroads and every doorway, and who must be greeted first in any ritual. He tests people, upends the confident, and delights in the gap between what was said and what was meant. Missionaries mistook him for the devil; he is not.

gods.name: Ògún
gods.description: The òrìṣà of iron and of everything made with it — the smith, the hunter, the soldier, and now the surgeon, the mechanic, and the driver. He cleared the first path from heaven to earth with his machete. Fierce, direct, and dangerous, but the patron of anyone whose work is done with metal.

gods.name: Ṣàngó
gods.domain: Thunder, lightning, justice, the double axe
gods.description: A historical king of Ọ̀yọ́ who became the òrìṣà of thunder — hot-tempered, magnificent, and associated with the double-headed axe, with drums, and with dance. He hurls thunderstones, and lightning striking a house is read as his judgement. One of the most widely venerated òrìṣà in the Americas.
gods.imageQuery: Shango Yoruba thunder god double axe

gods.name: Ọ̀ṣun
gods.description: Goddess of the Osun river in Nigeria, and of sweetness, beauty, love, and prosperity. Protector of women and children, and the one women turn to over difficulty in bearing children. Her sacred grove at Osogbo is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

gods.name: Yemọja
gods.domain: Motherhood, rivers and the sea, fertility
gods.description: The metaphysical mother of all the òrìṣà and giver of life. Her name comes from words meaning 'mother whose children are the fish'. Charged by Olódùmarè to help Ọbàtálá form human beings, she is patron of women, of childbirth, and of those who work and travel on water. In Brazil, Iemanjá draws enormous crowds to the shore each February.

gods.name: Ọya
gods.domain: Wind, storms, the Niger river, the dead
gods.description: The òrìṣà of the whirlwind and the tornado, of the Niger river, and of the gate of the cemetery — the force of sudden, total change. Warrior, and the only one who can stand with Ṣàngó in a storm.

gods.name: Olókun
gods.description: The primordial owner of the waters, ruler of the ocean depths — vast, unfathomable, and not always well disposed toward the dry land that displaced the waters or the humans who live on it. Yemọja's counterpart: she is the surface and the river, Olókun is the abyss.

yoruba.godsNote: The òrìṣà are traditionally numbered 400+1 — shorthand for 'innumerable'. This is a selection of the most widely venerated, not a complete pantheon; many òrìṣà are local to a single town or family.
imageQueries: Ifa divination tray and palm nuts

bantu.name:
Bantu
Animism
bantu.description: The Bantu-speaking peoples span central, eastern, and southern Africa and number in the hundreds of millions, so 'Bantu religion' names a family of traditions rather than one. Across that family a recurring structure appears: a supreme creator who is real, acknowledged, and largely absent, and a dense, active world of ancestors and spirits through which almost all religious business is actually conducted. The creator's names share a common architecture — Nzambi Mpungu among the Bakongo, Mulungu among the Yao and Chewa, Unkulunkulu among the Zulu, Leza among the Lamba, Modimo among the Sotho and Tswana, Mwari among the Shona, Ruhanga among the Banyoro, Ngai among the Kikuyu, Imana among the Banyarwanda. He is typically distant, sometimes identified with the sun or with the oldest of all ancestors, often said to have withdrawn upward after human beings became intolerable, and in many traditions is not credited with creating the earth at all — the earth is simply held to have been there first. What fills the space he vacated are the ancestors, the 'living dead', who remain members of the family: they enforce ethical conduct, send blessing or misfortune, appear in dreams, and are sustained by offerings, while nature spirits govern rain, fertility, and health, and witchcraft explains the harm that has no other explanation. Bakongo cosmology develops this into a formal geometry — Kalûnga, the force of change, divides the cosmos into Ku Nseke, the physical world, and Ku Mpèmba, the world of the ancestors below, with life understood as perpetual motion between them.

sources.title: Bantu Mythology
sources.mla: "Bantu Mythology." EBSCO Research Starters, https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/language-and-linguistics/bantu-mythology. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: A History of the Traditional Beliefs and Practices of Africa's Bantu People
sources.author: McIntosh, Matthew A.
sources.mla: McIntosh, Matthew A. "A History of the Traditional Beliefs and Practices of Africa's Bantu People." Brewminate, https://brewminate.com/a-history-of-the-traditional-beliefs-and-practices-of-africas-bantu-people/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Nzambi Mpungu
gods.description: The supreme god of the Bakongo — self-existent, all-knowing, identified with the sun, and described as made by no one. He is also Kalûnga, the force of change: in Bakongo cosmology Kalûnga grew until it burst into energy and formed the universe, and it draws the line that divides the physical world above from the ancestral world below. Having created, he withdrew.
gods.imageQuery: Kongo nkisi power figure Central Africa

gods.name: Mulungu
gods.description: The creator god of the Yao, Chewa, Kamba, Nyamwezi and many others across East and Central Africa — responsible for making everything, and then withdrawing to the sky and ceasing to interfere. Prayer for daily needs goes to the ancestors instead.

gods.name: Unkulunkulu
gods.description: The Zulu creator — 'the ancient one', who came out of the reeds, was the first man, and made all things. He sits exactly where the Bantu high god so often does: on the boundary between creator deity and original ancestor. Christian missionaries later took the name for the God of the Bible.

gods.name: Leza
gods.description: The supreme god of the Lamba and neighbouring peoples of Zambia — controller of the weather and giver of the moral law, who communicates through dreams and visions rather than through priests.

gods.name: Mwari
gods.description: The supreme creator of Shona tradition — author of all things and all life, in whom all things are held. Approached through spirit mediums and through the ancestral spirits (vadzimu) rather than directly.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: Not gods but the true centre of religious practice. The dead remain members of the lineage — the 'living dead' — enforcing right conduct, blessing those who honour them with fertility and prosperity, and afflicting those who do not. They are fed with offerings and answer through dreams, illness, and the diviner.

bantu.godsNote: There is no single Bantu pantheon. These are the recurring high-god names across hundreds of distinct traditions; in most of them the ancestors, not the creator, are the focus of practice.

nilotic.name:
Nilotic
Animism
nilotic.description: The religions of the Nilotic peoples of the upper Nile — the Dinka, Nuer, Shilluk and their neighbours — are the subject of two of the most admired ethnographies ever written: E. E. Evans-Pritchard's Nuer Religion (1956) and Godfrey Lienhardt's Divinity and Experience: The Religion of the Dinka (1961). Both describe traditions organized around a single high god who created the world and who is at once transcendent and immanent. The Nuer call him Kwoth — a word that also means spirit, or breath, or wind — or Kwoth Nhial, spirit of the sky; the Dinka say Nhialic. He is omnipotent, everlasting, everywhere, and beyond ordinary human comprehension or control. He is also remote: both traditions preserve myths of the separation of people from the sky, of a rope cut or a closeness lost. Yet he remains involved and may intervene at any moment, so prayers and offerings are made to him continually and informally, and he is never far from anyone's thoughts. Beneath him are lesser spirits, often understood as emanations of the high god rather than as rivals to him, along with ancestral and nature powers. Cattle are the medium of everything: the sacrifice of oxen by the Dinka 'masters of the fishing spear' is the central act of Dinka religious life, and the relation between people, herds, rain, and divinity is a single fabric. The tradition also produced prophets — Ngundeng among the Nuer, who built an earthen pyramid to prophesy from, and his son Gwek, who stood on it in possession and foretold the end of colonial rule.

sources.title: Nuer and Dinka Religion
sources.author: Middleton, John.
sources.mla: Middleton, John. "Nuer and Dinka Religion." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/nuer-and-dinka-religion. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: East African Religions: An Overview
sources.author: Middleton, John.
sources.mla: Middleton, John. "East African Religions: An Overview." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/east-african-religions-overview. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Nilotic Society, Religion and Moral Values In
sources.author: Deng, Francis M.
sources.container: Encyclopedia of African Religions and Philosophy, Springer
sources.mla: Deng, Francis M. "Nilotic Society, Religion and Moral Values In." Encyclopedia of African Religions and Philosophy, Springer, https://link.springer.com/rwe/10.1007/978-94-024-2068-5_282. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Kwoth
gods.domain: Spirit, breath, the sky, creation, moral order
gods.description: The supreme spirit of the Nuer — the word means spirit or breath, and he is described as being like wind or air: immaterial, dwelling in the sky yet permeating the earth. He created the universe and everything in it and enforces moral order, so that misfortune, illness, and death are read as his action. He is the world's owner and may take back what he gave.

gods.name: Nhialic
gods.description: The Dinka supreme being, whose name may be rendered as 'sky' — creator and head of the Dinka pantheon of spirits, generally distant from human beings and not interacting with them directly, but the ultimate source and the ultimate explanation.

gods.name: Deng
gods.description: A major Dinka divinity associated with rain and lightning, and one of the powers Nuer prophets have also claimed inspiration from — the prophet Wutnyang Gatakek invoked both Kwoth Nhial and Deng during the Sudanese civil war.

gods.name: Abuk
gods.description: The first woman in Dinka tradition and the patron of women's work — of gardens, of grain, and of the crops. Her emblem is a little snake.

gods.name: Juok
gods.description: The high god of the Shilluk, another Nilotic people of the Nile — a creator whose name recurs across the region with the same broad profile of omnipotence and distance.

nilotic.godsNote: The lesser spirits in these traditions are often understood as emanations of the high god rather than as independent deities — the boundary between 'one god' and 'many' is drawn differently here than in European categories.

egyptian.name:
Egyptian
Polytheism
egyptian.description: The religion of ancient Egypt ran, in one form or another, for well over three thousand years — from the fourth millennium BCE into the first centuries of the common era — and it absorbed both the elaborate court cult and the household practices of ordinary people. Its gods began as local powers attached to particular towns along the Nile and were progressively synthesized into national deities after political unification around 2925 BCE, which is why so many of them overlap, merge, and trade attributes with one another. The Egyptian word for god, netjer, covered a far wider range of beings than the term does in monotheistic usage; the gods were not omnipotent or omniscient, merely immeasurably greater than human beings, and their characters were never sharply fixed. Which god stood highest changed with the era: the sun god Ra, the hidden god Amun, the mother goddess Isis. Amun's priesthood at Thebes eventually grew wealthy enough to rival the throne itself. Binding the whole system together was maat — order, truth, right proportion — which the king existed to uphold on both the social and the cosmic scale, and which the dead were measured against. Egyptian religion is best known today for its treatment of death: mummification preserved the body because the body was needed on the other side, and the deceased faced judgment before Osiris, with those who failed condemned to die a second and final time.

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Religion
sources.author: Baines, John R.
sources.mla: Baines, John R. "Ancient Egyptian Religion." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/ancient-Egyptian-religion. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Gods
sources.author: Mark, Joshua J.
sources.mla: Mark, Joshua J. "Ancient Egyptian Gods." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/collection/194/ancient-egyptian-gods/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Deities
sources.mla: "Ancient Egyptian Deities." Wikipedia, Wikimedia Foundation, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_deities. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Ra
gods.description: The sun god, and for long stretches of Egyptian history the head of the pantheon. Ra sails across the sky by day and passes through the underworld by night, where he must be defended against the serpent Apophis so that dawn can happen at all. Creator gods were routinely fused with him — Amun-Ra, Ra-Horakhty — which is less confusion than a way of saying that supreme power and solar power were the same thing.

gods.name: Osiris
gods.description: Lord of the underworld and judge of the dead. Murdered by his brother Set, dismembered, and reassembled by his wife Isis, Osiris became the god who has already died and returned — which is precisely why the Egyptian dead hoped to become him. It is before Osiris that the heart is weighed.

gods.name: Isis
gods.description: Wife of Osiris, mother of Horus, and the most formidable magician among the gods — she reassembles her murdered husband and hides her son until he can claim his throne. Her cult outgrew Egypt entirely, spreading across the Greco-Roman world, and in the last millennium BCE she stood as the greatest of the goddesses.

gods.name: Horus
gods.description: The falcon-headed sky god, whose eyes are the sun and moon. Son of Osiris and Isis, he avenges his father against Set and thereby becomes the model of legitimate rule: every living pharaoh was Horus on earth, and every dead one became Osiris.
gods.imageQuery: Horus falcon god Edfu temple statue

gods.name: Set
gods.description: The god of the red land beyond the fields — of storm, violence, foreignness, and disorder. He murders Osiris and wars with Horus. But he is not straightforwardly evil: Set also rides in Ra's night barque and kills Apophis, which is to say Egypt understood that the force which threatens order is also the force that defends it.
gods.imageQuery: Set Egyptian god Seth animal relief

gods.name: Anubis
gods.domain: Mummification, cemeteries, the passage of the dead
gods.description: The jackal-headed god who presides over embalming and guards the necropolis — a jackal because jackals haunted the desert graveyards, and it was better to have that animal on your side. In the judgment hall he operates the scales on which the heart is weighed against the feather of maat.
gods.imageQuery: Anubis jackal god weighing of the heart

gods.name: Thoth
gods.description: The ibis-headed scribe of the gods, who invented writing, keeps the record, measures time, and records the verdict at the weighing of the heart. Where Ra is authority, Thoth is knowledge; the Greeks matched him to Hermes and built Hermeticism on the identification.
gods.imageQuery: Thoth ibis headed Egyptian god relief

gods.name: Amun
gods.description: Originally a local Theban god whose name means something like 'the hidden one', Amun rose to become the most powerful deity in Egypt, fused with Ra as Amun-Ra. His clergy at Karnak accumulated so much wealth and land that their power came to rival the king's outright.

gods.name: Ptah
gods.description: The creator god of Memphis, who brings the world into being by thinking it and then speaking it — a notably intellectual creation, as against Ra's more physical emanations. Patron of sculptors, masons, and builders.

gods.name: Hathor
gods.domain: Love, music, joy, motherhood, the sky
gods.description: Cow-horned goddess of love, music, drunkenness, and celebration, and a nurse to the king. She also has a ferocious aspect: unleashed as the lion Sekhmet, she nearly annihilates humanity and has to be tricked into stopping by being given beer dyed red to look like blood.
gods.imageQuery: Hathor Egyptian goddess cow horns relief

gods.name: Ma'at
gods.description: Less a personality than a principle wearing a face — the ostrich feather of truth and right order against which every heart is weighed. Maintaining maat was the king's entire job description, and the reason the cosmos did not collapse back into chaos.
gods.imageQuery: Maat Egyptian goddess feather of truth

gods.name: Bastet
gods.description: Cat-headed protector of the household, of women, and of children — guardian against disease and evil spirits. Her cult centre at Bubastis drew enormous festival crowds, and the cat's status in Egypt owes a great deal to her.

imageQueries: Book of the Dead weighing of the heart papyrus
Egyptian tomb wall painting gods offering

indoIranian.name:
Indo-Iranian
Polytheism
indoIranian.description: Proto-Indo-Iranian religion is the archaic offshoot of Proto-Indo-European religion from which both the Vedic tradition and Zoroastrianism grew, and it is reconstructed by setting the Rigveda and the Avesta side by side and reading what they share. What they share is remarkable. Both worshipped many gods and performed elaborate sacrificial rituals — yajña in Sanskrit, yasna in Avestan — at the end of which they drank a pressed, ecstatic, intoxicating elixir: Soma in India, Haoma in Iran, which was not only a plant but a god in its own right. Both organized the cosmos around a single universal force of truth and order — ṛta in Sanskrit, aša in Avestan. Both had a fire cult (Agni / Atar), a three-tiered cosmos, and a shared ritual vocabulary right down to the words for priest and libation. Whole rosters of names line up: Mitra/Mithra, Vayu/Vay, Yama/Yima, Apām Napāt in both, Varuṇa/Rashnu, Tvaṣṭar/Thworeshter. The famous divergence came later. In Iran, Zoroaster's reform elevated Ahura Mazda and rejected the daēvas as demons — while in India the cognate devas remained the gods and the asuras drifted toward the demonic. Indra, greatest of the Vedic devas, is listed in Zoroastrian texts among the worst of the evil powers, sometimes second only to Angra Mainyu. The same inheritance, split down the middle and turned inside out.

sources.title: Indo-Iranian Religion
sources.author: Gnoli, Gherardo.
sources.mla: Gnoli, Gherardo. "Indo-Iranian Religion." Encyclopaedia Iranica, https://www.iranicaonline.org/articles/indo-iranian-religion/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Asuras and Daevas: The Indo-Iranian Religious Connection
sources.mla: "Asuras and Daevas: The Indo-Iranian Religious Connection." Hinduwebsite, https://www.hinduwebsite.com/zoroastrianism/indoiranian.asp. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: *Mitra
gods.description: The god of the binding agreement — the contract itself, personified. He survives on both sides of the split without demotion: Vedic Mitra, Avestan and Old Persian Mithra, and eventually the Mithras of the Roman mystery cult. He watches what people promise each other.
gods.imageQuery: Mithra Persian relief investiture Taq-e Bostan

gods.name: *Varuna / Asura
gods.description: The great asura/ahura — 'lord' — who upholds ṛta/aša, the moral order of the cosmos, knows every person's inmost thoughts, and keeps an army of invisible spirits reporting on human conduct. He does not correspond to Ahura Mazda directly, but he shares his character: king among the gods and arbiter of law.

gods.name: *Sauma
gods.domain: The sacred pressed drink, ecstasy, vision
gods.description: Both a plant and a deity — the elixir pressed and drunk at the sacrifice, which grants poetic vision, strength, and ecstasy, and which is itself worshipped as a priestly god presiding over the offering. What the plant actually was remains one of the great unsettled arguments in the field.

gods.name: *Agni / Atar
gods.domain: Fire, sacrifice, the mouth of the gods
gods.description: Fire — the medium through which offerings reach the gods, and the one god present at every hearth and every altar. The fire cult is the deepest continuity in the whole family: it survives intact in Zoroastrian fire temples and in the Vedic ritual alike.

gods.name: *Indra / Verethragna
gods.domain: War, victory, the slaying of the dragon
gods.description: The warrior who kills the serpent and releases the waters — the single most consequential myth of the family. In India he becomes Indra, king of the devas, patron of the aristocracy, delighting in war and poetry. In Iran the epithet survives as Verethragna, victory, while Indra himself is demonized by name in Zoroastrian scripture.

gods.name: *Yama / Yima
gods.domain: The first man, the first to die, the realm of the dead
gods.description: The first mortal, and therefore the first to die — and therefore the king of the dead, who rules the place everyone else must follow him to. In Iranian tradition Yima becomes instead the ruler of a golden age who builds a refuge against the destroying winter.
gods.imageQuery: Yama god of death Indian art

indoIranian.godsNote: Names marked with an asterisk are reconstructions; the attested forms (Vedic and Avestan) are given alongside. The Zoroastrian reform inverted the valence of two whole classes of being — deva/daēva and asura/ahura — so the 'same' god can be divine on one side of the split and demonic on the other.

mesopotamian.name:
Mesopotamian
Polytheism
mesopotamian.description: Mesopotamian religion is polytheistic but also henotheistic — many gods exist, and different cities and devotees elevate different ones above the rest. Each Sumerian city had its own god and its own temple, and the god of the city that held power tended to rise. The Sumerian pantheon was adopted by the Akkadians, largely intact but renamed: An became Anu, Nanna became Sîn, Utu became Shamash, Enki became Ea, and Inanna became Ishtar. From the Ur III period a triad of foremost deities — Anu, Enlil, and Enki — is invoked in royal inscriptions. Then Hammurabi, in the eighteenth century BCE, promoted Marduk, a god of no great previous importance, to supremacy in Babylonia; by the first millennium some texts drop Anu and Enlil entirely and have Ea receive his position from Marduk. The gods are not remote. They ordered the world rather than simply creating it — the myth of 'Enki and World Order' has Enki assigning characteristics to cities and rivers and marshes and then instituting agriculture, herding, and housebuilding one by one — and they quarrel, get drunk, and are outmanoeuvred, most famously when Enki drunkenly hands the mes, the gifts of civilization, to his granddaughter Inanna, who takes them home to Uruk and refuses to give them back. The great creation account, the Babylonian Enūma Eliš, is a late compilation drawing on much older material, and its function is as much political as cosmological: it explains why Marduk rules.

sources.title: Mesopotamian Religion
sources.author: Jacobsen, Thorkild.
sources.mla: Jacobsen, Thorkild. "Mesopotamian Religion." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Mesopotamian-religion/Myths. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.author: Mark, Joshua J.
sources.mla: Mark, Joshua J. "Anu." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Anu/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: List of Mesopotamian Deities
sources.mla: "List of Mesopotamian Deities." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Mesopotamian_deities. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Anu
gods.description: The sky god and father of the gods — theoretically the highest, and in practice hardly present. Like many sky gods he plays a small role in the myths, hymns, and cults; he confers kingship on others (Enlil, Marduk, Assur) rather than exercising it. Depicted simply as a horned crown, sometimes on an empty throne. Father not only of the gods but of demons.

gods.name: Enlil
gods.description: The god of wind and storm and, for long stretches, the effective king of the gods and controller of the world. Sumer's cities each had their own gods but acknowledged the supremacy of Enlil, whose seat was Nippur. He separated sky from earth and took the earth as his domain. It is Enlil who decides to destroy humanity in the flood.

gods.name: Enki
gods.domain: Fresh water, wisdom, magic, craft, trickery
gods.description: God of the freshwater abzu, of wisdom, cunning, magic, and craft — patron and creator of humanity and sponsor of human culture. He is the one who warns the flood hero and saves the species behind Enlil's back, and the one who orders the world and assigns its offices. Clever, generous, and not entirely reliable.
gods.imageQuery: Enki Ea Mesopotamian god water cylinder seal

gods.name: Inanna
gods.domain: Love, sex, war, the morning and evening star
gods.description: The most vivid figure in the whole pantheon — goddess of sexual love and of war, patron of Uruk, associated with Venus. She takes the mes from a drunken Enki and keeps them; she descends into the underworld, is stripped at each of the seven gates, is killed, and comes back, at the price of sending someone else down in her place.
gods.imageQuery: Inanna Ishtar Burney Relief Queen of the Night

gods.name: Marduk
gods.description: Son of Enki, and a minor god until Hammurabi elevated him to supremacy in the eighteenth century BCE. In the Enūma Eliš he kills the primordial sea Tiamat, splits her body into sky and earth, and is acclaimed king of the gods — a creation myth doubling as a charter for Babylonian power.

gods.name: Shamash
gods.description: The sun god, who sees everything the light touches and is therefore the god of justice — Hammurabi's law code is shown being received from him. The sun as witness and judge is one of the oldest and most durable ideas in the region.
gods.imageQuery: Shamash sun god Hammurabi law code stele

gods.name: Sîn
gods.description: The moon god, whose city was Ur and whose reckoning gave the calendar. An Old Babylonian tradition even makes Nanna king of the gods with Anu, Enlil and Enki as mere advisers — probably the doctrine of his priests at Ur, later revived at Harran.
gods.imageQuery: Nanna Sin moon god ziggurat of Ur

gods.name: Ninhursag
gods.description: The great mother goddess and lady of the mountain — the earth to Enki's water, and consort or counterpart to him in the shaping of humankind. One of the oldest deities in the Sumerian record.

gods.name: Ereshkigal
gods.description: Queen of the great below — Inanna's sister, and the one who kills her when she descends uninvited. Her realm is the house from which no traveller returns, and she does not leave it.

gods.name: Tiamat
gods.description: The salt-water abyss, mother of the first gods, who turns on her own offspring and is destroyed by Marduk — her body split to make the sky and the earth. Chaos not as an absence of order but as the material out of which order is cut.

canaanite.name:
Canaanite
Polytheism
canaanite.description: For centuries nearly everything known about Canaanite religion came from the Hebrew Bible's condemnation of it — the prophets denounced Baal and demanded the destruction of Asherah poles without ever explaining what either was. Then, in 1929, a plough struck an ancient tomb at Ras Shamra on the Syrian coast, and the excavation of Ugarit produced thousands of cuneiform tablets that let the Canaanites speak in their own voice. The pantheon they describe is headed by El — the aged father of the gods, creator, associated with the bull, presiding over the divine council — with his consort Asherah, 'she who walks in the sea', mother of seventy gods. But the great cycle of texts belongs to Baal Hadad, the storm god, whose rain in a land far drier than Egypt or Mesopotamia was the difference between life and famine. The Baal Cycle tells how he defeats Yamm, the Sea, builds his palace, and is then swallowed by Mot, Death — 'Baal became as a lamb in his mouth' — until his sister Anat, the virgin goddess of war and the most vividly drawn character in the corpus, hunts Death down and cuts him with a sword, winnows him, burns him, grinds him in a handmill, and sows his remains in a field. Baal returns. It is fertility ritual as narrative: the god of rain dies and comes back, and the crops with him. El never loses his formal seat at the head of the pantheon, but he views Baal's ascent with something less than enthusiasm, and Canaanite theology was clearly not static.

sources.title: Baal
sources.author: Mark, Joshua J.
sources.mla: Mark, Joshua J. "Baal." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/baal/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Canaanite Religion: The Literature
sources.mla: "Canaanite Religion: The Literature." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/canaanite-religion-literature. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Canaanite Religion
sources.mla: "Canaanite Religion." New World Encyclopedia, https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/entry/Canaanite_Religion. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: El
gods.description: The father of the gods and head of the pantheon — old, bearded, associated with the bull, dwelling at the source of the rivers and presiding over the assembly of the gods. He remains formally supreme even as Baal takes over in practice, and his approval is still needed before Baal can build a palace. The Hebrew word El and the plural Elohim descend directly from him.
gods.imageQuery: El Canaanite god seated bronze figure Ugarit

gods.name: Asherah
gods.description: El's consort, mother of the gods — 'she who walks in the sea', and the progenitress of seventy divine children. Her cult symbol was a wooden pole or sacred tree planted beside an altar, which the biblical writers ordered cut down; the KJV translates asherah as 'grove', obscuring that a goddess is meant.

gods.name: Baal Hadad
gods.description: The storm god and rider of the clouds, whose rain made agriculture possible and whose absence meant famine. The Baal Cycle recounts his defeat of Yamm, his palace, his death in the jaws of Mot, and his return — a god who conquers death and takes the kingship. 'Baal' is also simply a title, 'Lord', applied to local deities across the region, which is why the Bible speaks of the Baalim in the plural.

gods.name: Anat
gods.description: Baal's sister, and the only goddess in the Ugaritic texts with a fully drawn character — 'the Virgin', a warrior of appalling ferocity. When Mot kills Baal, she destroys him with sword, sieve, fire, millstone, and field, and it is her violence that brings her brother back.

gods.name: Mot
gods.description: Death itself, with an appetite. He swallows Baal whole, and the earth withers accordingly. His annual defeat and return set the rhythm of the agricultural year — the summer drought is Mot's season.
gods.imageQuery: Mot Canaanite death god ancient Near East

gods.name: Yamm
gods.description: The Sea, El's beloved, and Baal's first great adversary — the chaotic water that must be beaten before order and kingship can be established. The same combat pattern runs through Marduk and Tiamat, and echoes in biblical language about Leviathan and the sea.
gods.imageQuery: sea serpent Leviathan ancient Near East relief

gods.name: Astarte
gods.description: Goddess of love and fertility with a martial edge, appearing more often in passing than in narrative at Ugarit, but hugely important in the wider Levant and later throughout the Phoenician world.

gods.name: Dagon
gods.description: A grain god of considerable antiquity, named as Baal's father in the Ugaritic texts, and known to the Bible as a chief god of the Philistines.
gods.imageQuery: Dagon ancient Near Eastern deity relief

gods.name: Resheph
gods.description: God of plague and of the arrow that brings it — a violent, feared power, adopted into Egypt as a war god, and glimpsed in the Hebrew Bible in the language of pestilence.

hinduism.name: Hinduism
hinduism.description: Hinduism is less a single religion than a family of traditions that share a set of themes without requiring agreement on any of them: dharma, the duty and ethical order appropriate to one's place; saṃsāra, the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth; karma, the moral weight of action and intention; mokṣa, liberation from that cycle; and the various yogas, the disciplines by which liberation is pursued. Behind the gods stands brahman, ultimate reality — and the relation between brahman and the gods is precisely what the schools disagree about. Introductory accounts reach for the trimūrti, the three forms: Brahmā the creator, Viṣṇu the preserver, Śiva the destroyer. It is a useful shorthand and a misleading one. Brahmā is barely worshipped and has almost no temples; the great majority of Hindus are Vaiṣṇavas or Śaivas, taking Viṣṇu or Śiva as supreme outright, while the Śāktas worship the Goddess, Devī or Śakti, as the supreme power — and Śākta traditions reassign the three cosmic functions to goddesses entirely. The Vedic gods of the second millennium BCE (Indra, Agni, Varuṇa) are still acknowledged but are rarely the focus of living practice; the deities who dominate now came to the fore in the last centuries BCE and the first centuries CE, through the Rāmāyaṇa, the Mahābhārata, and the Purāṇas. Over time local deities across India were identified as forms of the pan-Indian gods, and the multiplicity grew rather than shrank. Exclusivism — the claim that only one's own deity is real — is rare; polycentrism is the norm.

sources.title: List of Hindu Deities
sources.mla: "List of Hindu Deities." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/List-of-Hindu-Deities-2066639. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Brahman
gods.description: Not a god among gods but the absolute — the ground of being, without form, name, or attribute. In Advaita Vedānta the deities are heuristic: forms given to the formless so that the mind has something to hold. Whether brahman is personal, impersonal, or identical with the self is the central argument of Hindu philosophy.

gods.name: Vishnu
gods.description: The preserver — the god most involved in mortal affairs, who descends into the world whenever dharma collapses. Ten major avatars are counted, including Rāma and Kṛṣṇa. He reclines on the serpent Śeṣa in Vaikuṇṭha; his consort is Lakṣmī. Vaiṣṇavism is the largest of the Hindu denominations.

gods.name: Shiva
gods.description: The destroyer — but destruction here means dissolution and renewal, not malice. He is the ascetic on Mount Kailāsa smeared with ash, the yogi, and Naṭarāja, lord of the dance whose movement makes and unmakes the cosmos. His consort is Pārvatī, his śakti. Worshipped above all in the aniconic form of the liṅga.

gods.name: Devi
gods.description: The Goddess — and for the Śāktas, the supreme power of the universe, not the consort of anyone. She appears as Pārvatī, as Lakṣmī, as the protective warrior Durgā, and as the terrifying Kālī. Śāktism is closely bound to Tantra, and reassigns creation, preservation, and destruction to Mahāsarasvatī, Mahālakṣmī, and Mahākālī.

gods.name: Brahma
gods.description: The creator of the trimūrti, and the odd one out: almost no one worships him, and temples to him are vanishingly rare. Tradition explains this by saying he told a lie and lost his worshippers, and now creates only under the direction of the other two. He is not to be confused with brahman.

gods.name: Lakshmi
gods.description: Viṣṇu's consort and his śakti — goddess of prosperity in every sense, material and spiritual, and one of the most universally invoked deities in daily Hindu practice. Shown seated on a lotus, gold falling from her hand.

gods.name: Saraswati
gods.description: Goddess of learning, speech, music, and the arts — river-goddess in the Vedas, patron of students and musicians since. Shown white-robed with the vīṇā, a book, and a swan.

gods.name: Ganesha
gods.description: The elephant-headed son of Śiva and Pārvatī — remover of obstacles and, when he chooses, placer of them. Invoked first, before any undertaking, any journey, any other god. Probably the most immediately recognisable deity in the tradition.
gods.imageQuery: Ganesha elephant headed Hindu deity statue

gods.name: Krishna
gods.description: The eighth avatar of Viṣṇu and, for many, supreme in his own right — the butter-thief and cowherd of Vṛndāvana, the flute-player the gopīs cannot resist, and the charioteer who delivers the Bhagavad Gītā to Arjuna on the battlefield. Divine play and the hardest ethical teaching in the tradition, in the same person.

gods.name: Hanuman
gods.description: The monkey-god of the Rāmāyaṇa, who leaps the ocean to find Sītā and tears open his own chest to show Rāma inside it — the model of bhakti, devotion as total service. Enormously popular, and the deity most often invoked for strength and protection.

gods.name: Kali
gods.description: The black goddess, garlanded with severed heads, dancing on Śiva's body — time itself, devouring everything, and precisely for that reason the destroyer of the ego and a mother to her devotees. Terrifying and tender in equal measure, and central to Bengali devotion.

gods.name: Indra
gods.description: King of the Vedic gods, wielder of the vajra, slayer of the serpent Vṛtra and releaser of the waters — the dominant god of the Rigveda, and steadily demoted thereafter until he becomes a rather ineffectual sky-king in the Purāṇas. A record, in one figure, of the whole shift from Vedic to Puranic religion.

hinduism.godsNote: Hindu deities number in the many thousands, and are understood very differently by different schools — as independent persons, as forms of one supreme deity, or as forms given to a formless absolute. This is a selection, not a hierarchy.

chineseSham.name:
Wuism
(Chinese
Shamanism)
chineseSham.description: The wu 巫 appear in the Shang oracle-bone record, which makes them among the earliest religious specialists anywhere with a name we can read. The word denotes someone who can mediate with the powers that generate things, and the wu's work covered divination, exorcism, healing, and above all rainmaking — a Shang graph appears to show hands elevating a piece of jade, the rain-compelling mineral, inside an enclosure. In the Shang a wu could be of either sex; by the late Zhou the word had narrowed to female shamans, with xi 覡 for males. Under the Zhou they were absorbed into the state: the Rites of Zhou organizes them under a Manager of Spirit Mediums, with male mediums handling anointments and exorcisms and female mediums performing the ablutions and the rain dance, and a Grand Shaman (da wu) attending the royal court to secure dynastic legitimacy and cosmic harmony. From tribal practitioners to civil servants in a few centuries. Their descendants and relatives are everywhere in Chinese religion: the tongji spirit-mediums of Taiwan, the shigong of Fujian, the bimo of the Yi, the bumo of the Zhuang, and — through the pursuit of long life and the techniques of ecstatic flight — Daoism itself, which has some of its origins here. Scholars caution that 'shamanism' is an imported category that does not neatly fit the Chinese material, and that the meaning of wu shifted considerably from dynasty to dynasty.

sources.title: Shamanism Theory and the Early Chinese Wu
sources.author: Michael, Thomas.
sources.container: Journal of the American Academy of Religion, vol. 83, no. 3
sources.mla: Michael, Thomas. "Shamanism Theory and the Early Chinese Wu." Journal of the American Academy of Religion, vol. 83, no. 3, https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280953138_Shamanism_Theory_and_the_Early_Chinese_Wu. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Shangdi
gods.domain: The high god, sovereignty, harvest, war
gods.description: The high god of the Shang — remote, powerful, and approached not directly but through the royal ancestors, who could intercede. He governed harvest, weather, and victory, and the oracle bones are largely a record of asking him, through them, what would happen. Later absorbed into the concept of Tian, Heaven.

gods.name: Royal ancestors
gods.description: The true working centre of Shang religion. The dead kings retained power and stood between the living king and Shangdi, and the enormous sacrificial calendar of the Shang was addressed to them. Ancestor veneration outlives every other element here and runs straight through Chinese religion to the present.
gods.imageQuery: Shang dynasty bronze ritual vessel ding

gods.name: Nature powers
gods.description: The Yellow River, the mountains, the winds and the four directions all received sacrifice as powers in their own right. The wu's rain-making was addressed to this order of being, and it survives directly in the sacred mountains and river gods of later Chinese folk religion.

gods.name: Xiwangmu
gods.description: The Queen Mother of the West — originally a fearsome tiger-toothed power of plague and cosmic force, later smoothed into the keeper of the peaches of immortality. She belongs to the older stratum of Chinese religion that the wu inhabited, and her cult was later drawn into Daoism.
gods.imageQuery: Xiwangmu Queen Mother of the West Han relief

chineseSham.godsNote: Wuism is a practice tradition rather than a creed with a fixed pantheon; the powers listed here are those the wu mediated with, drawn mainly from the Shang and Zhou record.

muism.name:
Muism
(Korean
Shamanism)
muism.description: Muism — musok, Korean shamanism — puts a mudang, most often a woman, between the human world and the spirits, and has her negotiate. The Korean mu is cognate with the Chinese wu, and the tradition shares obvious ground with Chinese Wuism, Japanese Shinto, Ainu religion, and the Siberian and Manchurian complex. At the top is Haneullim or Hwanin, the Heavenly King, source of all being; below him are the mountain gods (sansin, typically shown as an old man in a blue gown with a tiger), the dragon kings of water (yong, headed by the Yong-Wang of the oceans), guardian spirits, generals, and the restless dead. The mudang's work is done through gut — elaborate rituals of song, dance, drumming, and offering — performed to heal, to exorcise, to divine, to bring fortune, to propitiate a village god, or to guide a dead person on. There are two routes into the role: seseummu, hereditary, born into a priestly line; and kangshinmu, chosen, which means undergoing shinbyeong, 'divine illness' — physical pain and psychosis that resist medical treatment and end only when the sufferer accepts the god and undergoes the initiation gut. Korean myth insists on the mudang's princely origin, tracing the office back to the Heavenly King and to Dangun, even though under Joseon Confucianism shamans were pushed down into the cheonmin, the lowest caste, and persecuted repeatedly across Korean history. The tradition is nonetheless thriving: South Korea has over 200,000 mudang today, and it remains one of the very few Korean institutions in which women have held real public authority.

sources.title: Korean Shamanism
sources.mla: "Korean Shamanism." New World Encyclopedia, https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/entry/Korean_shamanism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Hwanin
gods.domain: Heaven, the source of all being
gods.description: The Heavenly King and supreme deity — the source of all being and of all the gods of nature, the utmost god or supreme mind. He is the origin of the mudang's authority: every myth of the shaman's office traces it back to him, and to his descendants.

gods.name: Sansin
gods.description: The mountain god — typically the most important spirit of the earth, painted as a white-bearded man in a blue gown with a tiger beside him. Every mountain has one, and pilgrims add a pebble to a cairn at the summit to propitiate him. Mount Paektu is the most sacred of all, believed to channel ki to every other mountain on the peninsula.
gods.imageQuery: Sansin Korean mountain spirit painting tiger

gods.name: Yong-Wang
gods.description: The Dragon King, most senior of the yong — the water dragons who inhabit rivers, springs, and the sea. He rules the oceans, and fishermen and coastal villages have their own reasons to keep him satisfied.

gods.name: Dangun
gods.description: Son of the Heavenly King and legendary founder of the Korean nation — the figure through whom the shamanic tradition and Korean national origin are tied together. His mother, in the myth, was a bear who became a woman.

gods.name: Sinjang
