gods.description: The spirit generals, including the generals of the five cardinal points, whom the mudang calls on to drive off malevolent forces. Historical commanders like Ch'oe Yŏng and Im Kyŏngŏp are among them — and around Inchon, some mudang have venerated General Douglas MacArthur as a war hero turned spirit general.

muism.godsNote: The Korean spirit world is populous and locally variable — village gods, house gods, ancestral spirits, and the restless dead (gwishin) all figure in gut alongside the major deities listed here.

indus.name:
Indus
Valley
Shamanism
indus.description: The Indus Valley Civilisation left no readable texts — its script remains undeciphered — so its religion is reconstructed entirely from objects: seals, terracotta figurines, copper tablets, burials, and architecture. Two categories dominate. The first is the enormous number of terracotta female figurines, ornately decorated, often with exaggerated breasts and hips, sometimes shown with a tree sprouting from the womb, which are conventionally read as 'Mother Goddesses' and associated with fertility, abundance, and domestic ritual. The second is the Pashupati seal, excavated at Mohenjo-daro in 1928–29: a horned, possibly three-faced figure seated in what looks like a yogic posture, surrounded by an elephant, tiger, rhinoceros, buffalo, and deer. John Marshall named it 'proto-Śiva', on the grounds that Paśupati, Lord of Animals, is one of Śiva's epithets, and his reading was accepted almost universally for two generations — nearly every interpretation of Indus religion since has been forced to begin from it. It has also been vigorously attacked. Herbert Sullivan argued the figure is female, reading the supposed erect phallus as the dangling end of a waistband found on undoubtedly female terracottas; Shubhangana Atre argued it is a 'Lady of Beasts' whose worship centred on fire and water. Alongside these, the evidence suggests reverence for particular trees (the pipal), for animals (especially the bull), and for water — the Great Bath at Mohenjo-daro is usually read as ritual. No temples and no unambiguous cult statues have been found. The honest position is that we are interpreting pictures without captions.

sources.title: Pasupathi: The Shiva of Indus Valley?
sources.author: Awan, Arshad.
sources.mla: Awan, Arshad. "Pasupathi: The Shiva of Indus Valley?." The Express Tribune, https://tribune.com.pk/story/2404545/pasupathi-the-shiva-of-indus-valley. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Understanding Harappan Religion through Material Evidence: Mother Goddess, Pashupati Seal, and Ritual Practices
sources.mla: "Understanding Harappan Religion through Material Evidence: Mother Goddess, Pashupati Seal, and Ritual Practices." ResearchGate, https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404664994_Understanding_Harappan_Religion_through_Material_Evidence_Mother_Goddess_Pashupati_Seal_and_Ritual_Practices. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The 'Pashupati' figure
gods.description: The horned figure of the Mohenjo-daro seal, seated cross-legged and ringed by wild animals. Marshall read it as an early Śiva; others read it as a female 'Lady of Beasts', or as a shaman, or as a buffalo-deity, or as nothing we can name. It is the single most argued-over object in South Asian archaeology, and no reading is secure.

gods.name: The 'Mother Goddess'
gods.description: The label given to the many ornamented terracotta female figurines found across Indus sites, some shown with vegetation growing from the body. The name is a modern convention, not an Indus one; whether these are deities, ancestors, votives, toys, or something else is not established.
gods.imageQuery: Indus Valley terracotta mother goddess figurine

indus.godsNote: No Indus deity has a known name — the script is undeciphered. The two entries above are modern scholarly labels for recurring images, and both are actively disputed. Treat them as hypotheses, not gods.

vedic.name:
Vedic
(Early Hindu)
Polytheism
vedic.description: Vedic religion is the oldest stratum of Indian religious activity for which written material survives, brought by Indo-European-speaking peoples who entered India around 1500 BCE, and it takes its name from the Vedas. Its centre is the fire. The basic rite offered oblations into a sacred fire, itself deified as Agni, who carried them up to the gods; the Rigveda's very first word is Agni, and no offering reaches anyone without him. The great ceremonies for which the Rigvedic hymns were composed involved animal sacrifice and the pressing and drinking of soma, a mind-altering liquor from a plant now unknown — and Soma too is a god, the deified aspect of what is poured. The priests were drawn from the Brahman class. Of the gods, the highest in rank was Indra: a warlike figure who kills the dragon Vṛtra and releases the imprisoned waters, a deed that in some hymns amounts to the making of the cosmos itself, and to whom roughly a quarter of the Rigveda is addressed. Beside him stand Varuṇa, guardian of ṛta, the cosmic order; Sūrya the sun; Uṣas the dawn; Yama, first to die and therefore lord of the dead. The Indo-European sky god Dyaus, ancestor of Zeus, is present but barely regarded — already a fossil. Thirty-three devas are counted, eleven each of earth, air, and heaven. In the tenth book the hymns turn speculative, reaching for a single principle behind everything, and that turn is where Indian philosophy begins. Vedic religion did not vanish: the fire oblation, the initiation rite, and the language of dharma pass directly into Hinduism.

sources.title: Hinduism: The Rigveda
sources.mla: "Hinduism: The Rigveda." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Hinduism/The-Rigveda. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Indra
gods.domain: War, storm, thunder, kingship of the gods
gods.description: The greatest god of the Rigveda, with more hymns addressed to him than to any other — a warrior who conquers innumerable human and demon enemies, brings back the stolen sun, and above all slays the dragon Vṛtra to release the waters. Drinks soma in prodigious quantities. Patron of the aristocracy; delights in war and in poetry.

gods.name: Agni
gods.domain: Fire, sacrifice, the messenger of the gods
gods.description: The sacrificial fire, and the priest of the gods — the first word of the Rigveda. He is the fire of the sun, of lightning, and of burning wood, and he is simultaneously the material element of the offering and the god who carries it upward. Every sacrifice runs through him; he is the hinge between the finite and the infinite.

gods.name: Varuna
gods.domain: Cosmic and moral order, the waters, oaths
gods.description: The upholder of ṛta — the lawful order according to which everything happens — and the god who knows every person's innermost thought and keeps an army of invisible spies reporting on human conduct. Worshippers beg him to forgive their sins. He resembles Ahura Mazda closely, and where Indra is force, Varuṇa is law.

gods.name: Soma
gods.domain: The sacred drink, ecstasy, the moon
gods.description: The pressed elixir of the sacrifice and the god of it — a bringer of health, wealth, poetic vision, and immortality. The plant has never been identified with certainty; proposals include ephedra and various mushrooms. Later merged with the moon.

gods.name: Surya
gods.description: The sun, crossing the sky in a chariot drawn by seven horses — one of the few Vedic deities to survive into living Hindu worship with a temple tradition of his own.

gods.name: Ushas
gods.description: The dawn, and among the most beautiful subjects of Rigvedic poetry — a young woman who uncovers herself each morning, ancient and reborn daily. Cognate with Greek Eos and Roman Aurora.

gods.name: Yama
gods.domain: Death, the realm of the dead, justice
gods.description: The first mortal, and therefore the first to die and the one who found the path to the other world — which makes him its king. He remains the god of death throughout Hinduism, and he is cognate with the Iranian Yima.
gods.imageQuery: Yama god of death buffalo Indian art

gods.name: Rudra
gods.description: A fierce and dangerous outsider god of the storm and the wilderness, whose arrows bring disease and who is placated rather than loved — and who, under the euphemistic epithet Śiva, 'the auspicious', becomes one of the two supreme gods of later Hinduism.

gods.name: Dyaus Pitar
gods.description: Sky Father — the direct Indian continuation of the Proto-Indo-European *Dyḗws Ph₂tḗr, cognate with Zeus and Jupiter. And yet in the Rigveda he is barely regarded: a relic already at the moment of first attestation, outrun by Indra.
gods.imageQuery: Dyaus Prithvi sky father earth mother

vedic.godsNote: The Rigveda counts thirty-three devas — eleven each of earth, air, and heaven — but names many more. The devas are gods and the asuras are their counterparts; in Iran the valence of both classes was inverted.

sidea.name:
Sami
Shamanism
sidea.description: Sámi religion braids together three strands: animism, in which every significant natural thing — animal, plant, rock — has a soul; polytheism, with a large body of named deities; and shamanism, centred on the noaidi. It varies considerably across Sápmi, and the deities overlap and change names by region: one god in one district may be several elsewhere, and several may collapse into one. At the head is Radien-attje, creator and high god, who with his wife Raedieahkka made human souls — and who is passive or asleep, and therefore rarely a focus of practice. The gods who matter day to day are closer at hand: Beaivi the sun, mother of humankind, to whom sacrifices ensured grazing for the reindeer; Horagalles the thunder god, whose name likely means 'Thor-man' and who could shatter mountains with his hammer and scatter the herds; Bieggolmai, the wind-man; Leaibolmmái, the god of the hunt and of adult men, who owns the forest animals as his herds and grants or withholds luck; Jábmeáhkká, mistress of the dead. The Áhkká — Máttaráhkká and her daughters Sáráhkká, Juoksáhkká, Uksáhkká — are household and birth goddesses, worshipped by both women and men, with Sáráhkká living in the hearth itself. Sacred places are sieidis: distinctively shaped stones and landforms treated as gateways to the spirit world, given offerings of reindeer hide and horn. The noaidi mediates between the human world and saivo, the underworld, using a painted drum and a flute called a fadno, and the drums themselves carry maps of the cosmos. Christian missionaries found one belief especially intolerable: that the living and the dead are two halves of one family. Anders Poulsen, a noaidi, was tried for idolatry after his drum was seized in 1691 and killed in prison before sentence could be passed.

gods.name: Radien-attje
gods.description: The creator and head divinity, who made the souls of human beings together with his wife Raedieahkka. Passive, or asleep — and consequently seldom included in actual religious practice. The name Ipmil, later used for the Christian God, originally referred to him.

gods.name: Beaivi
gods.description: The sun goddess and mother of humankind, on whom the grazing of the reindeer and all vegetation depend. Offerings to her were burned, to symbolize the heat of the sun. She was also believed to help cure mental illness. In some districts the sun is male.

gods.name: Horagalles
gods.description: The thunder god — his name probably means 'Thor-man', and he wields a hammer, which is likely to be a memory of the boat-shaped stone axe. He could demolish mountains and scatter or injure the reindeer, and he was appeased with sacrifice.
gods.imageQuery: Horagalles Sami thunder god drum symbol

gods.name: Leaibolmmái
gods.description: The forest god, who owns the wild animals as his herds and dispenses good or bad luck in the hunt. His favour was so important that prayers and offerings were made to him every morning and every evening. His worship is bound up with bear hunting and bear burial ritual.

gods.name: Máttaráhkká and the Áhkká
gods.description: The mother goddess and her three daughters — the household and birth goddesses. Máttaráhkká gives the newborn its body; Sáráhkká lives in the hearth of every home and attends the birth itself; Juoksáhkká, 'the woman with the bow', guards children; Uksáhkká watches the door.
gods.imageQuery: Sami goat hut hearth traditional dwelling

gods.name: Jábmeáhkká
gods.description: Goddess of the dead and mistress of the underworld — the realm of the dead into which the noaidi must sometimes travel to bargain for a sick person's life.

finnish.name:
Finnish
Shamanism
finnish.description: Baltic Finnic religion — the pre-Christian religion of the Finns, Estonians, Karelians, Veps and their kin — is a polytheism of the forest, the lake, and the weather, best preserved in oral poetry that was not written down until the nineteenth century, when Elias Lönnrot assembled it into the Kalevala. At the top sits Ukko, god of the sky and weather, of thunder and rain and harvest, who strikes lightning with a hammer, axe, or sword, and who makes thunderstorms by riding his chariot across the clouds — the storms themselves being what happens when he and his wife Akka mate. Ukko is comparatively late: he probably entered Finnic religion in the first millennium BC under Baltic influence, displacing older regional sky gods. The one he displaced is visible in Ilmarinen, whose name is built on the word for air, and who is the earliest known sky god of the Uralic peoples (the Udmurt Inmar is his cousin) — but who, in the Kalevala, has been demoted to a blacksmith, though a blacksmith who forged the dome of the sky and the stars on it, and the Sampo, the magical mill that grinds out salt, grain, and money without end. Below the sky, the world is populated: Tapio rules the forest, with his wife Mielikki, and hunters pray to them for game; Ahti rules the water with Vellamo; Tuoni rules the underworld, Tuonela, reached by crossing a river; Louhi rules the cold northern realm of Pohjola and is the great adversary. Väinämöinen — the aged singer, the first man, born of the air-spirit Ilmatar — is a divine tietäjä whose weapon is song. The bear, Otso, is the king of the forest and sacred, and his killing required an elaborate reconciliation ceremony.

sources.title: Finnish Mythology and the Kalevala: The Complete Guide
sources.mla: "Finnish Mythology and the Kalevala: The Complete Guide." Routes North, https://www.routesnorth.com/finland/finnish-mythology-and-the-kalevala-the-complete-guide/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Ukko
gods.description: The supreme god — of the sky and the weather, and above all of the rain that decides whether anyone eats. He strikes lightning with a hammer called Vasara, and rides a chariot over the clouds to make thunder. His wife is Akka. He entered the Finnic pantheon relatively late, from the Baltic, and displaced older sky gods; even after Christianization he remained embedded in folk speech and ritual.

gods.name: Ilmarinen
gods.description: Once the sky god of the Uralic peoples — his name is built from ilma, 'air' — and remembered in the Kalevala as the eternal smith who hammered out the dome of the sky and the Sampo, the mill of plenty with three sides that grind salt, grain, and money. A god demoted into a craftsman, but a craftsman who made the firmament.

gods.name: Väinämöinen
gods.description: The old singer, son of Ilmatar the air-spirit — the first man and the first shaman, whose weapon is not a sword but a song. In the Kalevala he sings his enemies into swamps and sings a boat into being. Mikael Agricola said he 'formed the chants' on which Finnish magic depends.

gods.name: Tapio
gods.description: King of the forest, whose realm is Tapiola — bearded with lichen, eyebrows of moss. Hunters prayed to him for luck because the game is his, and permission had to be sought. His wife is Mielikki.

gods.name: Mielikki
gods.description: Goddess of the forest and the hunt, wife of Tapio — gentle, and the one to whom hunters, shepherds, and berry- and mushroom-gatherers alike bring their prayers.

gods.name: Ahti
gods.description: God of the water, living in the sea-castle Ahtola with his wife Vellamo, goddess of the waves. Fishermen answer to him.

gods.name: Tuoni
gods.description: God of Tuonela, the realm of the dead, reached by crossing the black river of Tuonela — a shadowy continuation of existence rather than a place of punishment. He walks the world sometimes as an old man in a hat of darkness with three fingers on each hand. His wife is Tuonetar.
gods.imageQuery: Tuonela river of the dead Finnish

gods.name: Louhi
gods.description: The witch-queen of Pohjola, the cold northern land — the great adversary of the Kalevala's heroes, who demands the Sampo as her daughter's bride-price and later steals it. Powerful, shape-shifting, and not straightforwardly evil so much as opposed.

gods.name: Otso
gods.description: The bear — king of the forest and the most sacred animal in Finnish tradition, spoken of only by circumlocution. Killing one required an elaborate ceremony of apology and reconciliation, and the return of the skull to a tree.
gods.imageQuery: bear skull tree Finnish bear cult

siberia.name:
Seidis
Shamanism
siberia.description: Siberian shamanism is the tradition from which the word 'shaman' itself was taken — the Evenki word saman entering Russian and then every European language — and it became, for better and worse, the template against which every other trance tradition on earth got measured. The cosmos is tiered: an upper world, this world, and a world below, joined by a world tree or a pole, and the shaman is the specialist who can travel between them. He or she is not self-appointed. The calling arrives as sickness, dreams, or breakdown, and refusing it makes things worse; initiation is often described as a dismemberment, in which the spirits take the candidate apart, count and clean the bones, and reassemble them with something added. The drum is the vehicle. Among the Kets and other peoples, skeleton imagery is stitched onto the shaman's coat — read by Mihály Hoppál as the emblem of shamanic rebirth, though it may also be the bones of the loon, the helper-bird that goes both under the water and into the air, exactly as the shaman goes both below and above. There are different kinds of shaman, differentiated by function — sacral rites versus curing — by power, and by the animal they work with, bear or deer. Maria Czaplicka noticed that Siberian languages have many unrelated words for a male shaman but almost all derive the word for a female shaman from a single root, and connected this to the theory that shamans were originally women. The practice was persecuted heavily, under the tsars and far more systematically under the Soviets, but it was never entirely destroyed, and it is being openly revived across Buryatia, Tuva, Sakha, and Khakassia.

sources.title: Shamanic Worlds: Rituals and Lore of Siberia and Central Asia
sources.author: Balzer, Marjorie Mandelstam.
sources.mla: Balzer, Marjorie Mandelstam. "Shamanic Worlds: Rituals and Lore of Siberia and Central Asia." North Castle Books, https://www.newworldencyclopedia.org/entry/Tengriism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Ülgen
gods.description: The creator and lord of the upper world in Altai and Turkic Siberian tradition — the good sky power, to whom the white shaman ascends. The counterpart and opponent of Erlik below.

gods.name: Erlik
gods.description: Ruler of the world below, and the one the shaman must descend to and bargain with when a soul has been taken or a sickness sent. Not evil so much as the necessary other half of a two-ended cosmos.

gods.name: Helper spirits
gods.description: The shaman does nothing alone. Helper spirits — bear, deer, loon, eagle — carry, guide, fight, and see for the shaman, and they are what distinguishes a shaman from anyone else who beats a drum. The loon in particular, which dives and flies, images the whole vocation.

gods.name: Master-spirits of place
gods.description: Every river, mountain, forest, and species has an owner who must be asked before anything is taken from it. Hunting is not extraction but negotiation, and a failed hunt is usually read as a failed negotiation.
gods.imageQuery: Siberian sacred site offering ribbons tree

siberia.godsNote: Siberia holds dozens of distinct peoples — Evenki, Buryat, Yakut, Ket, Chukchi, Nenets, Tuvan — with distinct cosmologies. 'Siberian shamanism' is a scholarly grouping, and the figures above recur across it rather than forming one shared pantheon.

archaicIndians.name:
Archaic Indian
Shamanism
archaicIndians.description: The Archaic period in North America — roughly 8000 to 1000 BCE — is the long middle passage between the big-game hunters and the pottery-and-mound societies, and its religion is known, again, mainly through the treatment of the dead. Red ochre is the throughline. It had been used ritually since the Paleo-Indian period, and by the Terminal Archaic it defines a whole burial complex: the so-called Red Ocher people of the Upper Great Lakes, the Illinois valley, and the Ohio valley, roughly 1000–400 BC, whose shallow burials in sandy river ridges were covered in the pigment and furnished with caches of flint blades, distinctive 'turkey-tail' points, and worked copper — copper carried enormous distances from Lake Superior. Ochre was never applied to everyone, which is the important detail: it marks certain individuals out, and those individuals are usually read as having held a special ritual standing. The other great development is monumental: mound-building begins here, far earlier than once thought. The burial mound on Horr's Island in Florida dates to about 3400 BCE, and Watson Brake in Louisiana to around 3500 BCE — earthworks raised by hunter-gatherers, not farmers. Poverty Point, later in the sequence, spreads over a square mile of concentric earthen ridges and drew goods from across the continent. Dog burials, deliberate and careful, appear at Archaic sites too. What all of it means in terms of belief is not recoverable in detail; what it establishes is that these were people who buried their dead with intention, moved sacred materials across a continent, and organized enormous collective labour for something other than shelter.

sources.title: Indian Mounds
sources.mla: "Indian Mounds." Encyclopedia of Arkansas, https://encyclopediaofarkansas.net/entries/indian-mounds-573/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

archaicIndians.godsNote: No names survive from the Archaic period. The rich later traditions of Indigenous North America (see plains, pueblo, iroquois, mississippi) have their own deities, but reading them backward into the Archaic record would be invention.
imageQueries: Archaic period copper artifacts Great Lakes

melanesian.name:
Melanesian
Animism
melanesian.description: Melanesian religion is organized less around gods than around power — mana — and around the dead. The concept entered anthropology through Robert Codrington, an Anglican missionary in the Banks Islands, whose The Melanesians (1891) described mana as a supernatural force derived from spiritual beings and capable of being transmitted through objects, words, and persons; later anthropologists, notably R. R. Marett, took it further, treating impersonal power as a category more basic than belief in spirits and coining 'animatism' as a precursor to Tylor's animism. Codrington's account has since been heavily qualified — his Mota-language material may itself have been shaped by Polynesian contact — and many Melanesianists now doubt that any single generalized 'mana' exists across the region at all. What is not in doubt is the practice. The beings toward whom Melanesian religion actually turns are spirits and ghosts, and the point of turning to them is to obtain their mana for use in the pursuits, dangers, and difficulties of life. Codrington noticed that the dead who are worshipped are often not worshipped as ancestors: as a figure becomes more clearly an ancestor, he tends to become less an object of worship, while newly powerful ghosts attract fresh veneration and fresh shrines. Magic is central, and works through mana channelled by secret forms of words. If the spell succeeds the practitioner is credited; if it fails, a stronger spirit with stronger mana must have opposed it, and he loses no standing. Tabu governs the whole system — regulating relations between the living, the dead, and the dangerous — and dangerous concentrations of mana are precisely what make a thing tabu.

sources.title: The Melanesians
sources.author: Codrington, R. H.
sources.mla: Codrington, R. H. "The Melanesians." Project Canterbury / Anglican History, https://anglicanhistory.org/oceania/codrington/melanesians1915.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Melanesian Religions: An Overview
sources.author: Middleton, John.
sources.mla: Middleton, John. "Melanesian Religions: An Overview." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/melanesian-religions-overview. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Mana
gods.description: Not a god but the central category — the invisible power that makes anything work: a spell, a chief, a canoe, a garden, a warrior. Codrington described it as derived from spiritual beings; others took it as impersonal force independent of them. It can be accumulated, transmitted, lost, and stolen, and a dangerous quantity of it makes a thing tabu.

gods.name: Tindalo
gods.description: The ghosts of men who were powerful in life and remain powerful after it, with shrines built to them. Notably, they are not venerated as ancestors: as a tindalo recedes into genealogy he loses force, and newer, more spectacular ghosts draw the veneration away.

gods.name: Spirits (never human)
gods.description: A class of beings that were never human at all — as against the ghosts, who were. They inhabit particular places and objects, and they are one of the two sources from which mana can be obtained.

gods.name: Tabu
gods.description: The system of prohibition that governs contact between the living, the dead, men, women, and dangerous objects. Not superstition but structure: tabu is the mechanism through which relationships of power among both the living and the dead are mediated.

melanesian.godsNote: Melanesia has enormous cultural diversity and no shared pantheon. These are categories of power and being, not deities — and whether 'mana' can even be generalized across the region is actively disputed by Melanesianists.

germanic.name:
Germanic
Polytheism
germanic.description: Germanic paganism before the Norse sources is known mostly through Roman eyes, and Roman eyes renamed everything. Tacitus, writing his Germania around 98 CE, reports that the most worshipped god is Mercury, who on certain days receives human sacrifice, followed by Hercules and Mars, who receive animals. Scholars read these as Wodanaz (Odin), Thunraz (Thor), and Tiwaz (Tyr) — a triad of sovereignty, strength, and law. Tacitus is a difficult witness: he writes with a rhetorical purpose, holding up virtuous barbarians against decadent Romans, and he must be read critically. But he preserves things that exist nowhere else, above all his account of Nerthus, an earth goddess venerated by a group of tribes, whose cart stands veiled in a sacred grove on an island; only her priest may touch it; when he senses her presence, the cart is drawn through the countryside by cows and there is feasting and peace wherever she goes — and afterward the slaves who wash the cart in a lake are drowned in it. Nerthus is the etymological ancestor of the Old Norse Njörðr, who is male. Beyond Tacitus there is the Merseburg Charms, Old High German and preserved uniquely without Christian overwriting, naming Wodan, Frija, and Sinthgunt; there are the Migration Period gold bracteates; there are place-names across the German-speaking world. And there are the felling of Donar's Oak by Boniface at Geismar around 723, and Charlemagne's destruction of the Irminsul, a sacred pillar possibly representing the world tree — both staged as demonstrations that the old gods would not strike back, and that neither did.

sources.title: Continental Germanic Paganism
sources.mla: "Continental Germanic Paganism." The Pagan Temple, https://thepagantemple.com/norse/continental-germanic-paganism/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Wodan
gods.description: Identified by Tacitus with Mercury, and named as the most worshipped god — the one to whom human sacrifices were given on certain days. A god of magic, of the ecstatic fury the name itself denotes, of the hanged and the fallen. His name is preserved in Wednesday, and in place-names across the Germanic world; he appears as a healer in the Second Merseburg Charm.

gods.name: Thunor
gods.description: Tacitus's Hercules — the thunder god, defender of the ordered world, and the god of the ordinary farmer rather than the war-band. His hammer and his oaks were so central that Boniface deliberately cut down Donar's Oak at Geismar to prove the god would not strike him down.

gods.name: Tiw
gods.description: Tacitus's Mars — god of war but more precisely of law and the sworn oath and the assembly that enforces both. His name descends from the same Proto-Indo-European root as Zeus and Jupiter, which suggests he was once the Germanic sky father before Wodan eclipsed him. He gives his name to Tuesday.

gods.name: Frija
gods.description: The chief goddess and Wodan's wife, who knows what will happen and does not say. Named in the Second Merseburg Charm — one of the very few pre-Christian Continental Germanic texts to survive uncensored — and remembered in Friday.

gods.name: Nerthus
gods.description: The earth mother of Tacitus's account, whose veiled cart is kept on an island in a sacred grove and drawn through the land by cows. Wherever she goes there is feasting and no weapon is touched. Afterward the cart is washed in a lake — and the slaves who wash it are drowned. Her name is the direct ancestor of the Norse Njörðr, who is male.

slavic.name:
Slavic
Polytheism
slavic.description: Slavic religion is known through a keyhole. There is no Slavic Homer and no Slavic Edda — the primary sources are the tenth-century Rus'–Byzantine treaties, in which warriors swear oaths on Perun and Veles; the Russian Primary Chronicle, compiled around 1113, which lists the pantheon Prince Vladimir erected in Kyiv in 980 (Perun, Khors, Dazhbog, Stribog, Simargl, and Mokosh) and then describes its destruction; and scattered chroniclers of the West Slavs, such as Saxo Grammaticus and Helmold, who describe many-headed idols — Svetovid with four faces, Rugievit with seven — kept in temples whose foundations archaeologists have since found. Everything else is reconstruction, from folk song, embroidery, ritual, and the great nineteenth-century collections. Britannica notes soberly that of all the recorded figures, only Perun and Svarog are at all likely to have been common to all the Slavs. The cosmology is the familiar three-tiered Indo-European world tree. The central structural claim — developed by Vyacheslav Ivanov and Vladimir Toporov in the 1970s and still the most widely accepted reading — is that Slavic religion turns on an opposition between Perun above and Veles below: sky against earth, order against chaos, the thunder-striker against the serpent, with Veles stealing Perun's cattle (or his wife) and Perun pursuing him with lightning. Whether this binary actually organized Slavic religious life, or is a scholarly pattern imposed on very thin evidence, remains genuinely disputed. Vladimir's baptism of Kyiv in 988 ended the official cult: the idols were hauled to the river and beaten with sticks as they went, and the people, the chronicle says, wept.

sources.title: Rodnovery — The Slavic Native Faith
sources.mla: "Rodnovery — The Slavic Native Faith." Tianmu, https://tianmu.org/good-work-library/ethnotheology/europe/rodnovery-the-slavic-native-faith. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Perun
gods.description: The thunder god, at the top of Vladimir's pantheon and the patron of the prince's war-band. Warriors swore treaties on him. He is the Slavic form of the Indo-European storm god — cousin to Perkūnas, Thor, and Parjanya — and his weapon is lightning, hurled at his serpentine rival below. Polish piorun, 'lightning', derives from his name.
gods.imageQuery: Perun Slavic thunder god idol statue

gods.name: Veles
gods.domain: Cattle, wealth, the underworld, magic, oaths, music
gods.description: God of cattle and wealth, of magic and music, and of the world below — invoked alongside Perun in the Byzantine treaties as 'the god of cattle', and with a temple in the merchants' quarter of Kyiv rather than on the prince's hill. Perun's eternal adversary: sky against earth. To ordinary people he probably mattered more than the prince's thunder god did.

gods.name: Mokosh
gods.domain: Earth, fertility, women's work, fate, spinning
gods.description: The only goddess in Vladimir's pantheon — mother earth, and the spinner of fate, which places her in the Indo-European line of fate-spinning goddesses. Patroness of women, childbirth, and the work of the household. She is the deity who survived Christianization most durably in folk practice.

gods.name: Svarog
gods.description: The divine smith who hammered the world into shape — compared by a Russian glossator to Hephaestus. Dimly attested but cosmologically central, and one of only two gods Britannica considers likely to have been common to all the Slavs. Some read him as the older supreme god whom Perun displaced.

gods.name: Dazhbog
gods.description: The sun, and the 'giving god' — son of Svarog, and named in the Primary Chronicle among Vladimir's five. The Tale of Igor's Campaign calls the Rus' the grandchildren of Dazhbog.

gods.name: Stribog
gods.description: God of the winds — in The Tale of Igor's Campaign, the winds are 'Stribog's grandsons', blowing arrows across the field.

gods.name: Simargl
gods.description: One of the strangest figures in Vladimir's pantheon: a winged dog, almost certainly borrowed from the Persian Simurgh, who guards seeds, plants, and the world tree — a reminder of how far the steppe reached into Kyiv.

gods.name: Svetovid
gods.description: The four-faced god of the West Slavs at Arkona on Rügen, described by Saxo Grammaticus — a war god and oracle whose temple held a great white horse used for divination, and whose idol looked out in all four directions at once.

slavic.godsNote: The evidence is genuinely thin. Britannica notes that of all the attested figures, only Perun and Svarog are at all likely to have been common to all the Slavs; much of the rest is regional, or known from a single hostile chronicler.

baltic.name:
Baltic
Polytheism
baltic.description: Baltic religion survived later than almost any other pagan tradition in Europe — Lithuania was not officially Christianized until 1387, and folk practice persisted far beyond that — with the result that Baltic mythology is unusually well preserved and unusually archaic. Both the languages and the myths are conservative to a degree that has made them a treasury for Indo-European studies. Two gods share the summit. Dievas is the sky god and the supreme being, his name the direct cognate of Greek Zeus, Latin deus, and Sanskrit deva — but in everyday worship it is Perkūnas, the thunder god, who is actively venerated, because he is the one who brings the rain, enforces justice, and beats back chaos. Dievas presides; Perkūnas acts. Perkūnas rides a chariot, wields an axe, is bearded, has stones in the sky that rumble during storms, and is a god of fertility as well as order — an unusual combination for a male deity. The oak is his tree, which is also the tree most often struck by lightning. He pursues a serpentine underworld rival, Velnias, across the sky, exactly as his Slavic twin Perun pursues Veles. The feminine side of the pantheon is ancient and probably pre-Indo-European in part: Žemyna, the earth mother, who was kissed each morning and given the first drink; Laima, who decides the length and fortune of every life and stands over every birth; the Latvian Māra, who takes the body when Dievs takes the soul. The sun, Saulė, is a goddess, and the Divine Twins appear as the Dieva dēli, the sons of god. The grass snake, the žaltys, was a guardian of the home, kept and fed. The moral centre is darna — harmony, maintained by right action.

gods.name: Dievas
gods.description: The sky god and supreme being — his name descends directly from Proto-Indo-European *Dyēus, making him a first cousin of Zeus and Jupiter. He creates and presides. But he is not the god people actually address most; that is Perkūnas. In Lithuanian folk memory he is Dievas senelis, 'old man God'.

gods.name: Perkūnas
gods.domain: Thunder, lightning, rain, justice, oaks, fertility
gods.description: The thunder god, second in rank and first in practice — bearded, axe-wielding, chariot-riding, and responsible for rain, order, law, war, and fertility all at once. The oak is sacred to him. He drives the serpent Velnias out of the sky and out of the world. There are said to be many Perkūnai: 'Perkūnų yra daug'.

gods.name: Žemyna
gods.description: Mother Earth — kissed in the morning and at night, given the first pour of every drink, and never struck or dug into carelessly. She belongs to the pre-Indo-European stratum of the Baltic pantheon and is one of the most durable figures in the tradition.

gods.name: Laima
gods.domain: Fate, luck, birth, the length of a life
gods.description: The goddess of fate, who decides how long a life will be and what fortune it will carry — and who is present at every birth, pronouncing the destiny of the infant. Patroness of pregnant women. She gives good luck and bad in the same hand.

gods.name: Saulė
gods.description: The sun — and she is female, riding across the sky and sinking into the sea at evening. Her daughter, Saules meita, and the Divine Twins (the Dieva dēli) form one of the best-preserved Indo-European mythic complexes anywhere.

gods.name: Velnias
gods.domain: The underworld, cattle, cunning, the swamp
gods.description: The chthonic adversary — a serpentine, shape-shifting underworld figure whom Perkūnas hunts across the sky with lightning. Christianization flattened him into the Devil, but he began as something more interesting: clever, wealthy, associated with cattle and water, and a brother-figure to Dievas rather than a pure enemy.

gods.name: Gabija
gods.description: The sacred fire of the household, personified as a goddess who must be covered at night and never insulted — fed bread and salt, and treated as a member of the family.

gods.name: Žaltys
gods.description: Not a god exactly but a sacred creature — the harmless grass snake, kept in the house, fed milk, and understood as a guardian of the household's health and fertility and as a creature of rain and rebirth. Killing one was a serious matter.

imageQueries: Baltic amber sun symbol folk art

celtic.name:
Celtic
Polytheism
celtic.description: Celtic religion is one of the largest bodies of European paganism and one of the least directly documented — the druids, who were its priests, teachers, and judges, are said to have refused to commit their learning to writing, and what survives comes to us through Roman observers, through Gaulish inscriptions and iconography, and through Irish and Welsh manuscripts written down centuries after Christianization by monks. The gods vary enormously by region and by tribe, and there is no fixed pantheon of twelve; but underneath the variation there is a real religious homogeneity. Widely worshipped figures include Lugus (Irish Lugh), the many-skilled god associated with light, craft, oaths, and sovereignty, whose name is embedded in Lugdunum (Lyon) and a dozen other places, and whom Caesar may have meant when he described the supreme Celtic god; the horned Cernunnos, the most frequently depicted god in Celtic art, shown seated with stag antlers and torcs on the Gundestrup Cauldron; Taranis the thunderer; Epona, the horse goddess, popular enough to be adopted by the Roman cavalry; the smith Gobannos; and Belenos and Sirona and Borvo, healing deities attached to sacred springs. Triplicity runs through everything — the Three Mothers, the triple Morrígan. In Ireland the gods are the Tuatha Dé Danann, the people of the goddess Danu, led by the Dagda, the 'Good God' with his club and his inexhaustible cauldron; Brigid of fire, healing, poetry, and smithcraft; the Morrígan, phantom queen of war, fate, and sovereignty, who appears as a crow over battlefields. The year turns on four festivals: Samhain, Imbolc, Beltane, Lughnasadh. Offerings were thrown into water; ritual was held in groves. By the early seventh century the church had reduced the druids to irrelevance — but the filidh, the masters of traditional learning, kept much of their status and much of their pre-Christian tradition, working in easy harmony with the clergy.

sources.title: Ancient Celtic Religion
sources.author: Cartwright, Mark.
sources.mla: Cartwright, Mark. "Ancient Celtic Religion." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Ancient_Celtic_Religion/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Lugus
gods.description: The many-skilled god — master of every art at once, which is how he wins his place among the Tuatha Dé Danann. Associated with light and sovereignty, and possibly the god Caesar identified as supreme. He kills the one-eyed Balor with a sling-stone at the Second Battle of Mag Tuired. Lyon, Laon, and Leiden all carry his name; his festival is Lughnasadh, the first harvest.

gods.name: The Dagda
gods.domain: Abundance, strength, druidic knowledge, the earth
gods.description: The 'Good God' — not morally good, but good at everything. Father-figure of the Tuatha Dé Danann, armed with a club that kills with one end and revives with the other, and owner of a cauldron from which no one goes away unsatisfied. His harp orders the seasons.

gods.name: The Morrígan
gods.description: The Phantom Queen — a triple goddess of battle and destiny who appears as a crow over the field and washes the armour of men about to die. She confers sovereignty and takes it away, and she is as much fate as violence.

gods.name: Brigid
gods.description: Goddess of the forge, the poem, and the healing well — the three fires. Her sacred flame burned at Kildare, and her festival is Imbolc. She was absorbed so completely into Christian Ireland as Saint Brigid that the flame kept burning.

gods.name: Cernunnos
gods.description: The most depicted god in Celtic art and one of the most mysterious — antlered, seated cross-legged, holding a torc and a ram-headed serpent, surrounded by animals. He appears on the Gundestrup Cauldron and on the Pillar of the Boatmen, found beneath Notre-Dame. Almost no myth about him survives; only the image.

gods.name: Danu
gods.description: The mother goddess after whom the Tuatha Dé Danann — the people of the goddess Danu — are named, associated with rivers and the fertility of the land. Barely attested in her own right, and known chiefly through what is named for her.

gods.name: Taranis
gods.description: The Gaulish thunderer, shown with a wheel and a thunderbolt — the Celtic branch of the Indo-European storm god, named by Lucan alongside Teutates and Esus as gods requiring human sacrifice.

gods.name: Epona
gods.description: The horse goddess of Gaul and Britain — so widely loved that the Roman cavalry adopted her outright and gave her a feast day in the Roman calendar, the only Celtic deity so honoured.

gods.name: Belenos
gods.description: A widely worshipped god of light and healing, associated with thermal springs and equated by the Romans with Apollo. The festival of Beltane — 'fire of Bel' — is generally connected to him.

celtic.godsNote: Celtic deities were regional and numerous — there was never a fixed Olympian-style roster. The Gaulish gods (Cernunnos, Taranis, Epona) and the Irish Tuatha Dé Danann (Dagda, Brigid, Morrígan) are related but distinct bodies of tradition.

greek.name:
Greek
Polytheism
greek.description: Greek religion had no dogma, no creed, no scripture, no church, and no priesthood in the sense of a trained and separately entitled class. What it had was practice. Piety meant performing the rituals and the sacrifices, and the sacrifices were the principal proof of it: an animal killed at dawn on the altar before the temple, burned for the god and eaten by the people. Religion was organized alongside the political structures of the polis — in Athens the archon basileus was the chief religious official, and the state sponsored the festival calendar — and every city held annual public festivals to remind the gods of past services and ask for future ones. It was simultaneously panhellenic: Herodotus has the Greeks define their own Greekness partly by shared shrines and shared sacrifices, and the poems of Homer and Hesiod gave the whole Greek world one set of stories, one Olympus, and one family of quarrelling, adulterous, magnificent gods with recognisably human faces. The twelve Olympians — Zeus, Hera, Poseidon, Demeter, Athena, Ares, Aphrodite, Apollo, Artemis, Hephaestus, Hermes, and either Hestia or Dionysus — are the core, but Greek religion also ran downward and sideways into the countryside gods (Pan, the nymphs, the satyrs), the chthonic powers who received wholly-burnt offerings rather than shared meals, the Fates, the Furies, and the venerated heroes: Heracles, Asclepius, the local dead who still had power. From the sixth century BCE the myths and the gods came under rational philosophical attack; most Greeks went on praying anyway.

sources.title: Ancient Greek Religion
sources.author: Cartwright, Mark.
sources.mla: Cartwright, Mark. "Ancient Greek Religion." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Greek_Religion/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Zeus
gods.description: King of the gods and god of the sky and the storm — and, importantly, the guarantor of the two things Greek society could not do without: the sworn oath and the obligation to strangers. His name descends directly from Proto-Indo-European *Dyēus. He is also, notoriously, a serial seducer, which is how half the heroes get born.

gods.name: Hera
gods.description: Zeus's sister and wife, queen of the Olympians, goddess of marriage — and permanently at war with her husband's lovers and their children. Her cult at Argos and Samos was among the oldest and grandest in Greece.

gods.name: Poseidon
gods.description: Lord of the sea and the earth-shaker — and the god of horses, an odd pairing that probably preserves something very old. Bad-tempered, and the reason Odysseus takes ten years to get home.

gods.name: Athena
gods.domain: Wisdom, strategic war, crafts, the city
gods.description: Born from Zeus's head fully armed — goddess of the disciplined intelligence that wins wars, as against Ares' bloodlust, and equally of weaving, pottery, and every skilled craft. Patron of Athens, which took her name and built her the Parthenon.

gods.name: Apollo
gods.domain: Prophecy, music, healing, plague, light, order
gods.description: The most Greek of the gods and the least comfortable — god of the lyre, of medicine, of purification, and of prophecy through the oracle at Delphi, and also the archer who sends the plague. 'Know thyself' and 'nothing in excess' were carved at his shrine.

gods.name: Artemis
gods.domain: The hunt, wild animals, virginity, childbirth
gods.description: Apollo's twin — mistress of the animals and of the wild country outside the city, protector of young girls and of women in labour, and utterly unforgiving of anyone who looks at her. She descends in part from a much older Mistress of Animals.

gods.name: Aphrodite
gods.description: Born from the foam where Ouranos's severed genitals fell into the sea — goddess of desire in its full, dangerous, indifferent-to-consequence sense. She starts the Trojan War with a bribe.

gods.name: Ares
gods.description: War as bloodshed rather than strategy — disliked even by the other gods, and by Zeus most of all. The Greeks admired Athena's kind of war; Ares is what it actually feels like.

gods.name: Hephaestus
gods.description: The smith of the gods — lame, thrown from Olympus, married to Aphrodite, who cheats on him with Ares, and by some distance the most sympathetic figure on the mountain. Everything the gods use, he made.

gods.name: Hermes
gods.domain: Messages, boundaries, travellers, thieves, the dead
gods.description: The messenger and the crosser of boundaries — of roads, of doors, of the border between the living and the dead, whose souls he escorts. Patron of merchants and of thieves, being much the same skill. The Greeks matched him to Egyptian Thoth, and Hermeticism grew out of the identification.

gods.name: Demeter
gods.domain: Grain, agriculture, the harvest, the Mysteries
gods.description: Goddess of the grain, whose grief at the abduction of her daughter Persephone stops the world growing and gives the seasons their shape. Her sanctuary at Eleusis housed the greatest of the mystery cults.

gods.name: Dionysus
gods.description: The god who arrives from outside and takes you over — of wine, of the mask, of the tragic theatre, and of ecstatic possession. Where the other gods want sacrifice and formal hymns, Dionysus wants you to lose control. His followers, the maenads, danced on mountainsides at night.

gods.name: Hades
gods.domain: The underworld, the dead, hidden wealth
gods.description: Lord of the dead — a major god who is emphatically not an Olympian, because he does not live on Olympus, and whose name most Greeks avoided saying. Not evil; simply final.

gods.name: Hestia
gods.domain: The hearth, the home, the city's fire
gods.description: The hearth itself. She has almost no myths, which is exactly the point — she never leaves. The public hearth in the Athenian Prytaneion was hers, and colonists carried fire from the mother city's hearth to light the new one.

grecoRoman.name:
Greco-Roman
Mystery Cults
grecoRoman.description: The mystery cults ran alongside the public religion of the polis and offered something it did not: a personal transformation, a promise about death, and a secret. Participation was reserved to initiates (mystai), and the particulars of the initiation could not be revealed to outsiders — which is why, two thousand years later, we still do not know exactly what happened inside the Telesterion at Eleusis. The Eleusinian Mysteries were the oldest and the greatest, centred on Demeter and Persephone and on the reenactment of the daughter's descent and the mother's grief, which explained the seasons and promised initiates a better fate after death. The Greater Mysteries ran nine days, with fasting and a night procession from Athens; the initiation hall held some three thousand people. Anyone who spoke Greek and had not committed murder could apply, which made it the most accessible cult in the ancient world. The Dionysian Mysteries went the other way: ecstatic rather than structured, using wine, music, night, and dance to strip away inhibition, with women — maenads, bacchants — at the core of the membership, and with liberation offered specifically to people the polis marginalized, including slaves and non-citizens. Orphism, drawing on the myth of Dionysus Zagreus torn apart by the Titans, taught that the human being contains a divine spark trapped in a corrupt body, imposed strict dietary rules and purifications, and aimed at escape from the cycle of reincarnation; its gold tablets, buried with the dead across southern Italy, Crete, and Thessaly, carry instructions telling the soul what to say in the underworld. Later the Romans brought in Isis, Cybele, Mithras, Sabazios, and Serapis on the same model. The initiate's death-and-rebirth, the sacred meal, the communal secret — the parallels with early Christianity were noticed at the time and have been argued about ever since.

sources.title: Mystery Cults in the Greek and Roman World
sources.mla: "Mystery Cults in the Greek and Roman World." The Metropolitan Museum of Art, https://www.metmuseum.org/essays/mystery-cults-in-the-greek-and-roman-world. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Demeter
gods.domain: Grain, the Mysteries, the promise of the afterlife
gods.description: At Eleusis, Demeter is not simply the goddess of the harvest but the mother whose grief remakes the world's relationship with death. The initiate who sees what she shows is promised a better lot in the world below.

gods.name: Persephone
gods.domain: The descent, return, the underworld queen
gods.description: The daughter taken below and returned — but only partly, and only for part of the year. The whole Eleusinian promise rests on her: what happened to her can happen to you.

gods.name: Dionysus Zagreus
gods.description: In the Orphic telling, the child Dionysus is torn apart and eaten by the Titans, whom Zeus destroys with lightning — and humanity is made from their ashes, which is why we contain both Titanic guilt and a Dionysian spark. The whole Orphic project follows from that anthropogony.

gods.name: Orpheus
gods.domain: Poetry, the descent, the revelation of the mysteries
gods.description: The Thracian singer who went down for Eurydice and came back without her — credited with introducing the mysteries into the Greek world, and the supposed author of the theogonic poems and hymns on which Orphism rests. A mystagogue, not a god.

gods.name: Cybele
gods.description: The Phrygian mother goddess, brought to Rome in 204 BC — worshipped in frenzied rites whose priests, the Galli, castrated themselves. A cult the Roman state simultaneously imported and tried to keep at arm's length.

gods.name: Isis
gods.description: The Egyptian goddess remade for the Mediterranean — offering personal devotion, initiation, and rebirth, and by the second century BC worshipped from Alexandria to Britain. Apuleius's Golden Ass ends with her.

gods.name: Sabazios
gods.description: A Thracian-Phrygian horseman god syncretized by the Greeks and Romans with Zeus, Jupiter, and Dionysus — known now mostly through the strange votive bronze hands raised in his blessing.

gods.name: Serapis
gods.description: A deliberately engineered god: Osiris-Apis given a Greek face by Ptolemy I to bind Greek and Egyptian subjects together, and given a mystery cult besides. He replaced Osiris as the consort of Isis outside Egypt.

grecoRoman.godsNote: The mysteries were not a single religion but a category — cults sharing secrecy, initiation, and a promise about death. Members of one could be initiated into others; Julian the Apostate belonged to three.
imageQueries: Villa of the Mysteries Pompeii fresco

hellenism.name: Hellenism
hellenism.description: After Alexander, the religious world of the Mediterranean stopped being local. From his death in 323 BCE to Actium in 31 BCE, Greek polytheism fused with Egyptian, Persian, Syrian and Anatolian traditions across an enormous zone of trade and empire, and the result was neither Greek nor Eastern but something new. Four developments define the period. Oriental cults moved west — Cybele arrived in Rome in 204 BC, along with the Syrian Atargatis and the Cappadocian Ma-Bellona, some worshipped in frenzied rites of self-mutilation, others, like Isis, in gentler rites of divine rebirth; by the mid-second century BC Isis was worshipped across the Greco-Roman world. Native cults were Hellenized: the outstanding case is Serapis, whom Ptolemy I promoted from around 305 BC — a composite of Egyptian Osiris-Apis with the attributes of Zeus and Hades, given a Greek face and a Greek statue by (probably) the sculptor Bryaxis, housed in the great Serapeum at Alexandria, and designed from the start to be worshipped by Greeks and Egyptians alike. Third, the ideology of divine kingship: the Ptolemies made themselves pharaohs and gods, and ruler cults spread across the successor kingdoms, blending Greek hero-cult with Eastern royal tradition. And fourth, the backlash — nationalist and messianic movements against Hellenization, of which the Maccabean revolt is the most consequential. The temper of the age was individualistic and increasingly dualistic; people wanted a god who would deal with them personally and say something about death, and the mystery cults, which the Hellenistic world spread everywhere, supplied exactly that. One striking side-effect: syncretism produced tolerance. Traditions willing to borrow do not usually condemn. Judaism and Christianity were the exceptions, and their intolerance turned out to be a survival advantage.

sources.title: Serapis and Isis: Religious Syncretism in the Greco-Roman World
sources.author: Mackay, Danielle.
sources.mla: Mackay, Danielle. "Serapis and Isis: Religious Syncretism in the Greco-Roman World." TheCollector, https://www.thecollector.com/serapis-and-isis-religious-syncretism-in-the-greco-roman-world/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Serapis
gods.domain: Healing, the afterlife, the sun, sovereignty
gods.description: The engineered god of the Hellenistic age — Osiris-Apis with the beard and throne of Zeus and the modius (a grain-measure) on his head, promulgated by Ptolemy I to give Greeks and Egyptians a deity they could share. It worked: the Serapeum of Alexandria became one of the great religious centres of the ancient world, and his cult spread through every port.

gods.name: Isis
gods.description: The great winner of the Hellenistic age — an Egyptian goddess who became a universal one, offering personal devotion, initiation, and a promise about death, and worshipped from Alexandria to the Rhine. She acquired Demeter's ears of wheat and everyone else's attributes as she went.

gods.name: Tyche
gods.domain: Fortune, chance, the fate of cities
gods.description: Luck, personified — and enormously important in an age when kingdoms rose and vanished within a lifetime. Every Hellenistic city had its own Tyche, crowned with its walls.

gods.name: The deified ruler
gods.domain: Kingship, legitimacy, the divine on earth
gods.description: Not a god so much as a technology of rule. The Ptolemies established themselves as pharaohs within a syncretic cult of the deified Alexander; elsewhere kings received divine honours in life or after death. Greek hero-cult met Eastern sacred kingship, and the result runs straight on into the Roman imperial cult.

gods.name: Atargatis
gods.description: The Syrian goddess, carried west into the Greek and Roman world — served by ecstatic, self-mutilating priests, and eventually fused with almost every other West Asiatic goddess into a single figure the Romans simply called 'the Syrian Goddess'.

gods.name: Cybele
gods.description: Brought to Rome from Phrygia in 204 BC at the direction of the Sibylline Books — a mother goddess whose rites the Roman elite found alarming and imported anyway.
gods.imageQuery: Cybele Magna Mater relief chariot lions

hellenism.godsNote: The defining feature of Hellenistic religion is not a new pantheon but syncretism — old gods merging, being renamed, and acquiring one another's attributes. Zeus, Dionysus, and the Olympians remained central throughout.

hermeticism.name: Hermeticism
hermeticism.description: Hermeticism is the tradition built on writings attributed to Hermes Trismegistus — 'thrice-great Hermes' — a syncretic figure fusing the Greek Hermes with the Egyptian Thoth, both gods of writing, wisdom, and the passage of souls. The core texts, the Corpus Hermeticum, are seventeen Greek treatises composed roughly between 100 and 300 CE, mostly in the form of dialogues in which Hermes instructs disciples named Tat, Asclepius, and Ammon on cosmology, theology, and the nature of the human being. They emerged in the same late-antique Alexandrian ferment as Gnosticism, Neoplatonism, the Chaldaean Oracles, and early Christianity, and share their preoccupation: the soul has descended from a divine source and can ascend back to it through knowledge and contemplation. God is not distant but the very fabric of existence; the cosmos is alive and shot through with divine intelligence; and the human being stands at the hinge between spirit and matter, which is what makes human consciousness cosmically significant. What happened next is one of the strangest episodes in intellectual history. Byzantine editors compiled the collection; Marsilio Ficino translated it into Latin in 1460 for Cosimo de' Medici — who had him drop Plato to do it first — and Renaissance Europe received it as the prisca sapientia, the primordial wisdom of an Egyptian sage contemporary with Moses, older and purer than Greek philosophy. On that assumption Hermeticism reshaped Renaissance magic, medicine, astrology, and natural philosophy. Then in 1614 Isaac Casaubon demonstrated on linguistic grounds that the texts were not ancient Egyptian at all but late-antique Greek, roughly contemporary with Plotinus. The tradition never fully recovered its prestige — but it had already done its work, and it runs directly into Rosicrucianism, the Golden Dawn, and modern Western esotericism.

sources.title: Hermeticism, the Cabala, and the Search for Ancient Wisdom
sources.mla: "Hermeticism, the Cabala, and the Search for Ancient Wisdom." Cambridge University Press, https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/magic-science-and-religion-in-early-modern-europe/hermeticism-the-cabala-and-the-search-for-ancient-wisdom/A34EAAD6820340AD3F27F1297127F506. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Hermes Trismegistus
gods.domain: Wisdom, writing, the revelation of divine knowledge
gods.description: Not quite a god and not quite a man — a syncretic sage fusing Greek Hermes with Egyptian Thoth, credited with the Hermetica and revered in the Renaissance as an Egyptian contemporary of Moses who had known God directly. The 'thrice-great' refers to his supremacy in philosophy, priesthood, and kingship — or, in another telling, to the merging of the two gods with the divine mind of the cosmos itself.

gods.name: Nous
gods.description: The divine intellect — and in the first treatise of the Corpus, a being who appears to Hermes and reveals the structure of the cosmos and the origin and destiny of the human soul. Nous is what human consciousness is made of and what it can return to.

gods.name: The One / God
gods.description: The Hermetic God is not a person set over against the world but the ground of it — the source of wisdom, the essence of the Good, and in some sense identical with the All. The living universe is imbued with him, which is why the tradition never draws a hard line between contemplation and magic.
gods.imageQuery: hermetic emerald tablet as above so below

hermeticism.godsNote: Hermeticism is a philosophical-religious tradition rather than a cult with a pantheon. Hermes Trismegistus is its revealer, not its object of worship; what is worshipped is the divine mind that pervades the cosmos.

neoPlatonism.name: Neo-Platonism
neoPlatonism.description: Neoplatonism is the last great philosophical system of the ancient world and, arguably, the most influential — a systematization of Plato worked out by Plotinus (204–270 CE) in the Enneads and extended by Porphyry, Iamblichus, and Proclus. At its centre is the One: an ineffable, transcendent source beyond being and beyond non-being, about which nothing can strictly be said, since to say anything is to make it a thing among things. From the One everything else emanates, not by decision but by a kind of overflow — first Nous, the Divine Mind, which contains the eternal Forms; then Soul, the World Soul, which mediates between the intelligible and the material; and finally matter, the lowest rung, the realm of multiplicity and change. Nothing is created out of nothing and nothing is truly separate; the whole is a graded outpouring from a single source, and the philosophical life consists in reversing the direction of travel, ascending back through contemplation toward union with the One — an experience Plotinus reportedly attained a handful of times, and which his biographer Porphyry says he could not discuss without difficulty. Iamblichus complicated this by insisting that contemplation alone was not enough for most souls, and introducing theurgy — ritual acts that engage the divine directly. Neoplatonism was pagan, and it became the intellectual backbone of late pagan resistance to Christianity — and then, in one of history's great ironies, it was absorbed wholesale by Christian, Jewish, and Islamic theology, through Augustine, through Pseudo-Dionysius, through Avicenna, through the Kabbalah. Ficino translated Plotinus alongside the Corpus Hermeticum in the Renaissance, and the two have been intertwined ever since.

sources.title: Plotinus, Iamblichus and Hermetic Spirituality
sources.mla: "Plotinus, Iamblichus and Hermetic Spirituality." The Way of Hermes, https://wayofhermes.com/hermeticism/plotinus-iamblichus-and-hermetic-spirituality/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Hermeticism, the Cabala, and the Search for Ancient Wisdom
sources.mla: "Hermeticism, the Cabala, and the Search for Ancient Wisdom." Cambridge University Press, https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/magic-science-and-religion-in-early-modern-europe/hermeticism-the-cabala-and-the-search-for-ancient-wisdom/A34EAAD6820340AD3F27F1297127F506. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The One
gods.description: Beyond being, beyond thought, beyond description — the source from which everything emanates and to which everything seeks to return. Not a creator who decides to create; an overflowing fullness. Any predicate you attach to the One is already false, which makes negative theology the only honest approach to it.

gods.name: Nous
gods.description: The first emanation from the One — the divine intellect, which contemplates the One and in doing so contains the whole realm of eternal Forms. Thinking and being are the same thing here.

gods.name: The World Soul
gods.domain: Life, mediation, the animation of matter
gods.description: The third hypostasis — the soul of the cosmos, which bridges the intelligible and the material and from which individual souls derive. It is because of the World Soul that the universe is alive rather than merely arranged.

neoPlatonism.godsNote: Neoplatonism is a philosophy, not a cult, and the One, Nous, and Soul are metaphysical principles (hypostases) rather than deities to be worshipped — though Iamblichus's theurgy moved decisively toward ritual, and later Neoplatonists defended the traditional gods as manifestations within this structure.

gnosticism.name: Gnosticism
gnosticism.description: Gnosticism is less a church than a stance — what Hans Jonas called an anti-cosmic world rejection, and what its texts present as the shattering discovery that the world is a mistake. In the Syrian-Egyptian systems, the true God, the Monad, is remote, unknowable, and utterly good, and from him emanate lesser divine beings, the Aeons, who together make up the Pleroma, the Fullness. Then something goes wrong. Sophia — Wisdom, the last of the Aeons — attempts to create alone, without her consort and without the Father's assent, and produces a misshapen, ignorant, arrogant being: the Demiurge, called Yaldabaoth or Saklas or Samael. Not knowing where he came from, he declares 'I am God and there is no other God beside me' — the line the Hebrew Bible puts in Yahweh's mouth, quoted here as evidence of his ignorance. The Demiurge makes the material world as a prison, and his Archons administer it. But sparks of the divine have fallen into it and lie latent in human beings. Salvation, therefore, is not forgiveness and not sacrifice: it is gnosis, direct inner knowledge of one's own origin, which awakens the spark and allows the soul to ascend past the Archons and out. Christ, in the Christian Gnostic systems, is the revealer who brings that knowledge, not a sacrifice for sin. Almost everything known about Gnosticism before 1945 came from its enemies — the heresiologists, whose purpose was to make it look absurd. Then the Nag Hammadi codices were found in Egypt, and the Gnostics finally spoke for themselves: the Apocryphon of John, the Gospel of Thomas, the Gospel of Truth. Scholars now doubt whether 'Gnosticism' is one thing at all, preferring to sort by movement (Valentinian), by myth (Sethian), or simply by the presence of a Demiurge.

sources.title: Gnosticism
sources.mla: "Gnosticism." Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, https://iep.utm.edu/gnostic/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Monad
gods.description: The real God — utterly transcendent, utterly good, and utterly uninvolved in the making of this world. He is not the God of Genesis. Everything that exists in the Pleroma emanates from him; everything below is a mistake.

gods.name: Sophia
gods.description: The Aeon whose reckless desire — to create alone, or to know the unknowable Father — produces the Demiurge and cracks the cosmos. She is at once the author of the catastrophe and the divine wisdom trapped inside it, and her restoration is the pattern of every soul's. The most compelling figure in the Gnostic myth, and the one Jung could not leave alone.

gods.name: The Demiurge
gods.domain: The false creator, the material world
gods.description: Sophia's misbegotten son — the craftsman who builds the material world in unconscious imitation of a higher realm he cannot see, and who believes himself the only god there is. Often shown lion-headed and serpent-bodied. In the Gnostic reading he is the god of the Old Testament, and his ignorance, not his malice, is the point.
gods.imageQuery: Yaldabaoth demiurge lion headed serpent gem

gods.name: The Archons
gods.description: The Demiurge's officers, who administer the material prison and man the checkpoints — typically seven, one for each planetary sphere — and whom the ascending soul must get past. Gnostic texts supply the passwords.

gods.name: Christ / the Revealer
gods.description: In the Christian Gnostic systems, Christ is sent from the Pleroma not to die for sin but to wake people up — to tell them what they are and where they came from. Knowledge saves; the crucifixion is, for many Gnostics, beside the point or not real at all.
gods.imageQuery: Nag Hammadi codex Gospel of Thomas

gnosticism.godsNote: Gnosticism was never a single system. Valentinian, Sethian, and later Manichaean and Mandaean traditions differ sharply, and scholars increasingly question whether 'Gnosticism' names one thing at all.

bogomilism.name: Bogomilism
bogomilism.description: In the mid-tenth century, in a Bulgaria exhausted by Tsar Simeon's wars and squeezed by its landowners, a village priest began preaching in people's houses. He called himself Bogomil — 'dear to God' — and he told his listeners to stop going to church, that the clergy were liars, that the icons were dead wood, and that the material world itself had been built by the devil. Bogomilism was a dualist and gnostic Christianity, formed from a fusion of neo-Manichaean doctrine imported from the Paulicians of Armenia and Asia Minor with a local Slavonic movement to reform the newly established Bulgarian Orthodox Church in the name of an evangelical Christianity. Its central teaching was that the visible, material world was the devil's creation — which meant denying the incarnation, since God would not clothe himself in the enemy's matter, and rejecting the whole sacramental system, since matter cannot be a vehicle of grace. There were no church buildings, no priesthood, no icons, no cross. In the Interrogatio Iohannis the Bogomils taught that angelic souls had been trapped in bodies of clay by Satanael. The movement split between a moderate dualism, in which Satan is God's subordinate, and an absolute dualism, in which good and evil have coexisted forever — and that split reproduced itself precisely in the West, where the Church of Concorezzo followed the Bulgarian moderate line and the Church of Desenzano the absolute Dragovitsan one. By the early thirteenth century the dualist communities of southern Europe — Paulicians and Bogomils in the east, Cathars in the west — formed a network from the Black Sea to the Atlantic, and the inquisitor Rainier Sacconi wrote around 1250 that all the heretic churches of the West had their origin in Bulgaria. The French called the heresy bougre, 'Bulgarian' — which is where the English word 'bugger' comes from.

gods.name: God the Father
gods.domain: The spiritual realm, the true creation
gods.description: The good God, creator of the invisible and spiritual world only — and emphatically not of the earth, the body, or anything you can touch. In the moderate Bogomil dualism he remains supreme, with Satanael as his subordinate; in the absolute version, the two have coexisted from the beginning.

gods.name: Satanael
gods.domain: Matter, the visible world, the body
gods.description: The maker of everything visible — the earth, the flesh, the institutional church. In the Bogomil account he trapped angelic souls in bodies of clay. Some versions call him the elder son of God and Christ the younger, which is exactly the sort of thing that got people burned.

gods.name: Christ
gods.domain: Liberation of the soul from matter
gods.description: For the Bogomils, not the Second Person of the Trinity incarnate but something less — a being who only appeared to have a body, since real flesh would have been the devil's material. His work is to free the trapped souls, not to redeem the world, which is beyond redeeming.

bogomilism.godsNote: Bogomilism is a dualist Christianity, so its 'gods' are the two principles — the good God of spirit and the evil maker of matter. Whether the second is co-eternal with the first or a fallen subordinate is exactly what the movement split over.

ethiopian.name:
Ethiopian
Polytheism
ethiopian.description: Before Christianity arrived in the fourth century, the kingdom of Aksum — in what is now northern Ethiopia and Eritrea — practised a polytheism derived largely from South Arabia, brought across the Red Sea by Semitic-speaking immigrants who settled first around Yeha and transplanted the cultural and religious traditions of ancient Sheba. In the Yeha period the religion was barely distinguishable from the Sabaean, centred on the familiar Semitic triad of Sun, Moon, and Venus, with the sun as a female deity called by the Sabaean name Zat-Badar. In the Aksumite period a different triad emerged, and it is this one that appears again and again in the protective formulae at the ends of royal inscriptions: Astar, Beher, and Mahrem — sometimes with Medr, the earth, in Beher's place. Astar is the celestial deity, connected with Venus and cognate with the South Arabian Athtar and the wider Semitic Ishtar/Astarte; Beher is the sea, appropriate for a kingdom whose wealth came through the Red Sea port of Adulis, and also of the land and its fertility; Medr is the earth. Above all of them in political importance stands Mahrem, the war god and tutelary deity of the kings, who called themselves the Sons of the Invincible Mahrem and tied their legitimacy directly to his parentage. Greek inscriptions from Aksum render the triad as Uranus, Gaia, and Ares — and the Monumentum Adulitanum gives Zeus, Ares, and Poseidon — which is how we know roughly what these gods were understood to be. Temples at Yeha, Aksum, and provincial towns followed South Arabian architecture: raised on stepped platforms, approached by monumental stairways, walls panelled in projecting and recessed courses. The crescent-and-disc symbol appears on Aksumite coins and stelae, until Ezana converts and it is replaced by the cross.

sources.title: Aksumite Religion
sources.author: Adams, William Y.
sources.mla: Adams, William Y. "Aksumite Religion." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/aksumite-religion. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Astar
gods.description: The celestial god at the head of the Aksumite triad — cognate with the South Arabian Athtar and with the wider Semitic Ishtar/Astarte, and identified in the Greek inscriptions with Uranus and Zeus. Associated with Venus and with fertility as well as sovereignty.

gods.name: Mahrem
gods.description: The invincible war god and the personal patron of the Aksumite kings, who styled themselves the Sons of Mahrem and credited their victories to him. Identified with Ares in the Greek inscriptions. In practice the most politically important god in the pantheon, because he is what made the throne legitimate.

gods.name: Beher
gods.domain: The sea, the land, agricultural fertility
gods.description: God of the sea — a serious matter for a kingdom whose wealth flowed through the Red Sea port of Adulis — and also of the land and its fertility, which is why he is sometimes identified with, or replaced by, Medr the earth. Identified with Poseidon. His name may survive inside the Ethiopian Christian word for God, egziabher.
gods.imageQuery: Adulis Red Sea port Aksumite archaeology

gods.name: Medr
gods.description: The earth deity, invoked alongside Astar and Beher in the inscriptions of Ezana, and identified with Gaia in the Greek. Whether Medr and Beher are two names for one chthonic god or two distinct deities is disputed — the identification is described by specialists as highly speculative.

gods.name: Zat-Badar
gods.description: The sun — and female, called by her Sabaean name, from the earlier Yeha-period religion in which the triad was Sun, Moon, and Venus, essentially indistinguishable from that of Sheba across the water.

zoroastrianism.name: Zoroastrianism
zoroastrianism.description: Zarathustra — Zoroaster to the Greeks — was a priest of the old Iranian religion who, according to tradition, received a vision at thirty and proceeded to reform the faith he had been trained in. He took the inherited Indo-Iranian pantheon and split it down the middle: the ahuras were divinities worthy of reverence, the daevas were to be rejected and condemned, and Indra, greatest of the Vedic gods, ended up listed among the worst of the evil powers. Above everything he set Ahura Mazda, the Wise Lord — supreme, uncreated, all-good creator, working through the visible material realm (gētīg) and the invisible spiritual one (mēnōg) by means of the Amesha Spenta, six (or seven) divine entities who are simultaneously moral qualities and guardians of the elements: Vohu Manah, Good Mind, who presides over animals; Asha Vahishta, Best Truth, over fire; Khshathra Vairya, Desirable Dominion, over metals; Spenta Armaiti, Holy Devotion, over the earth; and the sisters Haurvatāt and Ameretāt, Wholeness and Immortality, over water and plants. The central axis is ethical: asha, truth and cosmic order, against druj, the Lie. In the Gathas — the seventeen hymns believed to be Zarathustra's own — the primary opposition is not between God and a rival but between two Mentalities that exercised free will at the beginning: Spenta Mainyu, the constructive spirit, and Angra Mainyu, the destructive one, with Ahura Mazda standing above the conflict. The symmetrical cosmic dualism in which Ahriman is God's co-equal adversary is a later development, prominent in the Pahlavi texts. Human beings are not spectators: the outcome depends in part on which side we choose, three thousand times a day, in thought, word, and deed. At the end comes frashokereti, the making-wonderful: evil sealed away, the world renovated, the dead raised, existence made immortal forever.
