Almost every element of that eschatology — judgment, heaven and hell, a bridge, a saviour, resurrection, a final renovation — turns up afterwards in Judaism, Christianity, and Islam.

sources.title: Zoroastrianism
sources.author: Duchesne-Guillemin, Jacques.
sources.mla: Duchesne-Guillemin, Jacques. "Zoroastrianism." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Zoroastrianism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Ahura Mazda
gods.description: The supreme, uncreated, all-good creator — 'Lord' plus 'Wisdom'. Omniscient but, in Zarathustra's own formulation, not omnipotent, which is precisely why the cosmic struggle is real and why human choices matter. Darius the Great invoked him at Behistun; at the Achaemenid court he was represented by an empty chariot drawn by white horses, invited to accompany the army into battle.

gods.name: Angra Mainyu
gods.description: The destructive mentality, born of evil thought — the source of druj, the Lie, and of all sin and misery. In the Gathas Zarathustra never names him, referring only to 'the Lie'; the fully symmetrical Ahriman, co-equal adversary of God himself, is a later Pahlavi development. At the end he will be rendered harmless and hell sealed shut with molten metal.

gods.name: Spenta Mainyu
gods.description: The creative, progressive spirit through which Ahura Mazda acts and is immanent in humankind — the twin and opposite of Angra Mainyu, and the one through whom the world and everything good in it was made. The relationship between Ahura Mazda and Spenta Mainyu is famously hard to pin down, and has been compared to that between God and the Holy Spirit.

gods.name: Asha Vahishta
gods.description: One of the two greatest Amesha Spentas — the lawful order of the cosmos, according to which everything happens, and the direct antithesis of druj. He presides over fire, which Zoroastrians hold sacred as the inner nature of reality itself.

gods.name: Vohu Manah
gods.description: Good Mind — the spirit of divine wisdom and love, and the being who appeared to Zarathustra at the river and led him before Ahura Mazda. He welcomes the blessed in paradise, presides over domestic animals, and believers are enjoined to bring him down into their lives through love in marriage and toward their fellows.

gods.name: Spenta Armaiti
gods.description: Beneficent Devotion — the spirit of faith and devoted service, who guides and protects the believer and who presides over the earth as its mother. Female, as are Haurvatāt and Ameretāt.

gods.name: Khshathra Vairya
gods.description: The power of Ahura Mazda's kingdom, realized in action that is guided by Good Mind and Best Truth. He presides over metal — and at the end of time the molten metal that seals hell is his.

gods.name: Haurvatāt and Ameretāt
gods.domain: Water and plants, perfection and eternal life
gods.description: The two sisters, almost always named together — Wholeness and Immortality, presiding over water and plants. They come to the believer as a reward for having participated in the natures of the other Amesha Spentas. Because they represent food and drink, Zoroastrians traditionally do not talk while eating.

gods.name: Mithra
gods.description: The great yazata of the covenant, inherited from the Indo-Iranian past and never demoted — worshipped alongside Ahura Mazda and Anahita from Artaxerxes II onward, and eventually exported west into the Roman Mithraic mysteries, which are a very different thing.

gods.name: Anahita
gods.description: The goddess of the waters — 'the moist, the strong, the immaculate' — associated with fertility, healing, and wisdom, and named in Achaemenid royal inscriptions alongside Ahura Mazda and Mithra from the reign of Artaxerxes II.

zoroastrianism.godsNote: Zoroastrianism is not straightforwardly polytheistic. Ahura Mazda is supreme and uncreated; the Amesha Spentas are emanations of him — at once aspects of his nature and beings worthy of reverence in their own right — and the yazatas below them (Mithra, Anahita, Atar) are 'ones worthy of worship' rather than independent gods. Modern Zoroastrians generally regard them as archangels and angels.

mithraism.name: Mithraism
mithraism.description: Mithraism is the best-documented and least-understood religion of the Roman Empire. It has left more than 400 temples and over 700 versions of its central image — and not a single line of scripture. Everything we say about what it meant is inference. The initiates called themselves syndexioi, 'those united by the handshake'. They met underground, or in rooms built to feel underground: rectangular halls with raised stone benches down both long walls and a central aisle, deliberately dim, often cut straight into rock. At the far end, always, the tauroctony — Mithras in a Phrygian cap, one knee on a bull's back, pulling its head up and driving a blade into its neck, while a dog and a snake drink the blood, a scorpion attacks the bull's genitals, and a raven watches. The scene is saturated with astronomical symbolism: every figure has a plausible constellation. Initiates passed through seven grades — Corax (Raven), Nymphus (Bride), Miles (Soldier), Leo (Lion), Perses (Persian), Heliodromus (Sun-Runner), Pater (Father) — each linked to a planet. They ate ritual meals together on those benches. The cult was male-only, spread with startling speed from the late first century CE across the whole empire, from Hadrian's Wall to Syria, and was especially popular in the army and among merchants, imperial slaves, and freedmen. Its name comes from the Iranian Mithra, god of the contract — but the Romans took little else from Iran: the tauroctony, the seven grades, and the cave-temple all have no Iranian parallel. When Theodosius banned pagan worship in the 390s, Mithraism vanished almost overnight, leaving stone and silence.

sources.title: Mithraic Mysteries
sources.mla: "Mithraic Mysteries." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Mithraic_Mysteries/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Mithras: A Secret Cult at the Fringes of the Roman Empire
sources.mla: "Mithras: A Secret Cult at the Fringes of the Roman Empire." HISTORY, https://www.history.com/articles/mithras-cult-roman-empire. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Mithras
gods.domain: The bull-slaying, cosmic order, salvation, oaths
gods.description: The god of the tauroctony — shown killing the bull in a star-filled cave, in an act that seems to release life into the world, though what exactly the cult believed about it is unrecoverable. He is also shown born fully formed from solid rock (the petra genetrix), which bypasses the need for a mother entirely, a detail scholars have connected to the cult's exclusively male membership. Named for the Iranian Mithra, guardian of contracts and light — but almost nothing else about him is Iranian.

gods.name: Sol
gods.description: The sun god, shown in the tauroctony scene in the upper corner and, in other reliefs, feasting with Mithras and shaking his hand — the two are companions, and sometimes fused into a single figure, Sol Invictus Mithras.

gods.name: Cautes and Cautopates
gods.domain: Rising and setting, light and dark
gods.description: The two attendants who flank Mithras in nearly every tauroctony — twins in Phrygian caps, one holding his torch up and one holding it down. Almost certainly dawn and dusk, or the spring and autumn equinoxes, but as with everything else in the cult, the meaning is reconstructed rather than known.

mithraism.godsNote: No Mithraic sacred text survives. The cult's theology is reconstructed entirely from images and archaeology, and the reconstructions are contested — Franz Cumont's Iranian reading dominated for a century and is now largely abandoned in favour of astronomical interpretations, which are themselves disputed.

christianity.name: Christianity
christianity.description: Christianity began as a movement within Judaism, around a Galilean Jew named Jesus, executed by Roman crucifixion under Pontius Pilate in the early 30s CE — and around the claim of his followers that he had been raised from the dead. That claim is the whole thing; without it there is no Christianity. From it followed a set of convictions that took three centuries and several bitter councils to formalize: that Jesus was not merely a prophet but God incarnate, fully divine and fully human; that his death was a sacrifice reconciling humanity to God; that God is one being in three persons, Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. None of this was obvious, and the fights were savage. Arius held that the Son was created, and therefore not fully God; the First Council of Nicaea in 325 rejected him and declared the Son homoousios — of one substance — with the Father, and the Council of Constantinople in 381 extended the same status to the Spirit. The resulting Nicene Creed remains the one statement of faith accepted across Roman Catholic, Eastern Orthodox, Anglican, and most Protestant churches, though the Filioque clause — whether the Spirit proceeds from the Father *and the Son* — split East from West and still does. The movement spread first through the Jewish diaspora and then, decisively, into the Gentile world through Paul, who dropped the requirement of circumcision and the food laws and turned a Jewish sect into a universal religion. It was persecuted, then legalized under Constantine, then made the official religion of the Roman Empire. It is now the largest religion on earth.

sources.title: Creed of Nicaea
sources.mla: "Creed of Nicaea." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Creed-of-Nicaea. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: God the Father
gods.description: The one God of Israel, maker of all things visible and invisible — inherited directly from Judaism, and in Christian doctrine the first person of the Trinity, from whom the Son is eternally begotten and the Spirit proceeds.
gods.imageQuery: God the Father Christian art fresco

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: The second person of the Trinity — begotten of the Father before all ages, God from God, light from light, of one substance with the Father, and made human in the womb of Mary. He was crucified under Pontius Pilate, buried, and, Christians hold, rose on the third day. The Council of Nicaea insisted on the word homoousios precisely because everything depends on whether he is fully God or something less.

gods.name: The Holy Spirit
gods.domain: Inspiration, sanctification, the life of the church
gods.description: The third person — the Lord, the giver of life, who spoke through the prophets and is worshipped and glorified together with the Father and the Son. The Council of Nicaea in 325 said almost nothing about him; Constantinople in 381 fixed his status. Whether he proceeds from the Father alone or from the Father *and the Son* is the Filioque, and it split Christendom.
gods.imageQuery: Holy Spirit dove Pentecost Christian art

gods.name: The Virgin Mary
gods.description: Not a god, and not worshipped — but venerated across most of historic Christianity as Theotokos, 'God-bearer', a title fought over at Ephesus in 431 because it is really a claim about Christ, not about her. Central to Catholic and Orthodox devotion; sharply de-emphasized in most Protestantism.

christianity.godsNote: Christianity is monotheistic. The Trinity is one God in three persons — not three gods — and the doctrine was hammered out precisely to hold both claims at once. Saints and angels are venerated in some traditions, never worshipped.

atenism.name: Atenism
atenism.description: In the fifth year of his reign, the pharaoh Amenhotep IV changed his name to Akhenaten — 'one who is effective for the Aten' — abandoned Thebes, and founded a new capital in the desert halfway to Memphis: Akhetaten, 'Horizon of the Aten', now Amarna. There, the only god to be worshipped by the king was the sun. The Aten was not new; it had been a minor solar deity, elevated already under Akhenaten's father Amenhotep III. What was new was the exclusion of everything else. Akhenaten closed the temples of the other gods, sent workmen to chisel out their names — Amun's especially — and built temples that were vast open courts with altars beneath the sky, rather than the dark inner sanctuaries Egypt had always used. And the Aten was not depicted as a man with an animal's head, as every other Egyptian god was; it was shown as the sun disc itself, its rays ending in small human hands offering the ankh. The Great Hymn to the Aten, preserved in the tomb of the courtier Ay, praises the sun as sole creator and sustainer of all life, in language that has struck readers for a century as strikingly close to Psalm 104. Whether this was true monotheism is disputed, and the honest answer is: not quite. Maat continued to be venerated; statuettes of Bes have been found in the houses at Amarna; and Akhenaten and Nefertiti themselves were treated as divine, with the Aten reachable only through the king. It was not a religion anyone could join. When Akhenaten died it collapsed almost at once, and his successors erased him.

sources.title: Great Hymn to the Aten
sources.mla: "Great Hymn to the Aten." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Great_Hymn_to_the_Aten. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Amarna Belief
sources.mla: "Amarna Belief." Digital Egypt, University College London, https://www.ucl.ac.uk/museums-static/digitalegypt/amarna/belief.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Great Hymn to the Aten
sources.mla: "Great Hymn to the Aten." Egypt Museum, https://egypt-museum.com/great-hymn-to-the-god-aten/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Aten
gods.domain: The sun, light, life, sole creation
gods.description: The visible disc of the sun — and, uniquely in Egyptian religion, shown as exactly that, a disc with rays ending in hands that hold the ankh out to the royal family. The Great Hymn calls it the sole god beside whom there is none, who creates the earth alone and gives life to everything under the sky. It has no myth, no consort, no animal form, and no story. It is simply there, every morning.
gods.imageQuery: Aten sun disc rays hands Amarna relief

gods.name: Akhenaten
gods.domain: Kingship, the sole mediator of the Aten
gods.description: The pharaoh — and in practice, part of the religion. The Hymn describes him as the Aten's son, come forth from its body, and he was the only route by which anyone else could approach the god. This is the strongest argument against calling Atenism monotheism: it was a cult of two.

gods.name: Nefertiti
gods.description: Akhenaten's queen, given unprecedented prominence in the art of the period — shown worshipping the Aten alongside him, and in some reliefs performing rites usually reserved for the king. Part of the divine triad that the Amarna religion actually addressed.

atenism.godsNote: Whether Atenism was genuinely monotheistic is contested. Other gods (Maat, Shu, Tefnut, Bes) continued to be venerated in some form, and the deification of the royal couple sits awkwardly with the claim of a single god. Freud's theory in *Moses and Monotheism* that Akhenaten's religion fathered Judaism is not supported by current scholarship.
imageQueries: Great Hymn to the Aten hieroglyphs tomb

judaism.name: Judaism
judaism.description: Judaism's key is history, not metaphysics. It affirms a God who is disclosed in creation and in events — a person, not an abstraction — who entered into a relationship with a particular people and gave them a program of action. That program is Torah, which means teaching rather than merely law, and it governs conduct at every level, from the most public to the most private, and covers not only dealings between people and God but between people and each other and between people and the cultivated land, its produce, and its animals. The central affirmation is the Shema, taken from Deuteronomy: Hear, O Israel — the Lord is our God, the Lord is one. Recited morning and evening, it is a declaration of God's uniqueness, of allegiance to his kingship, and of the duty to study and observe Torah; and it has been the last words of Jewish martyrs from Rabbi Akiba onward. The tradition traces itself to Abraham, who did not discover God but entered a new covenantal relationship with him, and to the Exodus, where YHWH liberated the Israelites from Egypt and at Sinai made them his people and gave them the terms of the covenant. The Israelite religion was not primitive: it appears late among high civilizations, on the land bridge between Africa and Asia, thoroughly imbued with the elements of the ancient Near East, and exposed to foreign currents throughout its history. After the destruction of the Second Temple in 70 CE, sacrifice ended and the rabbis rebuilt the religion around study, prayer, and the synagogue — the form it still has.

sources.title: Judaism: Basic Beliefs and Doctrines
sources.mla: "Judaism: Basic Beliefs and Doctrines." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Judaism/Basic-beliefs-and-doctrines. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: YHWH
gods.domain: Creation, history, covenant, law, justice, mercy
gods.description: The one God — creator of the universe, who chose Israel in love and showed that love by the giving of Torah, and who is to be loved in return through obedience. Not an abstraction and not a force: a person, who acts in history, makes promises, keeps them, and holds people to theirs. The name itself is not pronounced.

judaism.godsNote: Judaism is strictly monotheistic — one God, unnameable and unrepresentable, with no consort, no pantheon, and no images. Angels appear in scripture as messengers, never as objects of worship.

hellenisticJudaism.name:
Hellenistic
Judaism
hellenisticJudaism.description: After Alexander, Jews found themselves living inside a Greek-speaking world, and a large community grew up in Alexandria — where the distance from Jerusalem intensified the pressure of Hellenistic culture rather than relieving it. Two things came out of that encounter. The first was the Septuagint: the Hebrew scriptures translated into Greek, traditionally under Ptolemy II, which made the Torah readable by anyone in the Mediterranean and which became, in due course, the Bible of the early church. The second was Philo of Alexandria (c. 20 BCE–50 CE), the most philosophically consequential figure Hellenistic Judaism produced. Philo was thoroughly educated in both the scriptures and Greek philosophy, and he set out to show that the Law of Moses was not merely compatible with philosophy but was philosophy — the highest form of it, divine and universal. His instrument was allegory: read the patriarchs as exemplars of temperance, courage, and wisdom; read Genesis as an account of the soul and the structure of reality; read the literal text as true but also as pointing past itself. At the centre of his system is the Logos — God's reason, his creative word, his image — the intermediary through which a transcendent and unknowable God creates, orders, and reveals himself to a material world he cannot touch directly. Philo had almost no reception in rabbinic Judaism, which went a different way. But the Church Fathers loved him, and his Logos runs directly into the prologue of John's gospel: In the beginning was the Word.

sources.title: Philo of Alexandria
sources.mla: "Philo of Alexandria." Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, https://iep.utm.edu/philo/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: God
gods.description: The God of Israel, described by Philo in the vocabulary of Middle Platonism — utterly transcendent, beyond quality and description, knowable only through what he does rather than what he is. The move that lets Judaism speak Greek, and that leaves a gap needing to be bridged.

gods.name: The Logos
gods.description: Philo's bridge — God's reason and creative word, the intermediary principle through which the unknowable God makes and orders the world and is revealed within it. Called God's image, his firstborn, his instrument. Not a second god, and Philo is careful about that; but the concept passed straight into Christian theology and became one.

gods.name: The Powers
gods.description: The distinct divine activities — goodness, sovereignty, creative power — through which the otherwise unapproachable God relates to and acts within the world. A way of preserving God's transcendence without making him irrelevant.

hellenisticJudaism.godsNote: Hellenistic Judaism remained monotheistic. The Logos and the Powers are aspects or instruments of the one God, not separate deities — though later Christian and Gnostic readers took them in exactly that direction.

phoenician.name:
Phoenician
Polytheism
phoenician.description: The Phoenicians left no Bible. There is no surviving Phoenician sacred book — if there ever was one — and their religion is reconstructed from inscriptions dug out of their cities, from hostile Greek and Roman accounts, and from the archaeology of their colonies. What is clear is that religion was inseparable from daily life: gods had temples, offerings and sacrifices were made constantly, kings served as high priests, and ships carried images of the gods aboard. The pantheon was largely city-specific. Melqart — the name means 'king of the city' — was the god of Tyre, associated with kingship, the underworld, navigation, and strength, and identified by the Greeks with Heracles; his temple received a tenth of Carthage's annual profits for its first centuries, and every year the resurrection of Melqart was celebrated. Astarte was the great goddess of love, fertility, and war, matched to Aphrodite. Eshmun of Sidon was the healer, equated with Asclepius. Baal was the storm and rain. And in the western colonies the pantheon shifted: at Carthage, from the fifth century BC, the supreme pair were Baal Hammon — 'lord of the incense altar' — and Tanit, who is called 'Face of Baal' and who begins to be named *before* him in the inscriptions, which suggests she came to matter more. Tanit may be of Libyan origin, fused with Phoenician Astarte. The tophet is the distinctive and terrible feature: an enclosed precinct at Carthage and elsewhere holding urns of cremated infants and small animals under votive stelae inscribed to Tanit and Baal Hammon. Whether these are sacrifices or a cemetery for children who died young remains one of the sharpest disputes in ancient history.

sources.title: Phoenician Religion
sources.author: Cartwright, Mark.
sources.mla: Cartwright, Mark. "Phoenician Religion." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Phoenician_Religion/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Melqart
gods.domain: Kingship, the city, the underworld, navigation, resurrection
gods.description: The god of Tyre — 'king of the city' — patron of the monarchy and of seafaring, and equated by the Greeks with Heracles. His annual festival celebrated his resurrection. Carthage sent a tenth of its yearly profit to his temple for generations.

gods.name: Astarte
gods.description: The great goddess — of desire and of aggressive war, the two together — worshipped from Sidon to Cyprus to Cádiz, and matched by the Greeks to Aphrodite and sometimes Artemis. In Sidon she formed a triad with Baal and Eshmun.

gods.name: Baal Hammon
gods.description: The supreme god of Carthage, though rare in the Phoenician homeland — 'lord of the incense altar', which connects him directly to the fire installations found in the tophets. The Romans assimilated him to Saturn.

gods.name: Tanit
gods.domain: Fertility, the moon, protection, war in defence
gods.description: The great goddess of Carthage, consort of Baal Hammon and titled 'Face of Baal' — and from the fifth century BC named *before* him in inscriptions, which suggests she overtook him in popular devotion. Possibly of Libyan origin, fused with Astarte. Her sign — a triangle topped by a bar and a disc — appears everywhere on Carthaginian stelae, though we don't actually know that the Carthaginians connected it with her.

gods.name: Eshmun
gods.description: The god of Sidon, patron of healing, identified by the Greeks with Asclepius and also compared to Adonis. His sanctuary outside Sidon is one of the best-preserved Phoenician religious sites.
gods.imageQuery: Temple of Eshmun Sidon Lebanon ruins

gods.name: Baal
gods.description: The storm god — rain, thunder, and therefore the harvest — inherited from the wider Canaanite world and invoked for good crops. 'Baal' means simply 'lord', and attaches to many gods.
gods.imageQuery: Baal storm god bronze figure Levant

gods.name: Reshef
gods.description: God of fire and lightning, and of the plague-arrow — equated by the Greeks with Apollo, who also shot disease from a distance.

buddhism.name: Buddhism
buddhism.description: Buddhism begins with a diagnosis, not a god. Siddhartha Gautama, active in northern India in the fifth century BCE, taught in his first sermon four propositions known — somewhat misleadingly — as the Four Noble Truths, since 'noble' refers not to the truths but to those with the insight to see them. First: dukkha. Usually rendered 'suffering', but the word is broader and harder — unsatisfactoriness, the standing-unstable of everything, the fact that nothing lasts and that this hurts. It covers birth, ageing, sickness, death, meeting what you don't want, being parted from what you do, not getting what you want, and finally the five aggregates that make up a person at all. Second: dukkha has a cause, and the cause is craving — thirst, grasping, clinging to a transient world. Third: therefore it can stop. That cessation is nirvana, literally a 'blowing out', the mind like a fire unbound. Fourth: there is a way to get there, the Eightfold Path — right view, intention, speech, action, livelihood, effort, mindfulness, and concentration — grouped into ethics, concentration, and wisdom, and described as a middle way between sensual indulgence and ascetic self-torture, both of which the Buddha had tried and found useless. Underneath sits anatta: no-self. There is no permanent, unchanging soul; there is nothing solid to grasp with, and no one there to do the grasping — only processes, arising and passing. The Buddha refused to speculate on metaphysics he considered unhelpful. He did not deny that gods exist; he simply held that they are not the point, being as caught in the cycle as anyone else.

sources.title: Four Noble Truths
sources.author: Lopez, Donald S.
sources.mla: Lopez, Donald S. "Four Noble Truths." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Four-Noble-Truths. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Buddha
gods.description: Not a god, and explicit about it — a human being who woke up. He is honoured, not worshipped; what is taken refuge in is the Buddha, the Dharma (the teaching), and the Sangha (the community). Later traditions elaborate him enormously, but the earliest texts have him refusing to be anything other than one who found a way and pointed at it.

buddhism.godsNote: Buddhism is non-theistic. There is no creator god and no soul, and the devas of Indian cosmology, while real in the texts, are themselves trapped in the cycle of rebirth and cannot help anyone escape it. Later schools (see mahayana, vajrayana) develop bodhisattvas and cosmic buddhas who function devotionally, but these are not creator deities.

jainism.name: Jainism
jainism.description: Jainism teaches a path to liberation through disciplined non-violence to every living thing, and it means every living thing — not only humans and large animals, but insects, plants, and microbes. Its history runs through twenty-four tirthankaras, 'ford-makers', supreme teachers who crossed the river of rebirth and showed the crossing; the last two, Parshvanatha (c. 8th–7th century BCE) and Mahavira (c. 599–527 BCE, a contemporary of the Buddha), are historical. Mahavira did not found the religion so much as consolidate it, adding celibacy as a fifth great vow and organizing a fourfold community of monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen that has held its shape for two and a half millennia. There is no creator god. The universe is eternal and runs on natural law. What binds the soul (jiva) is karma — and in Jainism karma is not a process or a moral bookkeeping system but an actual subtle *substance*, a material film that adheres to the soul and weighs it down, and which must be stopped from entering and then burned off through austerity. Three ethical pillars carry everything: ahimsa, non-violence; aparigraha, non-possession, taken so seriously that Digambara monks wear nothing at all; and anekantavada, many-sidedness — the doctrine that reality is complex and multi-aspected, that every viewpoint is partial, and that no single statement can exhaust the truth, so that each claim should be qualified with syat, 'in some sense'. Dogmatism, on this view, is itself a kind of violence. Jain monks sweep the path before them, filter their water, and refuse root vegetables. It is the most rigorous ethic of non-harm any human culture has produced.

sources.title: Jainism
sources.author: Strohl, G. Ralph.
sources.mla: Strohl, G. Ralph. "Jainism." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Jainism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Mahavira
gods.description: The last of the twenty-four ford-makers, a contemporary of the Buddha, who renounced a royal life at thirty, spent twelve years in extreme asceticism, and then taught for thirty. His emblem is the lion. He is revered and his image is worshipped in temples — but as an exemplar who achieved liberation, not as a god who can grant it.

gods.name: Rishabhanatha
gods.description: The first ford-maker of the present cosmic cycle, credited in tradition with establishing civilized society itself. His emblem is the bull. Named in the Rigveda, according to Jain tradition.

gods.name: Parshvanatha
gods.description: The twenty-third ford-maker, dated by historians to the 8th or 7th century BCE and probably the earliest historical figure in the tradition. His emblem is the serpent, and he is shown with a many-headed cobra rearing over him. He taught four vows; Mahavira added the fifth.

jainism.godsNote: Jainism has no creator god. The tirthankaras are liberated souls (siddhas), venerated as perfect examples and as objects of devotion — but they have passed entirely beyond the world and cannot intervene in it. Lesser deities exist in Jain cosmology but are themselves within the cycle of rebirth.

taoism.name: Taoism
taoism.description: The Dao is the Way — the fundamental principle and the rhythmic fluctuation from which the cosmos generates itself, an indeterminate force that latently contains everything and spontaneously brings it forth. It cannot be adequately named, and the Daodejing says so in its first line. Attributed to Laozi, a shadowy figure of the sixth century BCE about whom almost nothing is known and who was probably not its author, the Daodejing is addressed to the sage-ruler and turns on wu wei — 'non-action', which does not mean doing nothing but not forcing: acting spontaneously, without straining against the grain, letting things take their course. Politically it means avoiding war, harsh laws, and heavy taxes. Personally it means returning to one's natural state and treating conventional wisdom and language as biased and artificial, which is why the text is so full of paradox. The Zhuangzi, from the fourth to third century BCE, is the other pillar and the greater literary work — and where the Daodejing addresses the ruler, the Zhuangzi is the first Chinese text to offer a philosophy for private life, a wisdom for the individual, full of butterflies, useless trees, and cheerful indifference to death. Then, in 142 CE, Zhang Daoling announced that Laozi had appeared to him and commanded him to establish a new order — and Daoism became a religion, with a priesthood, liturgy, and a lineage of Celestial Masters. Laozi himself was deified as Taishang Laojun. Religious Daoism developed alchemy, breathing and dietary regimens, a vast pantheon of gods and immortals, and techniques for communicating with them, and the movements at Mount Mao at one point outdrew the Celestial Masters themselves.

sources.title: Way of the Celestial Masters
sources.mla: "Way of the Celestial Masters." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Way_of_the_Celestial_Masters. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Dao
gods.domain: The source and pattern of everything
gods.description: Not a god — the Way itself: unseen but not transcendent, immensely powerful and supremely humble, the root of all things and the pattern they follow when left alone. Naming it is already to have missed it.

gods.name: Taishang Laojun
gods.description: Laozi, elevated into a god — the principal deity of the Celestial Masters, who appeared to Zhang Daoling in 142 CE and designated him and his successors as his representatives on earth. The philosopher becomes the revelation.

gods.name: The Three Pure Ones
gods.description: The supreme triad of religious Daoism — the Jade Pure, the Upper Pure, and the Grand Pure — presiding over the highest heavens and the transmission of the scriptures. Taishang Laojun is the third of them.
gods.imageQuery: Three Pure Ones Sanqing Daoist painting

gods.name: The Jade Emperor
gods.domain: Rule of heaven, the celestial bureaucracy
gods.description: The ruler of heaven in popular Daoism and Chinese folk religion, at the head of a celestial bureaucracy that mirrors the imperial one with unnerving exactness — complete with ministries, paperwork, and promotions.

gods.name: The Eight Immortals
gods.description: The most beloved figures in Daoist popular religion — eight transcendents who achieved immortality and travel together, each with an emblem and a personality: the cripple with the gourd, the flute-player, the woman with the lotus, the drunkard.

taoism.godsNote: Philosophical Daoism (the Daodejing, the Zhuangzi) is not theistic — the Dao is a principle, not a person. Religious Daoism, which emerged from the second century CE, developed an enormous pantheon. Both are Daoism, and the relationship between them is genuinely contested by scholars.

shinto.name: Shinto
shinto.description: Shinto is the way of the kami. The word 'kami' is not a synonym for 'god': it covers deities, ancestors, sacred presences, and forces — anything that inspires awe, which can be a storm, a waterfall, an emperor, a fox, or a particularly old tree. The classics speak of the yao-yorozu no kami, the eight million kami, which is a way of saying: uncountable. In the beginning a certain number of kami simply emerged, and the pair Izanagi and Izanami gave birth to the Japanese islands and to the kami who became the ancestors of the clans. From Izanagi's purification came the three most important: Amaterasu, the sun, born from his left eye; Tsukuyomi, the moon, from his right; and Susanoo, the storm, from his nose. Amaterasu's great myth is the rock cave: outraged by her brother's violence, she hides in a cave and the world goes dark, and the other kami lure her out with a mirror hung on a sakaki tree, crowing cocks, and a bawdy dance by Ame-no-Uzume that makes the assembled gods roar with laughter — curiosity, not force, brings the sun back. Her descendant Jimmu became the first emperor, and the mirror, sword, and jewels she gave her grandson remain the imperial regalia. Her shrine at Ise is the holiest place in Shinto. There is no weekly service and no creed. There are shrines, marked by a torii gate, with a basin for washing and an inner sanctuary housing the object in which the kami is present; there is purification; and there are matsuri, the festivals. Most Japanese are Shinto and Buddhist at once, with two altars in the house — the kami for life, the Buddha for death.

sources.title: Shinto: Shinto Literature and Mythology
sources.mla: "Shinto: Shinto Literature and Mythology." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinto/Shinto-literature-and-mythology. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Amaterasu Ōmikami
gods.domain: The sun, the heavens, the imperial line
gods.description: The sun kami and ruler of Takamagahara, the High Celestial Plain — born from Izanagi's left eye, and the mythical ancestress of the imperial house through her grandson Ninigi. Her withdrawal into the rock cave plunges the world into darkness; she is drawn out by a mirror, a rooster, and a dance. Her shrine at Ise is the most venerated in Japan.
gods.imageQuery: Amaterasu sun goddess cave woodblock print

gods.name: Susanoo
gods.description: The impetuous storm kami, born from Izanagi's nose — brother of Amaterasu, whose violence in heaven drives her into the cave, and who is expelled for it. In exile he redeems himself by slaying the eight-headed serpent Yamata-no-Orochi and finding a sword in its tail.

gods.name: Tsukuyomi
gods.description: The moon kami, born from Izanagi's right eye — the third of the 'Three Precious Children', and by far the quietest. He kills the food goddess in a fit of disgust at her manners, and Amaterasu never looks at him again, which is why the sun and moon are never in the sky together.

gods.name: Izanagi and Izanami
gods.domain: Creation, the islands, life and death
gods.description: The pair who stirred the ocean with a jewelled spear and made the Japanese islands from the drops, then gave birth to the kami. Izanami dies bearing fire; Izanagi goes to Yomi, the land of the dead, to fetch her back, looks at her when told not to, sees what she has become, and flees — and the purification after that flight produces Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi, and Susanoo.

gods.name: Inari
gods.description: The kami of rice and, by extension, of prosperity and business — with more shrines in Japan than any other kami, and attended by fox messengers. The tunnels of vermilion torii at Fushimi Inari are hers.

gods.name: Ame-no-Uzume
gods.description: The kami of dawn and revelry, who danced on an upturned tub outside Amaterasu's cave, partially disrobing, and made the eight hundred myriad gods laugh so hard that the sun got curious and came out. The origin of sacred dance.

gods.name: Hachiman
gods.domain: War, archery, the protection of Japan
gods.description: The kami of war and archery, tutelary of warriors and of the Minamoto clan — and one of the clearest cases of Shinto-Buddhist fusion, having been treated for centuries as a bodhisattva as well.

shinto.godsNote: 'Kami' is not equivalent to 'god'. It includes deities, ancestors, natural forces, and sacred presences, and Shinto has no fixed hierarchy across all shrines and communities. The tradition counts eight million of them, which is a figure of speech meaning: innumerable.

confucianism.name: Confucianism
confucianism.description: Confucius — Kong Fuzi, 551–479 BCE — said he transmitted rather than invented, and was faithful to the ancients. What he transmitted was a claim about how human beings become fully human. His concerns are ethical rather than metaphysical, and they are organized around a handful of terms. Ren is humaneness, benevolence, the inherent nature of humanity: it lives in the self and manifests as care for others, and Confucius treats it as a virtue that comes from Heaven. Its most famous formulation is negative and all the better for it — do not impose on others what you would not wish for yourself. Li is ritual propriety: the proper performance of ceremony, custom, and everyday manners, from state sacrifices to how one greets a neighbour. Without li, ren is only a feeling with nothing to guide it into action; li is the instrument through which family, state, and world are aligned with the moral order of Tian, Heaven. Xiao is filial piety, respect and devotion toward parents and ancestors, and Confucius makes it the foundation of every other relationship. Above them stands Tian — Heaven — which is aligned with moral order but depends on human agents to actualize its will, and which endows people with the capacity to act rather than intervening for them. Confucius was himself a ritual and sacrificial master, and he did not dismiss the gods: he said that to pray properly to any of them, one must first know and respect Heaven, and that a sacrifice performed by proxy is the same as no sacrifice at all. The tradition became the curriculum of the Chinese state under Han Wudi and stayed there for two thousand years.

sources.title: Confucius
sources.mla: "Confucius." Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, https://iep.utm.edu/confucius/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Tian
gods.domain: Moral order, the mandate, the pattern of the cosmos
gods.description: Heaven — not a personal god exactly, and not merely the sky. Tian is aligned with the moral order and is its source, but depends on human beings to actualize its will. It gives the seasons and gives life, and it grants the Mandate (tianming) to rule, which can be withdrawn. Confucius says its power is pervasive and that it responds to a sincere heart.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: Not gods but the dead of one's own line, to whom offerings are owed. The Analects records that Confucius always set aside a portion of his food for them. Ancestor veneration is the practical face of xiao, filial piety, and it is the most durable religious practice in Chinese life.

gods.name: The shen
gods.description: The gods and spirits that enliven nature and the household — the god of the stove, the god of the soil. Confucius held it appropriate to worship them, but only through proper rites, and only by someone who first knows and respects Heaven. He declined to speculate about them further.
gods.imageQuery: Chinese kitchen god stove deity print

gods.name: Confucius
gods.domain: Learning, ritual, the transmission of the Way
gods.description: Himself the object of state cult for two millennia, with temples across China where offerings were made — though not as a supreme being, and not as anything approaching Tian or the Dao. A teacher, elevated.

confucianism.godsNote: Whether Confucianism is a religion at all is genuinely contested — it is simultaneously a philosophy, an ethic, a political programme, and a state cult. Tian is a moral ordering principle rather than a personal creator, and Confucius consistently redirected metaphysical questions back to human conduct.

tengrism.name: Tengrism
tengrism.description: Tengrism is the religion of the open steppe, and its central figure is the sky itself: Tengri, Köke Möngke Tengri, the Eternal Blue Sky. It is not a sky god who lives in the sky; it is the sky, vast and impartial, the source of order and of the mandate to rule. Beneath it is Umai, the earth-and-fertility mother, protector of women and infants, and below her Erlik, the lord of the world below. The cosmos is three-tiered — upper, middle, lower — joined by a world tree, and the specialist who moves between the tiers is the shaman (kam), using drum, trance, and helper spirits. The moral demand is not doctrinal but practical: live in balance with the world, honour the ancestors, respect the master-spirits of rivers, mountains, and animals, and take from the land only what is offered. Tengri legitimated the Turkic khaganates and, most consequentially, the Mongol empire: Genghis Khan attributed his rise directly to the Eternal Blue Sky, and the Mongol claim to universal rule was framed as a mandate from it. That claim came with a corollary the medieval world found startling — since all religions ultimately pray to the same sky, none needs to be suppressed, and Mongol rulers extended a religious tolerance that European visitors found bewildering. The Orkhon inscriptions of the eighth century, the oldest surviving Turkic texts, open by invoking Tengri above and the brown earth below. Tengrism was displaced by Buddhism and Islam across most of Inner Asia, but it never fully went away, and it is being consciously revived today in Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, Tatarstan, Buryatia, and Sakha.

gods.name: Tengri
gods.description: The Eternal Blue Sky — the supreme power, and not a person so much as the sky itself, impartial and immense. It grants the mandate to rule and withdraws it. Genghis Khan credited his empire directly to it. The Orkhon inscriptions begin with it.
gods.imageQuery: Tengri eternal blue sky Mongolia steppe

gods.name: Umai
gods.domain: Earth, fertility, childbirth, the protection of infants
gods.description: The mother goddess of the earth and the womb — protector of women in labour and of small children, and the necessary counterpart to the sky above. Her name is still used for the placenta in some Turkic languages.

gods.name: Erlik
gods.description: The lord of the world below — the one the shaman descends to bargain with when a soul has been taken. Not simply evil: the necessary lower half of a three-tiered cosmos.

gods.name: Master-spirits of place
gods.description: Every mountain, spring, tree, and species has an owner who must be asked before anything is taken. Ribbons tied to trees at sacred sites, and the ovoo cairns of the Mongolian steppe, are addressed to them.
gods.imageQuery: ovoo cairn Mongolia sacred site ribbons

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: The dead of the clan, honoured and consulted, and understood as remaining involved in the fortunes of the living. Ancestor veneration is as central to Tengrist practice as the sky itself.

hopi.name:
Hopewell
Shamanism
hopi.description: Between roughly 100 BCE and 500 CE, along the rivers of the Ohio valley and across a huge swathe of eastern North America, people built earthworks of a scale and precision that still isn't fully explained. The Hopewell tradition was not a single culture or a single society; it was a widely dispersed set of populations connected by an enormous network of trade routes — an exchange system reaching from Lake Ontario to Florida, and from the Rockies to the Atlantic, carrying obsidian, mica, copper, shark teeth, and grizzly bear canines across a continent. Those routes were also communication networks, and a means of bringing people together for ceremonies. The earthworks themselves — the eight complexes now inscribed by UNESCO as the Hopewell Ceremonial Earthworks — are huge geometric enclosures: squares, circles, octagons, executed with a consistency of form, technique, and dimension across a vast region, and sculpted hilltops enclosing level plazas. They are aligned to the cycles of the sun and, more remarkably, to the far more complex 18.6-year cycle of the moon, which requires generations of sustained observation to detect at all. They were built by dispersed, non-hierarchical groups, and they were ceremonial centres, not cities. Buried within and beneath them are finely crafted ritual objects made from raw materials obtained from extraordinary distances — the whole continent was being drawn toward these places, in the form of goods and, presumably, of people. What was believed there is unrecoverable. The mounds and the exotic grave goods and the lunar alignments are what remain, and they are not nothing: they establish astronomy, ceremony, hierarchy in death, and a continental sense of the sacred.

sources.title: Hopewell Ceremonial Earthworks
sources.mla: "Hopewell Ceremonial Earthworks." UNESCO World Heritage Centre, https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1689. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Hopewell People
sources.mla: "Hopewell People." EBSCO Research Starters, https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/anthropology/hopewell-people. Accessed 14 July 2026.

hopi.godsNote: No Hopewell deity is known. The name itself comes from a nineteenth-century Ohio farmer on whose land the earthworks stood. What survives is architecture, astronomy, burial practice, and traded materials — not names, not myths, and not a pantheon. Inventing one would be fabrication.

anasazi.name:
Anasazi
Shamanism
anasazi.description: The Ancestral Puebloans — long called Anasazi, a Navajo word their descendants dislike — farmed maize across the Four Corners from the beginnings of cultivation until the Spanish arrived in 1540, and left behind pit houses, great pueblos, and the cliff dwellings at Mesa Verde. Their religion is legible mainly through one structure: the kiva. A kiva is a subterranean ceremonial chamber, usually round — a shape that recalls the older pit houses from which it descends, and which stands in deliberate contrast to the square and rectangular rooms people actually lived in. It is entered from above, by ladder. In the floor is a small hole, sometimes cut through a plank, sometimes just dug: the sipapu, in Hopi, the symbolic place from which the people emerged into this world. That single detail is the theological centre of the whole architecture — the community gathers by climbing down into the earth, and the earth is where they came from. Kivas were used for ritual, and also for political meetings and for the ordinary gathering of the men of the village; women's ceremonies were held elsewhere, and women rarely entered them. Their walls carry murals painted in warm mineral pigments on adobe plaster, geometric and linear rather than curved, laid out in bands around the room, and repainted many times over, so that archaeologists have peeled apart the layers. The wider religious system is one that Ancestral Pueblo peoples' descendants still hold: a set of beliefs in which rain is sacred and the annual cycle of ritual is organized, above all, around bringing it.

sources.title: Ancestral Puebloan
sources.mla: "Ancestral Puebloan." National Park Service, https://www.nps.gov/subjects/swscience/ancestral-puebloan.htm. Accessed 14 July 2026.

anasazi.godsNote: No named Ancestral Puebloan deity survives from the archaeological record. The living Pueblo peoples — Hopi, Zuni, and the Rio Grande pueblos — are their descendants and hold rich religious traditions with named beings (katsinam among them), but much of that is deliberately not shared with outsiders, and reading it backward onto the archaeological record would be presumptuous. What the record itself shows is the kiva, the sipapu, and an annual cycle of ritual centred on rain.

olmec.name:
Olmec
Shamanism
olmec.description: The Olmec left colossal stone heads, jade celts, and a set of supernatural beings nobody can name. Their religion is reconstructed entirely from iconography, and the reconstruction is a running argument. The single most-discussed figure is the so-called were-jaguar — a snarling, downturned mouth, almond eyes, a cleft head, often depicted as an infant held in an adult's arms — which was long read as a jaguar-human hybrid and connected to shamanic transformation and to rulership. That reading is now under sustained attack: the very term 'were-jaguar' is a modern coinage, and Carolyn Tate has argued the imagery is not jaguar at all but embryo-and-maize-kernel iconography, which would change everything. Whether the were-jaguar is the same being as the Rain Spirit is also disputed — Christopher Pool and Karl Taube equate them, Peter Joralemon separates them, and Taube later proposed that the Rain Spirit is actually the seed-phase of the Maize God. The Feathered Serpent, which would become Quetzalcoatl, appears first in Olmec times, on La Venta Stele 19 and in a cave painting at Juxtlahuaca hundreds of miles away — though Richard Diehl thinks its rarity means it was a minor figure. What is not disputed is the influence. Marshall Saville proposed in 1929 that Olmec deities were the forerunners of later Mesoamerican gods, and Miguel Covarrubias drew a famous family tree in 1957 tracing nineteen later rain deities back to a single jaguar-masked figure on a votive axe. That continuity hypothesis is now generally accepted — how far Olmec influence actually reached is still argued.

sources.title: Olmec Gods: Serpents, Jaguars, and Deities of Rain
sources.mla: "Olmec Gods: Serpents, Jaguars, and Deities of Rain." Mexico Histórico, https://www.mexicohistorico.com/paginas/Olmec-Gods--Serpents--Jaguars--and-Deities-of-Rain.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The 'were-jaguar'
gods.domain: Rain and storms — possibly; rulership; possibly maize
gods.description: The most-depicted and most-argued-over Olmec supernatural: downturned snarling mouth, cleft head, almond eyes, frequently shown as a limp infant in an adult's arms. Long read as a jaguar-human hybrid tied to shamanic transformation and to the legitimacy of rulers. Pool calls it flatly the god of rain and storms; Joralemon separates it from the Rain Spirit; Tate argues the whole 'were-jaguar' concept is a modern misreading of embryo and maize-kernel imagery. The term itself was coined only in the 1980s.

gods.name: The Maize God
gods.description: The god of the crop everything depended on — shown with maize sprouting from a cleft in the head. Taube has proposed that the Rain Spirit is really the seed-phase of this same deity, which would collapse two of the biggest categories in Olmec iconography into one.
gods.imageQuery: Olmec maize god sculpture cleft head

gods.name: The Feathered Serpent
gods.description: The earliest appearance in Mesoamerica of the being who becomes Quetzalcoatl — a serpent with feathers, joining sky to earth. It appears on La Venta Stele 19 and in a cave painting at Juxtlahuaca, hundreds of miles apart; but Diehl argues its rarity suggests it was a minor member of the pantheon, not a major one.
gods.imageQuery: Olmec feathered serpent La Venta Stele 19

gods.name: The Bird Monster
gods.description: A raptorial sky supernatural — associated with rulership, and among the recurring beings Joralemon catalogued by Roman numeral because nobody knows what the Olmec called any of them.

gods.name: The Fish Monster
gods.description: A shark-like supernatural with crossed bands, a dorsal fin, and a split tail — shown in full-body form on San Lorenzo Monument 58 and in profile on the Las Limas figure.

olmec.godsNote: No Olmec god has a known name — the script is barely attested and undeciphered. Scholars refer to these beings by Roman numerals (Joralemon's God I through God X) or by descriptive nicknames, and even the count is unstable: Joralemon himself later merged and split several. Everything here is inference from images.

mayan.name:
Mayan
Polytheism
mayan.description: The Maya cosmos has thirteen heavens above, the earth, and nine levels of underworld below — and the underworld, Xibalba, is not an abstraction but a place with roads and houses and lords with names like One Death and Seven Death. The Popol Vuh, written down in K'iche' in the sixteenth century but far older, preserves the oldest complete Maya myth: Hun Hunahpu and his brother are lured to Xibalba for a ballgame, defeated, and executed; Hun Hunahpu's severed head is hung in a tree, where it becomes a calabash and speaks to a passing maiden and impregnates her by spitting in her hand. Her sons are the Hero Twins, Hunahpu and Xbalanque, who grow up, go down to Xibalba, out-trick and out-play the lords of death, defeat them, and rise into the sky — and their father is reborn as the Maize God. Maize was not merely the food; it was the substance human beings were made of. The high god is Itzamná, lord of the sky and the day, inventor of writing, patron of learning — so exalted that he is almost invisible in the art, overshadowed by more dramatic figures. The one everyone actually depicted is Chaac, the rain god, reptilian, wielding an axe that makes the lightning, and answered to by every farmer; in severe droughts, offerings — including, at times, children — were cast into the sacred cenotes for him. Ix Chel is the moon, midwifery, and weaving, and also a crone of floods and destruction. Blood was the medium of contact: rulers and elites drew their own with obsidian blades and stingray spines, and captives taken in war were sacrificed after victories.

sources.title: List of Maya Gods and Supernatural Beings
sources.mla: "List of Maya Gods and Supernatural Beings." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Maya_gods_and_supernatural_beings. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Maya Gods & Deities: A Scholarly Guide to the Divine Pantheon
sources.mla: "Maya Gods & Deities: A Scholarly Guide to the Divine Pantheon." Mayan.org, https://mayan.org/gods/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Itzamná
gods.domain: The sky, creation, writing, learning, the day
gods.description: The supreme creator — lord of the heavens and the day cycle, inventor of writing, patron of learning, the source of all knowledge and culture. So high above the other gods that he is barely visible in the art, outshone by Chaac and Kukulkán. In the codices he is an old man with a huge nose, sunken cheeks, and a single tooth.

gods.name: Chaac
gods.description: The rain god, and the most frequently depicted deity in Maya art — reptilian, long-snouted, carrying an axe with which he makes the thunder. Every region had its own local Chaac. Farmers held ceremonies to summon him, and offerings were thrown into cenotes; in severe droughts these included children.
gods.imageQuery: Chaac Maya rain god mask Uxmal

gods.name: The Maize God
gods.description: Decapitated in Xibalba, hung in a tree as a calabash, and reborn through his sons — the pattern of the maize itself, buried and rising. He is shown young, beautiful, and flourishing, surrounded by vegetation. Human beings, in the Popol Vuh, are made of maize dough; he is the flesh people are made of.

gods.name: Kukulkán
gods.description: The Feathered Serpent — the Yucatec Kukulkán and the K'iche' Q'uq'umatz, who with Tepeu creates humanity in the Popol Vuh, and the close cousin of the Aztec Quetzalcoatl. At Chichén Itzá the equinox sun throws a serpent of shadow down the pyramid stair.

gods.name: The Hero Twins
gods.domain: Trickery, the ballgame, the defeat of death
gods.description: The central figures of the Popol Vuh and the oldest Maya myth preserved whole — twins who descend to Xibalba to avenge their father, survive the houses of knives and bats and cold, out-trick the lords of death at the ballgame, and rise into the sky. Plausibly the mythic ancestors of Maya ruling lineages.

gods.name: Ix Chel
gods.description: The moon goddess — patroness of childbirth, medicine, and weaving, and consort of Itzamná. Shown holding a serpent or pouring water from a jar. Also a destroyer: as a crone she brings storms and floods. Midwives and healers made pilgrimage to her sanctuary on Cozumel.
gods.imageQuery: Ix Chel Maya moon goddess codex

gods.name: Kinich Ahau
gods.description: The sun god — square-eyed, filed teeth, a k'in sign on his brow — who crosses the sky by day and passes through the underworld as a jaguar by night.

gods.name: Hun Came and Vucub Came
gods.description: The lords of Xibalba, who kill Hun Hunahpu and are finally out-tricked and destroyed by his sons. In the Popol Vuh the underworld is not an abstract state but a place, with a bureaucracy of death and a ballcourt.

teotihuacan.name:
Teotihuacan
Polytheism
teotihuacan.description: Teotihuacan is the great anonymous city. At its height it was among the largest urban centres in the world, laid out on a grid around a two-kilometre ceremonial avenue the Aztecs later called the Avenue of the Dead, dominated by the Pyramid of the Sun — over 200 feet tall, built over a cave, the tallest structure in the Americas at the time — and the Pyramid of the Moon. We do not know what language its people spoke, and we do not know the names of their gods. What we have is iconography, and the iconography recurs, unmistakably, in Aztec religion eight centuries later. So the gods of Teotihuacan are conventionally called by their Aztec names, with the understanding that this is a convenience and possibly a distortion. The Storm God, whom the Aztecs called Tlaloc, is everywhere — goggle-eyed, fanged, god of rain and fertility and also of the destructive power of water, in a valley where water was the whole question. The Feathered Serpent, later Quetzalcoatl, gives its name to the Temple in the Ciudadela, whose facade alternates serpent heads with another creature and beneath which were found the burials of more than a hundred people, many of them young men in warrior gear, hands tied behind their backs. And there is a figure with no clear Aztec descendant: the being scholars call the Great Goddess of Teotihuacan, possibly the city's principal deity, associated with water, fertility, the earth, and creation, shown in the Tepantitla murals presiding over a paradise of flowing water — represented by a basalt statue over three metres high found near the Pyramid of the Moon, and sometimes called the Spider Woman. If she was the chief god of the city, Teotihuacan had a female supreme deity, which is unusual and interesting and unprovable.

sources.title: Teotihuacan
sources.author: Cartwright, Mark.
sources.mla: Cartwright, Mark. "Teotihuacan." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Teotihuacan/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Temple of the Feathered Serpent, Teotihuacan
sources.mla: "Temple of the Feathered Serpent, Teotihuacan." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Temple_of_the_Feathered_Serpent,_Teotihuacan. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Teotihuacan: Ancient City of Pyramids
sources.mla: "Teotihuacan: Ancient City of Pyramids." Live Science, https://www.livescience.com/22545-teotihuacan.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Great Goddess of Teotihuacan
gods.description: Possibly the city's principal deity, and possibly female — which would be remarkable. Associated with water, the earth, fertility, and creation itself, and represented by a basalt statue 3.2 metres high found near the Pyramid of the Moon, dating to before 300 CE. She presides over the watery paradise in the Tepantitla murals. She has no clear Aztec successor, which is why her name is a modern label and her identity is disputed.

gods.name: The Storm God
gods.description: Goggle-eyed and fanged, and the most ubiquitous image in the city — god of the rain that made agriculture possible, and equally of the floods and thunderstorms that ruined it. The Aztecs called him Tlaloc; what Teotihuacan called him is unknown.

gods.name: The Feathered Serpent
gods.domain: Fertility of the earth, wind, sky and earth joined
gods.description: The serpent with quetzal feathers, whose temple in the Ciudadela carries some of the earliest known representations of the figure — its facade covered in serpent heads alternating with another, more contested creature. Beneath it were buried more than a hundred people, dated to 150–200 CE, many bound and apparently sacrificed.
gods.imageQuery: Temple of the Feathered Serpent Teotihuacan facade

gods.name: The Old Fire God
gods.description: A hunched old man with a brazier on his head — one of the oldest deities in Mesoamerica, and a creator figure. Represented in stone braziers found throughout the city's apartment compounds.

gods.name: The Water Goddess
gods.description: Goddess of the waters — represented at Teotihuacan in a three-metre stone statue. In a valley this dry, the preoccupation with water gods is not hard to explain.

gods.name: The Flayed God
gods.domain: Agricultural renewal, maize, the shedding of skin
gods.description: 'Our Lord the Flayed One' — the god of springtime renewal, whose priests wore the skins of sacrificial victims, on the logic that the seed also sheds its husk. Present in Teotihuacan's art and architecture long before the Aztecs.

teotihuacan.godsNote: The names of Teotihuacan's gods are not known — the city's language and script are undeciphered. The iconography is the same as the later Aztec pantheon's, so the Aztec names are used by convention. This is a scholarly convenience, and it may well be importing Aztec meanings into a religion that had different ones.
imageQueries: Avenue of the Dead Teotihuacan aerial

polynesian.name:
Polynesian
Animism
polynesian.description: Polynesian religion is one system stretched across the largest ocean on earth, so that the same gods appear under transformed names from Hawai'i to Aotearoa to Rapa Nui — Tangaroa in Maori, Ta'aroa in Tahiti, Tangaloa in Samoa, Kanaloa in Hawai'i. Creation is told two ways. In Samoa and the Society Islands there is an uncreated creator, Tangaloa, who exists in the darkness of contemplation and calls the other gods into being. In eastern Polynesia, and above all among the Maori, existence differentiates into a male sky, Rangi, and a female earth, Papa, locked together in an embrace, with their sons crushed in the darkness between them. The sons debate what to do. Tāne, god of forests, lies on his back and pushes his father up with his legs — and light comes into the world. Tāwhirimātea, god of storms, disagreed, and rose with his father, and has attacked his brothers ever since; Tangaroa's fish fled into the sea, the reptiles into Tāne's forests, and Tangaroa has never forgiven him for sheltering them, which is why the sea eats the land and the land makes canoes and nets from Tāne's wood to take the sea's children. Rangi and Papa have never been reconciled: her sighs rise as mist, his tears fall as dew. Everything in the world is descended from someone, connected by whakapapa, genealogy, and everything possesses mauri, life force. Two concepts govern conduct. Mana is the state of being that comes from the strengthening influence of the gods — chiefs have great mana, and so do women, whose evidence is the ability to reproduce. Tapu is prohibition, protecting mana from contact that would compete with or defile it. Breaking a serious tapu could cost a life. Above all the gods in popularity is Māui, the trickster, who fished up islands and slowed the sun.

sources.title: Polynesian Religions: An Overview
sources.mla: "Polynesian Religions: An Overview." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/polynesian-religions-overview. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Polynesian Culture: Religion
sources.mla: "Polynesian Culture: Religion." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/place/Polynesia/Religion. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Religion of Māori People
sources.mla: "Religion of Māori People." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religion_of_M%C4%81ori_people. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Tangaroa
gods.description: God of the ocean and ancestor of everything that lives in it. In western Polynesia he is the supreme being and the uncreated creator who raised the islands from the depths; in eastern Polynesia he is one of the sons of Rangi and Papa and specifically the sea. Fishermen and voyagers make offerings to him before setting out — because to go to sea is to enter the realm of Tāne's enemy.

gods.name: Tāne
gods.domain: Forests, birds, light, the making of humanity
gods.description: God of the forest and of the birds and trees in it — and the one who separated his parents and let the light in, pushing his father up with his legs. He shapes the first woman from the earth and breathes life into her. He gives the wood for canoes, spears, and fishhooks. In Hawai'i, as Kāne, he heads the pantheon.
gods.imageQuery: Tane Mahuta forest god Maori carving

gods.name: Tū
gods.description: God of war — and the only one of the brothers who stood against Tāwhirimātea's onslaught and beat him. He then took revenge on the brothers who had abandoned him, trapping Tāne's birds, netting Tangaroa's fish, and uprooting Rongo's crops — which is why humans may eat all of them. In Hawai'i, as Kū, he received human sacrifice.

gods.name: Rongo
gods.description: God of cultivated plants — the kumara above all — and of peaceful pursuits. In Hawai'i, as Lono, he presides over the Makahiki festival; and when Captain Cook arrived during it, in 1778, the Hawaiians took him for Lono, with consequences that turned out badly for everyone.

gods.name: Tāwhirimātea
gods.description: God of the winds — the one son who opposed the separation of the parents, and who rose into the sky with his father and has been attacking his brothers ever since with the four great winds, the hurricanes, and the rain.

gods.name: Rangi and Papa
gods.description: The primal parents, locked together in an embrace, their children crushed in the dark between them until Tāne forced them apart. They have never been reconciled to the separation: Papa's sighs rise to Rangi as mist, and Rangi's tears fall to Papa as dew.
gods.imageQuery: Rangi Papa sky father earth mother Maori

gods.name: Māui
gods.domain: Trickery, fire, the sun, the fishing-up of islands
gods.description: Not a god exactly — a demigod, and by far the most popular figure in Polynesian tradition, known from Hawai'i to New Zealand. He fished up islands with a magic hook, snared the sun and beat it until it agreed to move slower, stole fire for humanity, and died trying to win immortality, which is why we don't have it.

gods.name: Pele
gods.description: The Hawaiian goddess of the volcano — the most feared and most present deity in the islands, who lives in Kīlauea and whose lava both destroys the land and makes it. Beautiful, jealous, and utterly unpredictable.

gods.name: Mana and tapu
gods.description: Not deities but the two concepts that structure everything. Mana is the state of being that comes from the gods' strengthening influence — possessed by chiefs, by women, by stones, tools, and buildings. Tapu is the prohibition that protects it. In some societies, if a commoner touched a chief's shadow, only that person's death could restore the chief's mana.

polynesian.godsNote: Polynesian deities have been classified as supreme, 'departmental' (Tāne, Rongo, Tū, Tangaroa — each ruling an aspect of nature), tribal, and family. Most were strictly local. Ancestor deification was probably the original form, and the process of making gods continued right up to Christianization in the early nineteenth century.

micronesian.name:
Micronesian
Animism
micronesian.description: Micronesian religion is decentralized to a degree that early European visitors mistook for absence — they looked for temples and lavish trappings, found none, and concluded there was no religion. In fact there were no temples because the spirits were addressed in small shrines, indoors and out, and in the family dwelling and the boathouse. The cosmos is populated by énú (the Chuukic term, with cognates across the islands): nature spirits bound to reefs, trees, and animals; goblin-like things hiding in the jungle and on the reef; a family of high sky gods, distant but not disengaged; and, most numerous and most important of all, the spirits of the ancestors. The relationship with the ancestral dead is characteristically ambivalent — approach and avoidance — because the spirit may be fickle, or the relative may have been of ambiguous character in life, and so the same ghost might help or harm. On Yap and Chuuk each person has two souls, a good one that becomes a helping spirit and can possess living kin, and an evil one. Because the sea is the whole problem of life here, navigation is sacred: Aluluei, god of seafaring, was killed by his jealous brothers and brought back by his father Pälülop in spirit form with many eyes, and those eyes became the stars that navigators steer by. Myths about him carried real navigational instruction. Trickster figures recur — Olofat in the Carolines, Nareau in Kiribati, Letao in the Marshalls, each often doubling as a creator. Rituals were led by diviners and mediums who worked by trance and possession; possession trance persists on Chuuk, where an ancestor descending onto the shoulders of a living woman gives her the standing to say things she otherwise could not. Nan Madol, the megalithic city of ninety-odd artificial islets off Pohnpei, was the ceremonial centre of the Saudeleur dynasty and is still, in local telling, the residence of gods and kings.

sources.title: Micronesian Religions: An Overview
sources.author: Lessa, William A., and Jay Dobbin.
sources.mla: Lessa, William A., and Jay Dobbin. "Micronesian Religions: An Overview." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/micronesian-religions-overview. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Nan Madol: Ceremonial Centre of Eastern Micronesia
sources.mla: "Nan Madol: Ceremonial Centre of Eastern Micronesia." UNESCO World Heritage Centre, https://whc.unesco.org/en/list/1503/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Aluluei
gods.description: The god of navigation, killed by his jealous brothers Big Rong and Little Rong and restored in spirit form by his father Pälülop, who gave him many eyes that shine — and those eyes are the stars. Myths about him were carriers of actual navigational instruction, which is why they were told so precisely.
gods.imageQuery: Micronesian stick chart navigation Marshall Islands

gods.name: Olofat
gods.description: The great trickster of the Carolines — sometimes cruel, sometimes a culture hero, and on Yap split into two: Yelafath the Elder, who creates the other gods, and Yelafath the Younger, who is the trickster. He escaped being burned alive and became the god of fire, sending it to earth in a starling's beak.
gods.imageQuery: Micronesian carved figure spirit Caroline Islands

gods.name: Anulap
gods.description: The great sky god of the Chuukic islands — distant but not disengaged, and largely represented in practice by Luuk (Nuuk), 'middle of heaven', who does most of the actual work. On Yap, however, Luk is the god of death, who flies about snaring humans in a net.

gods.name: Nareau
gods.description: The high god of Kiribati — who pries open a primordial clam to make the world, and who, like Olofat, appears in two manifestations, one creator and one trickster. Riki, at his command, raises the vault of the heavens.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: By far the most numerous and most important spirits, and the ones actually dealt with day to day. In Chuuk, offerings were placed in model canoes hung from the roof of the men's house. An ancestor may descend onto the shoulders of a living relative and speak through them. The relationship is one of approach and avoidance: the dead can help, and the dead can turn.

gods.name: Legerem
gods.description: The Yapese goddess who taught canoe building, navigation, and the stars — one of three women in Micronesian tradition credited with transmitting navigational knowledge to human beings.

micronesian.godsNote: Micronesia has no shared pantheon and no supreme creator common across the islands. The same name shifts function completely from island to island — Olofat is a trickster in the east and a creator on Yap; Luk is heaven's middle in Chuuk and the god of death on Yap. Cosmologies are lineage-level and highly localized.

norse.name:
Norse
Polytheism
norse.description: Old Norse religion is known mostly from Iceland, mostly from the thirteenth century, and mostly from Christians — Snorri Sturluson wrote the Prose Edda more than two hundred years after Iceland's conversion, and the Poetic Edda was compiled around the same time from older material. That gap matters, and it should be held in mind. What comes through it is a cosmos organized around Yggdrasil, the world tree, whose trunk stands at the centre and whose roots and branches hold the Nine Worlds together — Asgard of the Æsir, Vanaheim of the Vanir, Midgard of humans, Jötunheim of the giants, Hel below. The tree's name means 'Odin's horse', a reference to the god's self-sacrifice: he hung nine nights on the windy tree, wounded with a spear, given to himself, to win the runes. Odin is the Allfather, and he is not comforting — a god of war, poetry, magic, ecstasy, and the hanged, who traded an eye at Mímir's well for knowledge and who collects the dead for a battle he already knows he will lose. Thor is the god people actually liked: hammer, thunder, and the defence of the ordinary world against the giants, with more amulets in the archaeological record than any other. The Vanir — Njörd, Freyr, and Freyja — came to Asgard after a war and stayed; they govern fertility, wealth, seafaring, love, magic, and half the slain. Loki is a genuine problem: companion and saboteur, whose children are the wolf, the serpent, and death. And it all ends. Ragnarök is prophesied, not feared: the tree shakes, Fenrir devours Odin, the serpent poisons the sky, Surtr burns the world — and then a new one rises from the sea, and two humans, Líf and Lífthrasir, come out of hiding. Worship was blót: sacrifice, communal feasts, and animals killed before battles, voyages, bargains, and funerals.

gods.name: Odin
gods.domain: War, wisdom, poetry, magic, death, frenzy
gods.description: Ruler of Asgard and leader of the Æsir — and a deeply unsettling god. He gave an eye at Mímir's well for knowledge, and hung nine nights on Yggdrasil, spear-wounded, sacrificed to himself, to take up the runes. He is the god of the hanged, of the ecstatic fury the name denotes, of poetry, and of kings; he gathers the slain into Valhalla; and he does it all knowing exactly how it ends. His ravens are Thought and Memory.

gods.name: Thor
gods.domain: Thunder, strength, protection, the common people
gods.description: Odin's son, strongest of the Æsir, who defends gods and humans from the jötnar with Mjölnir, the hammer that returns when thrown. Not subtle and not required to be. The most popular god in practice — his hammer amulets vastly outnumber every other religious object in the Viking archaeological record, and Thursday is his.

gods.name: Freyja
gods.domain: Love, fertility, magic, war, the slain
gods.description: The great Vanir goddess — of love and fertility, and equally of war and death: she takes half the slain, and Odin gets the other half. She practises seiðr, the magic that even Odin had to learn from her, and she rides a chariot drawn by cats and weeps tears of gold.

gods.name: Freyr
gods.description: Freyja's brother, god of the harvest and of peace and plenty — with a ship that folds into a pocket and a golden boar. He gave away his sword for love, and at Ragnarök he will die because of it. Much loved in Sweden, where his cult at Uppsala was central.

gods.name: Frigg
gods.description: Odin's wife and the foremost of the goddesses — who can see the whole future and tells no one. She extracts an oath from everything in the world not to harm her son Baldr, and forgets the mistletoe. Friday is hers.

gods.name: Loki
gods.description: Neither Æsir nor giant and welcome nowhere for long — a companion of the gods who solves the problems he has usually created, and who eventually engineers Baldr's death and is bound beneath a serpent's venom until he breaks loose at Ragnarök. His children are Fenrir the wolf, Jörmungandr the world-serpent, and Hel.

gods.name: Baldr
gods.description: Odin and Frigg's beautiful son, invulnerable to everything but the mistletoe nobody thought to ask — killed by his blind brother with a mistletoe dart at Loki's suggestion, and burned on his ship Hringhorni. His death is the first move of Ragnarök, and it cannot be undone because one giantess refuses to weep.

gods.name: Njörd
gods.description: Father of Freyr and Freyja, a Vanir god of the sea, of favourable winds, and of the wealth that comes from both. He is the direct descendant of the Germanic earth goddess Nerthus, and the change of sex between them is one of the great unresolved puzzles of Germanic religion.

gods.name: Tyr
gods.description: God of law and the sworn oath — who put his right hand in the wolf Fenrir's mouth as a pledge of good faith, knowing the pledge was false, and lost the hand when the binding held. The gods needed the wolf bound; someone had to pay for it. Tuesday is his.

gods.name: Heimdall
