gods.description: Guardian of Bifröst, the rainbow bridge — who needs less sleep than a bird, can see a hundred leagues, and can hear the grass growing and the wool on a sheep's back. He will blow the Gjallarhorn when Ragnarök comes.

gods.name: The Norns
gods.domain: Fate, wyrd, the past and what must be
gods.description: The three who tend Yggdrasil at the well of Urd and carve what will happen — and they are not goddesses and are not negotiable. Norse religion runs on wyrd, fate: even Odin cannot get out of what is coming, and the whole ethical structure is about how you meet it.

norse.godsNote: Almost everything we have was written down in Christian Iceland two centuries or more after conversion, chiefly by Snorri Sturluson, who was systematizing material that may never have been systematic. Regional and period variation was probably enormous, and the tidy pantheon of the Eddas should be treated with some suspicion.

asatru.name: Asatru
asatru.description: Asatru means loyalty to the Aesir, and it is the modern reconstruction of pre-Christian Germanic and Norse religion. It is not a survival. No organized pagan tradition persisted through Christianization, and the honest practitioners say so. It is a revival, built from the Poetic and Prose Eddas, the sagas, Beowulf, archaeology, and folklore, and consciously reassembled into a living practice. It began, in its modern form, in 1972: a group of Icelandic artists led by the sheep farmer and poet Sveinbjorn Beinteinsson founded the Asatruarfelagid, and persuaded the Icelandic government to recognize it as a religion the following year. It has grown steadily, and now claims well over three thousand members in Iceland, more than one percent of the population, with cultural influence far larger than the numbers. Practice is organized into small local groups, called kindreds, hearths, or garths, usually led by a godi, a priest who speaks for the gods. Ritual centres on the blot, a sacrifice, now usually of mead, and the sumbel, a formal round of toasts. Ethics are framed around lists of virtues rather than commandments; there is no dogma, and personal relationships with individual deities are expected to differ. The movement has a serious problem, which it discusses openly: white-nationalist groups have latched onto Norse symbolism, and Heathenry is split between folkish currents that treat it as an ethnic inheritance and universalist ones that hold that anyone may worship the Aesir. Scholars of the movement generally observe that those racialist currents did not evolve out of Heathenry at all; they evolved out of racial politics and reached for a religion that suited them.

gods.name: Odin
gods.description: The Allfather, revered in Asatru much as in the Eddas: the seeker who gave an eye and hung nine nights on the tree for knowledge. In modern practice he draws those who value the relentless pursuit of understanding.

gods.name: Thor
gods.domain: Thunder, protection, strength, the common person
gods.description: The protector, and, as in the Viking Age, the most popular god in actual practice. His hammer is the movement's most worn symbol, which is also precisely why its appropriation by white-power groups has caused so much grief.

gods.name: Freyja
gods.domain: Love, fertility, magic, war, the slain
gods.description: The great Vanir goddess, and in modern Heathenry often the entry point for practitioners drawn to seidr, the magic she taught even Odin. She takes half the battle-slain to Folkvangr.

gods.name: Freyr
gods.description: God of peace and plenty, of harvests, good weather, and the land. Central to the agricultural year in a religion much of whose modern ritual calendar tracks the seasons.

gods.name: The land-wights
gods.description: The spirits of the land and the honoured dead of one's own line, an animist layer beneath the gods, and in practice as important as them. Iceland's coat of arms still bears the four landvaettir.

asatru.godsNote: Asatru is a reconstruction, not a survival, and reputable practitioners are explicit about it. The pantheon is drawn from the Eddas, which were themselves written down in Christian Iceland two centuries after conversion, so modern Heathenry is a reconstruction from sources that were already, in a sense, a reconstruction.

rodnovery.name: Rodnovery
rodnovery.description: Rodnovery, native faith, is the modern Slavic pagan revival, emerging in the early twentieth century and expanding enormously after the fall of the Soviet Union, across Russia, Ukraine, Poland, and the Slavic diaspora. It venerates the pre-Christian Slavic gods: Perun, the thunderer with his axe, whose idol Vladimir threw into the Dnieper in 988; Veles, lord of cattle, wealth, magic, and the underworld, Perun's adversary; Dazhbog the sun-giver; Mokosh, the moist earth mother and the only goddess named in the Kievan pantheon; Svarog the smith. And it faces an unusually severe version of the problem every reconstructionist movement faces, because the Slavic sources are worse than almost anyone else's. There is no Slavic Edda. What survives is a handful of hostile Christian chronicle entries, a few place-names, and folklore, and the reconstruction leans heavily on the last of these. Critics, including scholars sympathetic to the project, have been blunt: the calendars and pantheons are often assembled from fragments borrowed across East and West Slavic, Indian, and Scandinavian sources rather than any single local tradition, and the Book of Veles, a document that philologists universally regard as a twentieth-century forgery, is treated as scripture by some groups. Politically it is fraught. Several Rodnover organizations have been declared extremist by Russian courts, and there have been associated hate crimes. Others are apolitical, ecological, and reconstructionist in good faith. The distinction between the two matters enormously and is not always easy to make from outside.

sources.title: Criticism of Modern Paganism
sources.mla: "Criticism of Modern Paganism." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Criticism_of_modern_paganism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Perun
gods.description: The thunder god and head of the Kievan pantheon: oak, axe, and the sworn oath. Vladimir's Christianization in 988 began with dragging his idol behind horses and throwing it in the Dnieper, which tells you what he had been.

gods.name: Veles
gods.domain: Cattle, wealth, magic, poetry, the underworld
gods.description: Perun's adversary: god of herds, of wealth, of oaths sworn on goods rather than weapons, of magic, and of the world below. The recurring Slavic myth has Perun chasing him across the sky and striking him with lightning.

gods.name: Mokosh
gods.domain: Earth, moisture, women's work, spinning, fate
gods.description: Mother Moist Earth, the only goddess named in the Kievan pantheon, patroness of spinning and weaving and of women's work generally, and surviving longest of any Slavic deity in folk practice, where she blurred into St Paraskeva.

gods.name: Dazhbog
gods.description: The giving god and the sun, whose name means roughly 'god who gives'. The Lay of Igor's Campaign calls the Rus his grandchildren, which is the closest thing we have to a Slavic origin claim.

gods.name: Svarog
gods.description: The smith and heavenly fire, father in some reconstructions of Dazhbog and of Svarozhich, the fire of the hearth. The reconstructions vary considerably, which is the whole problem.

rodnovery.godsNote: The Slavic sources are exceptionally thin, a few hostile chronicle entries and folklore, with no equivalent of the Eddas, so almost everything in modern Rodnovery is reconstructed, and scholars including sympathetic ones have criticized the results as pantheons assembled from borrowed fragments. The Book of Veles, treated as scripture by some groups, is a recognized forgery. Some Rodnover organizations have been declared extremist by Russian courts; many others are apolitical.

romuva.name: Romuva
romuva.description: Lithuania was the last pagan nation in Europe. It converted formally only in 1387, and Baltic religious practice persisted in the countryside for centuries afterward, which gives Romuva a claim to continuity that most reconstructionist movements simply do not have. It has been praised, even by scholars generally sceptical of neopaganism, for maintaining a real historical thread: the Lithuanian folk song tradition, the dainos, preserved an extraordinary body of ritual and mythological material through the Christian centuries and into the twentieth, and Romuva builds on that rather than on invention. The revival began in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, in the same nationalist ferment that produced the Lithuanian language movement. The Soviet occupation of 1940 crushed it, and practitioners were deported or killed, precisely because the movement was nationalist. It re-registered as a religion in 1992, two years after independence. Under Lithuanian law a religion may be considered for full state recognition after twenty-five years of registration; Romuva applied and, in 2019, was refused, after a parliamentary debate in which its members were accused of foreign affiliations. It appealed to the European Court of Human Rights, which found in Romuva's favour. The theology centres on darna, harmony, the right ordering of relations between people, nature, and the gods, and on Dievas, the sky god; Perkunas, the thunderer; Zemyna, the earth; and the sacred fire, tended and never allowed to go out, which is the oldest and most persistent element of Baltic religion.

gods.name: Dievas
gods.description: The sky god, cognate with the Indo-European Dyeus and therefore with Zeus, Jupiter, and the Sanskrit Dyaus. The word simply became the Lithuanian word for God, which it still is, and the continuity is right there in the language.

gods.name: Perkunas
gods.description: The thunderer, the most vividly attested of the Baltic gods, associated with the oak, with rain, and with the enforcement of justice. His name shares a root with the Slavic Perun.

gods.name: Zemyna
gods.description: Mother Earth, who receives the dead and gives the harvest, and to whom the first drink from any cup is poured on the ground. That gesture survived Christianization in Lithuanian folk custom, and it is a fair emblem of the whole tradition.

gods.name: Gabija
gods.description: The goddess of fire, of the hearth fire that must be tended, fed, and covered rather than extinguished at night. The eternal fire is the oldest and most tenacious element of Baltic religion, and Romuva rituals still centre on it.

gods.name: Laima
gods.description: The goddess of fate, who determines the lot of a person at birth. She is one of the most prominent figures in the Latvian and Lithuanian folk songs, which is where most of what we know about any of these gods actually comes from.

romuva.godsNote: Romuva has a stronger claim to historical continuity than most reconstructionist movements, because Lithuania Christianized so late and because the folk song tradition preserved an unusually rich body of ritual and mythological material, but it remains a modern revival, not an unbroken lineage.

druidism.name: Druidism
druidism.description: Modern Druidry has almost nothing to do with the ancient Druids, and its more honest practitioners say so cheerfully. The ancient Celtic priesthood left no writings of its own, was described only by hostile Romans, and its institutions were destroyed. Roman forces broke the ritual centre at Anglesey in 60 or 61 CE, and no artefact or record suggests Druidic institutions survived. What is actually being revived is an eighteenth-century invention. The Ancient Order of Druids was founded in London in 1781, probably by a carpenter named Henry Hurle, in a pub in Poland Street, and it was modelled on Freemasonry: a fraternal and mutual-aid society, not a religion. Then came Edward Williams, a Welsh poet, stonemason, Unitarian, and laudanum enthusiast who took the name Iolo Morganwg and produced a body of manuscripts claiming to preserve an unbroken Welsh bardic-Druidic tradition. They were forgeries, brilliant ones, interpolated into genuine medieval texts, and not exposed until the late nineteenth century. In 1792 he staged his invented Gorsedd of Bards ceremony on Primrose Hill; it was adopted by the Welsh eisteddfodau, and it is still performed at the National Eisteddfod today. Whatever else it was, it restored a pride in their own heritage to the Welsh, Cornish, and Bretons at a moment when their languages were being crushed. Religious Druidry proper arrives in the twentieth century: Ross Nichols founded the Order of Bards, Ovates and Druids in 1964 and deliberately shifted the emphasis toward real Celtic scholarship, and Morganwg's contribution shrank to a prayer and a few ritual fragments. Modern Druids are mostly polytheist, animist, or pantheist, oriented to nature, the seasonal wheel, and the land.

gods.name: The Celtic gods
gods.description: Modern Druids who are polytheist generally honour the Irish and Welsh deities attested in medieval literature: Brigid of poetry, smithcraft, and healing; Lugh of many skills; the Dagda; Cernunnos the horned one. These are drawn from the Celtic material, not from anything the historical Druids are known to have taught.

gods.name: Awen
gods.description: The flowing inspiration, the central concept of Revival Druidry, and Iolo Morganwg's most enduring contribution, along with the three-ray symbol that represents it. Not a god but a force, invoked and chanted.

gods.name: The land, sea, and sky
gods.description: The actual object of most modern Druid devotion: an animist and pantheist orientation to the natural world, organized around the eight festivals of the wheel of the year and the specific places practitioners live in.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: Honoured alongside the spirits of place, one of the three kindreds, gods, ancestors, and nature spirits, that many modern Druid orders address in ritual.

druidism.godsNote: Modern Druidry is not a continuation of the ancient Celtic priesthood. It is an eighteenth-century revival built substantially on Iolo Morganwg's forgeries, and later orders have consciously worked to replace that material with genuine Celtic scholarship. Practitioners' theologies vary enormously: polytheist, animist, pantheist, and some who are Christian or simply non-theistic.

wicca.name: Wicca
wicca.description: Wicca is the modern pagan witchcraft religion introduced to the public by Gerald Gardner, a retired British civil servant, folklorist, and Freemason, in the years after Britain repealed its last Witchcraft Act in 1951, through books including Witchcraft Today (1954). Gardner claimed to have been initiated into a surviving coven of an ancient pre-Christian witch-cult in the New Forest, and presented Wicca as the continuation of that 'Old Religion'. Modern historians reject the survival claim: Wicca is a twentieth-century construction, assembled from Margaret Murray's now-discredited witch-cult hypothesis, ceremonial magic (especially the Golden Dawn and Aleister Crowley, from whom Gardner borrowed directly), Freemasonry, folk custom, and Romantic literature, much of its liturgy written by Gardner and his priestess Doreen Valiente, who composed the beloved Charge of the Goddess. What Gardner built, however, became a genuine and fast-growing religion. It is a nature-based, initiatory, duotheistic faith centred on the worship of a Mother Goddess and a Horned God, understood as the complementary female and male principles of nature, associated respectively with the moon and the sun. The ritual year turns on the Wheel of the Year, eight seasonal Sabbats, the solstices, equinoxes, and the cross-quarter days like Samhain and Beltane, together with the Esbats, the monthly full-moon rites. Practice centres on the coven, traditionally led by a High Priestess, with three degrees of initiation, and on the working of magic within a ritually cast circle using tools like the athame (ritual knife), chalice, and pentacle. Its ethical core is the Wiccan Rede, 'an it harm none, do what ye will', reinforced by the belief that one's actions return threefold.

sources.title: Gerald Gardner: Founder of Modern Wicca and Witchcraft Revival
sources.mla: "Gerald Gardner: Founder of Modern Wicca and Witchcraft Revival." The Pagan Club, https://www.paganclub.com/gerald-gardner/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Goddess
gods.domain: The moon, fertility, the earth, life and death
gods.description: The great female principle of nature, often conceived as a Triple Goddess of Maiden, Mother, and Crone corresponding to the phases of the moon, honoured in the Esbats and given voice in the Charge of the Goddess. In much of Wicca she is the more central of the two deities, the source and matrix of life.

gods.name: The Horned God
gods.domain: The sun, the hunt, wild nature, the cycle of the year
gods.description: The great male principle, lord of the wild, the forest, the hunt, and the animals, associated with the sun and the yearly cycle of birth, death, and rebirth. His horns connect him to the horned deities of ancient Europe. His demonization by Christianity as the Devil is, Wiccans hold, a slander on an old nature-god.

gods.name: The elements and the quarters
gods.domain: The ritual circle, the directions, natural forces
gods.description: Not deities but the powers invoked at the four quarters of the cast circle, earth, air, fire, and water, each with its direction, tool, and associations, together forming the sacred space in which Wiccan ritual and magic are worked, with spirit as the uniting fifth.

wicca.godsNote: Wicca is a modern religion (mid-twentieth century), not, as its founder claimed, a surviving ancient tradition; scholars trace its elements to ceremonial magic, Freemasonry, folklore, and Romantic and Murrayite sources. It is broadly duotheistic (Goddess and God), though individual Wiccans range from viewing them as literal deities to as archetypes or as faces of a single divinity or of all gods and goddesses. Many traditions and solitary forms exist beyond Gardner's original.
imageQueries: Wheel of the Year sabbats pagan

thelema.name: Thelema
thelema.description: Thelema is the religious and philosophical movement founded by the English occultist Aleister Crowley in the early twentieth century, taking its name from the Greek word for 'will'. Its founding event was, by Crowley's account, the dictation to him in Cairo in 1904 of The Book of the Law (Liber AL vel Legis) by a discarnate being named Aiwass, over three days, one chapter each, spoken by three deities. From it Crowley proclaimed the dawn of a new age, the Aeon of Horus, governed by a new ethical law expressed in its two central maxims: 'Do what thou wilt shall be the whole of the Law', and 'Love is the law, love under will'. The first is easily misread as licence; Crowley meant something more demanding. Every person has a True Will, a unique essential purpose or calling, distinct from ordinary wants and desires, and to 'do what thou wilt' is to discover and accomplish that True Will, aligning oneself with one's proper orbit in the cosmic order. The complementary axiom, 'every man and every woman is a star', affirms the sovereign uniqueness of each person. The cosmology of The Book of the Law is built on three deities adapted from ancient Egyptian religion, whom Crowley described as a 'literary convenience': Nuit, the infinite goddess of the night sky and boundless space, the source of all possibility; Hadit, the infinitely small point at the centre of every star, manifestation and motion; and Ra-Hoor-Khuit, a form of Horus, the crowned and conquering child of the new Aeon, representing the sun and active force. Thelema is pursued through ceremonial magic (Crowley's 'Magick'), the discovery of one's Holy Guardian Angel, and organizations such as the A∴A∴ and the Ordo Templi Orientis.

sources.title: The Book of the Law
sources.mla: "The Book of the Law." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Book_of_the_Law. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Thelema, Do What Thou Wilt: History, Founder & Beliefs
sources.mla: "Thelema, Do What Thou Wilt: History, Founder & Beliefs." Study.com, https://study.com/academy/lesson/thelema-founder-beliefs.html. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Nuit
gods.domain: Infinite space, the night sky, boundless possibility
gods.description: The goddess of the infinite night sky, depicted as a naked woman arched over the world and covered in stars, the boundless source of all possibility, from whom all things come and to whom they return. She speaks the first chapter of The Book of the Law. Her call is to experience: 'love is the law, love under will.'

gods.name: Hadit
gods.domain: The infinite point, manifestation, motion, the core of the self
gods.description: The infinitely condensed point at the centre of every star and every being, the principle of manifestation, motion, and the individual self, complementary to Nuit's infinite expanse. He speaks the second chapter. The union of Nuit and Hadit is the perpetual creative marriage from which all existence issues.

gods.name: Ra-Hoor-Khuit
gods.domain: The sun, active force, the Aeon of Horus
gods.description: A form of the Egyptian Horus, the crowned and conquering child who presides over the new Aeon that Crowley proclaimed, representing the sun and active, martial energy. He speaks the third and most forceful chapter of The Book of the Law, and his 'twin' is the silent Hoor-paar-kraat (Harpocrates).

gods.name: The Holy Guardian Angel
gods.domain: Higher self, spiritual attainment, the True Will
gods.description: Not a god of the pantheon but the central goal of Thelemic magical practice: the attainment of the Knowledge and Conversation of one's Holy Guardian Angel, a higher or truer self (Aiwass was Crowley's own), through which one comes to know and accomplish one's True Will.

thelema.godsNote: Thelema centres on the discovery and accomplishment of the True Will. Crowley described its three Egyptian-derived deities (Nuit, Hadit, Ra-Hoor-Khuit) as a 'literary convenience', and Thelemites vary in whether they treat them as literal gods, as symbolic principles, or as psychological forces; some also practise other religions alongside. 'Do what thou wilt' denotes alignment with one's true purpose, not hedonistic licence. The Book of the Law contains harsh passages whose interpretation is debated within the movement.

theosophy.name: Theosophy
theosophy.description: Theosophy was founded in New York in 1875 by Helena Petrovna Blavatsky, a widely travelled Russian mystic, together with Henry Steel Olcott and William Quan Judge. Blavatsky described it as the synthesis of science, religion, and philosophy, and claimed it revived an ancient wisdom-religion, a single secret doctrine, that had once underlain every faith on earth and was known to Plato and the sages of India alike. Her two enormous books, Isis Unveiled and The Secret Doctrine, drew together Neoplatonism, Kabbalah, Hermeticism, Hinduism, and Buddhism into a sweeping esoteric cosmology built on karma, reincarnation, and the evolution of the soul through a series of planes and cycles. She said the teachings were not her own invention but had been transmitted to her by the Masters or Mahatmas, a hidden brotherhood of spiritual adepts based chiefly in Tibet, who possessed supernatural powers and had initiated the modern movement through her. This claim is the heart of the matter and the heart of the controversy: sympathetic and hostile scholars alike have concluded that the Masters, the root races, and the lost continents of Lemuria and Atlantis are fiction. Blavatsky was formally accused of faking paranormal phenomena. And yet the movement mattered enormously: it did more than almost anything else to introduce Hindu and Buddhist ideas to the West, popularized concepts like karma, reincarnation, and the astral plane, shaped the entire New Age milieu, and influenced figures from Gandhi to Kandinsky. Its racial cosmology, the doctrine of successive root races, also fed into darker currents, and has been criticized as fostering later racial ideologies.

gods.name: The Divine Principle
gods.domain: The impersonal ground of all being
gods.description: Blavatsky's Theosophy is pantheistic and its 'God' is not personal: a universal divine principle, the root of all, from which everything proceeds and into which all is reabsorbed at the end of the great cycle, with a spark of the divine in every particle of matter. She was scathing about the personal God of Western Christianity.

gods.name: The Masters
gods.description: The brotherhood of spiritual adepts, based chiefly beyond the Himalayas, who Blavatsky said had preserved the ancient wisdom across the ages, possessed paranormal powers, and transmitted the Theosophical teachings through her. For most Theosophists they are the real founders of the movement. Scholars regard them as fictional.
gods.imageQuery: Theosophy Masters Morya Koot Hoomi portrait

gods.name: The root races
gods.domain: Cosmic evolution, the history of humanity
gods.description: Not deities but the framework of Theosophical anthropology: seven successive stages of human evolution across millions of years and lost continents, from ethereal beings on Hyperborea through the Lemurians and Atlanteans to the present. Fiction, and criticized for feeding later racial ideologies.

theosophy.godsNote: Theosophy is pantheistic rather than theistic: it posits an impersonal divine principle, not a personal creator. Many adherents deny it is a religion at all, calling it a philosophy or a body of ancient wisdom. Its distinctive claims, the Masters, the root races, and the lost continents, are regarded by scholars as fiction, and some have been criticized as racist.

rosicrucian.name: Rosicrucian
rosicrucian.description: Rosicrucianism began, as far as history can tell, with three anonymous pamphlets published in Germany between 1614 and 1616. The Fama Fraternitatis announced the existence of a secret brotherhood of the Rose Cross, founded by one Christian Rosenkreuz, a German who had travelled to the East, gathered esoteric wisdom in Damascus, Fez, and elsewhere, returned to found an order of eight physician-sages sworn to heal the sick without charge, and died at the age of 106. His tomb, the Fama claimed, had just been discovered after 120 years, perfectly preserved and full of secret knowledge, and the time had come for the brotherhood to reveal itself and reform all human learning. The Confessio Fraternitatis elaborated the philosophy; the Chymical Wedding of Christian Rosenkreuz, an allegorical novel of alchemical initiation, completed the trio. The manifestos caused a sensation across a Europe torn by religious war, generating hundreds of published responses from people desperate to make contact. But there was no brotherhood to contact. Christian Rosenkreuz almost certainly never existed, and most scholars attribute the manifestos to Johann Valentin Andreae and a circle of Lutheran utopians in Tubingen; Andreae later called the Chymical Wedding a youthful jest. What the manifestos launched, though, was real: a durable Western esoteric current fusing Hermeticism, alchemy, and Kabbalah with an esoteric, anti-papal Christianity and a dream of universal reformation. It fed into Freemasonry and the Golden Dawn, and modern Rosicrucian organizations, the largest being AMORC, founded in 1915, carry the name still.

sources.title: Rosicrucians
sources.mla: "Rosicrucians." Catholic Encyclopedia, New Advent, https://www.newadvent.org/cathen/13193b.htm. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Christian Rosenkreuz
gods.description: The founder of the Rose Cross brotherhood, said to have been born in 1378, travelled east for hidden knowledge, and lived 106 years. Almost certainly an allegorical figure rather than a historical person, and read by scholars as a symbol encoding the spiritual path rather than a man.

gods.name: God
gods.domain: Creation, the divine source, esoteric Christianity
gods.description: Rosicrucianism is a form of esoteric Christianity, and its God is the Christian one, approached through Hermetic and alchemical philosophy rather than church doctrine. Its anti-papal cast is explicit in the manifestos, which imagine a coming reformation freeing knowledge from tyranny.

gods.name: The rose and the cross
gods.domain: The union of matter and spirit, transformation
gods.description: Not a deity but the central emblem: the rose blooming on the cross, read as the soul unfolding through suffering, or spirit crucified in matter and redeemed. The whole tradition is organized around alchemy as spiritual transformation, the perfecting of the human being rather than the making of literal gold.

rosicrucian.godsNote: Rosicrucianism is an esoteric-Christian and Hermetic current, not a polytheistic religion. Its founding narrative is regarded by scholars as fiction, and its 'brotherhood' as, at least originally, a literary device. It venerates the one God of Christianity through alchemical and Hermetic symbolism.

tsao.name:
Golden
Dawn
tsao.description: The Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn was the most influential magical society of the modern West, and it lasted barely fifteen years. It was founded in London in 1887-1888 by three Freemasons: William Wynn Westcott, a coroner; Samuel Liddell MacGregor Mathers, a folklorist; and William Robert Woodman. The founding story is a fabrication that everyone now recognizes as one: Westcott claimed to have decoded a set of cipher manuscripts outlining the rituals of an ancient order, and to have obtained from them the address of a German Rosicrucian adept, Anna Sprengel, who chartered the new temple. The manuscripts were real but not ancient, Sprengel almost certainly never existed, and Westcott probably wrote the authorizing letters himself. None of which mattered, because what Mathers built on that dubious foundation was genuinely new and genuinely coherent: the first comprehensive Western magical curriculum, synthesizing Hermetic Kabbalah, tarot, astrology, geomancy, alchemy, and the Enochian system of John Dee into a single graded structure mapped onto the Kabbalistic Tree of Life. Members advanced through ten grades corresponding to the ten Sefirot, divided into an Outer Order of study and an Inner Order of practical magic, and, remarkably for the period, women were admitted on equal terms. Its members included W. B. Yeats, Arthur Machen, Algernon Blackwood, Maud Gonne, and, fatefully, Aleister Crowley, whose conflict with Mathers helped tear the order apart around 1900. It fragmented, but its synthesis survived: the Rider-Waite tarot, modern Wicca, Thelema, and virtually every Western ceremonial magic system since descend directly from it, largely through Israel Regardie's publication of its rituals in the 1930s.

sources.title: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
sources.mla: "Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Hermetic-Order-of-the-Golden-Dawn. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
sources.mla: "Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermetic_Order_of_the_Golden_Dawn. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn
sources.mla: "Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn." Occult World, https://occult-world.com/hermetic-order-of-the-golden-dawn/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Hermetic Kabbalah
gods.domain: The structure of reality, the map of magic
gods.description: Not a deity but the framework onto which everything else was mapped. The Golden Dawn took the Jewish Sefirot and rebuilt them as a system of universal correspondence, so that every tarot card, planet, element, angel, and Egyptian god had its assigned place, and every grade was an ascent up the Tree.
gods.imageQuery: Hermetic Qabalah Tree of Life diagram

gods.name: The Secret Chiefs
gods.description: The superhuman adepts from whom Mathers claimed to receive the order's authority and its higher teachings, communicating across astral distance. The claim is a direct ancestor of Theosophy's Masters, and when Mathers could no longer produce evidence of them, the order's authority collapsed.
gods.imageQuery: Golden Dawn ritual temple ceremonial magic

gods.name: The Egyptian and angelic god-forms
gods.description: The gods, angels, and entities invoked in ritual, drawn from Egyptian mythology, Kabbalah, and the Enochian system of Dee and Kelley. In Golden Dawn practice these were not worshipped but assumed, the magician taking on the god-form to accomplish the work.

tsao.godsNote: The Golden Dawn was a magical order, not a devotional religion, and it had no pantheon of its own in the ordinary sense. Its 'gods' are the deities and entities of many traditions, systematized into a single framework of correspondence and used as instruments of ritual rather than objects of worship. Its founding documents were, by scholarly consensus, forged.

anthroposophy.name: Anthroposophy
anthroposophy.description: Anthroposophy was founded in 1912-1913 by the Austrian philosopher Rudolf Steiner, who had been the general secretary of the German Theosophical Society and broke with it over its promotion of the young Jiddu Krishnamurti as a world teacher and its de-emphasis of Christianity. Steiner called his system spiritual science: he claimed that disciplined thinking could be applied to the spiritual world as rigorously as natural science is applied to the physical one, and that through inner training a person could develop higher faculties, imagination, inspiration, and intuition, to perceive spiritual realities directly. His cosmology is elaborate. The human being has four bodies, the physical, the etheric (life forces), the astral (emotions), and the I or ego, unfolding in seven-year stages; the soul reincarnates across many lives shaped by karma; and the earth itself passes through successive incarnations. At the centre stands Christ: Steiner gave the incarnation, death, and resurrection, which he called the Mystery of Golgotha, a cosmic role, treating it as the turning point of the world's evolution and a Sun Being's descent into the earth. Steiner claimed direct clairvoyant access to all of this and recorded it in dozens of books and over six thousand lectures. What makes anthroposophy unusually visible is its practical offshoots, most of which have long outgrown their esoteric roots: Waldorf education, now a worldwide network of schools built on Steiner's account of child development; biodynamic agriculture, which anticipated organic farming by decades while adding cosmic rhythms and homeopathic preparations; anthroposophic medicine; and eurythmy, a movement art. The biodynamic planting calendars and some medical claims are regarded by mainstream scientists as pseudoscientific.

sources.title: Anthroposophy: Rudolf Steiner's Spiritual Science Explained
sources.mla: "Anthroposophy: Rudolf Steiner's Spiritual Science Explained." Philosophy Professor, https://www.philosophyprofessor.com/philosophies/anthroposophy/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Rudolf Steiner Anthroposophy: Life, Philosophy, and Global Influence
sources.mla: "Rudolf Steiner Anthroposophy: Life, Philosophy, and Global Influence." Studium Goetheanum, https://studium-goetheanum.org/rudolf-steiner-anthroposophy/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Christ
gods.domain: Cosmic evolution, the Mystery of Golgotha
gods.description: Central to anthroposophy, but reconceived: a great Sun Being whose incarnation, death, and resurrection, the Mystery of Golgotha, Steiner treated as the pivotal event of cosmic evolution, infusing the earth's spiritual sphere with new life and conquering death. This reading of Christ is rejected by mainstream Christian churches.

gods.name: The spiritual hierarchies
gods.description: The ranks of spiritual beings, drawn partly from Christian angelology, that Steiner said populate the higher worlds and guide human and cosmic evolution, perceptible to trained clairvoyant faculties.

gods.name: The fourfold human being
gods.domain: The constitution of the person, reincarnation
gods.description: Not deities but the core of Steiner's anthropology: the human being as physical body, etheric life-body, astral soul-body, and the immortal I, unfolding in seven-year stages and reincarnating across many lives under the law of karma. Waldorf education is built directly on this developmental scheme.

anthroposophy.godsNote: Anthroposophy is an esoteric-Christian spiritual movement centred on a highly unorthodox cosmic reading of Christ, not a polytheistic religion. Its practical offshoots, Waldorf schools, biodynamic farming, anthroposophic medicine, are widespread; some of its scientific claims are regarded by the mainstream as pseudoscientific.

catharism.name: Catharism
catharism.description: The Cathars, from the Greek katharos, pure, flourished in Languedoc and northern Italy in the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, and taught a dualism they had probably received, through Latin translations of Greek texts, from the Bogomils of Bulgaria. There are two principles, one good and one evil, and the material world is the work of the evil one. The spirit is good and longs for God; the flesh is a prison. Jesus, on this reading, was an angel whose human suffering and death were an illusion, because a good God would not take on matter. Their church had bishops, a liturgy, and a doctrine, and their society split in two. The perfecti, men and women alike, and this is one of the very few medieval movements in which women could hold the office, underwent the consolamentum, a laying on of hands that replaced Catholic baptism, and thereafter abstained from meat, from sex, and from possessions, travelling in pairs, ministering, living austerely. Ordinary believers were held to no such standard and typically received the consolamentum on their deathbed. And this, more than the theology, is why Catharism spread: historians increasingly argue that the example set by the perfecti's way of life mattered far more to their sympathisers than dualist metaphysics did. The contrast with a wealthy, worldly, absentee clergy was not subtle. Innocent III called a crusade in 1209. Beziers was taken in the first season and nearly the entire population massacred. The Albigensian Crusade ran twenty years, failed to eliminate Catharism, and succeeded in destroying the culture of southern France and delivering it to the French crown. The Dominican inquisition finished the job within a century. The last documented consolamentum was performed in 1321.

gods.name: The Good God
aka: the God of the New Testament
gods.domain: Spirit, light, the world to come
gods.description: The benevolent deity of the spiritual realm, who did not make matter and has nothing to do with it. Human souls are his, trapped in bodies that are not.

gods.name: The Evil God
gods.description: The creator of the material world, and in the more radical Cathar systems identified with the God of the Old Testament, which tells you how the Church took it. Everything you can touch is his.

gods.name: Christ
gods.description: An angel, in Cathar teaching, whose body was an appearance and whose suffering and death were therefore an illusion, since a spiritual being could not truly take on evil matter. This is docetism, and it is a very old idea that the Church had condemned a thousand years earlier.

catharism.godsNote: Almost everything we know about Cathar belief comes from their enemies, in inquisition records and polemics, and historians are increasingly cautious about how coherent or unified the 'Cathar church' actually was. Some scholars now argue the dualist system was substantially a construction of the inquisitors.

manichaeism.name: Manichaeism
manichaeism.description: Mani, who lived from 216 to 274 CE and was born in Mesopotamia, called himself the Apostle of Light and the final prophet in a line running through Adam, Zoroaster, the Buddha, and Jesus, and set out deliberately to found a universal religion that would synthesize all of them. His system is a rigorous dualism, and it differs from Gnosticism on one crucial point: the Darkness is coeternal with the Light, not a fall from it. There is no omnipotent good power. In the beginning the two realms existed apart, the Light in peace, the Darkness in ceaseless internal conflict. The Darkness attacked, and the material universe is the temporary result of that attack: a mixture. Particles of Light are trapped throughout matter, and human souls are fragments of it, imprisoned in bodies. Salvation is the liberation of the trapped Light through gnosis and ascetic discipline, and history is the long process of separating what was mixed. The community was structured in two tiers, apparently modelled on the Buddhist sangha: the Elect, celibate, vegetarian, propertyless, bound by the Three Seals of mouth, hands, and heart, whose pure lives released the Light; and the Hearers, who could marry and eat meat and who earned merit by supporting the Elect with alms. It spread with astonishing speed, from North Africa to China, along the Silk Road, adapting to local cultures as it went. Augustine was a Hearer for nearly a decade in his twenties, drawn by a system that took the problem of evil seriously in a way that the Christianity of his youth seemed to him to dodge, and his eventual repudiation of it shaped Western theology permanently. It was persecuted by Christians, Zoroastrians, and Muslims in turn, and is extinct. Its shape survived in the Bogomils and Cathars.

gods.name: The Father of Greatness
gods.description: The ruler of the realm of Light: good, but crucially not omnipotent, since the Darkness is coeternal with him and he did not make it. This is the whole point of the system. It explains evil by refusing to make God responsible for it.

gods.name: The Prince of Darkness
gods.description: The ruler of the realm of Darkness, collectively made up of five evil archons in constant conflict with one another, dwelling in smoke, fire, wind, water, and darkness, and consumed by unrestrained passion. Not a fallen creature. He was always there.

gods.name: The Living Spirit
gods.domain: Creation, the ordering of the cosmos
gods.description: An emanation of the realm of Light who constructs the material universe out of the mixture of Light and Darkness, building the cosmos not as a good in itself but as a machine for separating the two again.

gods.name: Jesus the Splendour
gods.description: A divine figure of Light who reveals the saving knowledge. Not incarnate, not crucified in any real sense, and quite unlike the Christian Jesus. Mani claimed to be his successor and the Paraclete he had promised.

gods.name: Mani
gods.description: The founder, who saw himself as the last in a prophetic line including Adam, Zoroaster, the Buddha, and Jesus, and who wrote his own scriptures precisely so that his teaching would not be corrupted after his death, as he believed theirs had been. He was executed under the Sasanian king Bahram I.

catholicism.name: Catholicism
catholicism.description: The Roman Catholic Church is the largest body of Christians on earth, and it differs from the other branches chiefly in four places: the sacraments, the relation of Scripture to Tradition, the place of Mary and the saints, and the papacy. It has always understood itself as the one worshipping community traceable back to the group Jesus established, and its bishops as successors to the apostles. The pope, the bishop of Rome, is its head; the title was used for any bishop as early as the third century, and the claim to universal jurisdiction hardened over the Middle Ages. Vatican I declared the Roman pontiff vested with the entire teaching authority of the Church; Vatican II softened that by stressing the collegial character of the papal primacy, abandoned the strict 'one true church' position, and formally ended the thousand-year schism with the Orthodox. Seven sacraments are celebrated: baptism, confirmation, the Eucharist, reconciliation, marriage, anointing of the sick, and holy orders. Augustine called a sacrament the visible form of an invisible grace, and Catholic teaching holds that they perpetuate the union of God and humanity rather than merely symbolizing it. The Eucharist is the centre. Catholic teaching on it is transubstantiation: the substance of the bread and wine becomes the body and blood of Christ, while the physical appearances remain unchanged. This is what the Reformation broke over, and it remains the fundamental Catholic understanding. Faith itself, in Catholic analysis, is not compelled by evidence: reason can show that belief in God is reasonable, but it cannot force the conclusion, and the will, moved by grace, does the rest.

sources.title: The Seven Sacraments of the Roman Catholic Church
sources.mla: "The Seven Sacraments of the Roman Catholic Church." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/list/the-seven-sacraments-of-the-roman-catholic-church. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Sacraments of the Catholic Church
sources.mla: "Sacraments of the Catholic Church." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sacraments_of_the_Catholic_Church. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: God the Father
gods.description: The first person of the Trinity, creator of all things visible and invisible. Catholic theology distinguishes revelation in the broad sense, what can be deduced from nature and human existence, from revelation in the strict sense, the utterances of God.
gods.imageQuery: God the Father Sistine Chapel Michelangelo

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: The second person, incarnate, crucified, risen. Present in the Eucharist not symbolically but substantially. The Mass is a memorial that repeats the sacrifice Jesus told his disciples to repeat, and the consecrated bread and wine are his body and blood in their inner reality.

gods.name: The Holy Spirit
gods.domain: Grace, the sacraments, the life of the Church
gods.description: The third person, and in Catholic teaching proceeding from the Father and the Son. That clause, the Filioque, is precisely what the Eastern churches reject.

gods.name: The Virgin Mary
gods.description: Venerated, not worshipped, but the veneration is enormous and it is one of the sharpest markers dividing Catholicism from Protestantism. Catholic dogma holds her immaculately conceived and bodily assumed into heaven.

gods.name: The saints
gods.description: The holy dead, who may be asked to intercede. Canonization cannot normally begin until five years after death, though a pope can waive the wait. Every parish, profession, and calamity has one.

catholicism.godsNote: Catholicism is monotheistic. Mary and the saints are venerated and asked to intercede, never worshipped, a distinction Catholics consider fundamental and many Protestants consider unpersuasive.

eastOrthodox.name: East Orthodox
eastOrthodox.description: The Eastern Orthodox Church separated from Rome, conventionally in 1054, though scholars propose 1009, 1204, 1277, and 1484, and 1204, when Crusaders sacked Constantinople, may be the real point of no return. The immediate causes were a tangle: leavened versus unleavened bread, the coronation of Charlemagne, iconoclasm, the closure of the Latin churches in Constantinople. But two things were structural. The first was papal primacy: Constantinople accepted a primacy of honour for Rome and flatly rejected direct Roman jurisdiction over the Eastern patriarchates. The second was the Filioque. The original creed said the Holy Spirit proceeds from the Father; the Latin West added 'and the Son'. Eastern critics objected to the theology, and even more to the process. A creed received through ecumenical councils, they argued, cannot be amended unilaterally by one regional church. That was not a quibble about manners; it was a claim of papal supremacy written into the creed itself. Where the West went in for legal and juridical categories, the East went in for mysticism, and above all for theosis: the doctrine that the point of salvation is not merely forgiveness but participation in the divine life, becoming by grace what God is by nature. Worship is the Divine Liturgy, long, sung, unaccompanied, in a church filled with icons, which are not decoration but windows, and whose defence during the iconoclast controversies cost the Byzantine world two centuries of violence. In 1965 Paul VI and Patriarch Athenagoras nullified the mutual excommunications of 1054. Nothing else was settled, and the churches remain apart.

gods.name: God the Father
gods.domain: The source of the Godhead, creation
gods.description: The sole origin of both the Son and the Spirit, a point Orthodox theology guards jealously, since the Filioque appears to it to give the Godhead two sources.

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: God who became human so that humans might become god, the formula of Athanasius that sits at the centre of Orthodox soteriology. The Pantocrator gazing down from the dome of every Orthodox church is the visual form of it.

gods.name: The Holy Spirit
gods.description: Proceeding from the Father, alone. The Orthodox Church has never accepted the Filioque, and its absence from the Eastern creed is the most durable doctrinal fact of the schism.

gods.name: The Theotokos
gods.description: God-bearer, the title fought over and won at Ephesus in 431, and central to Orthodox devotion. Her icons are everywhere, and the title is really a claim about her son rather than about her.

gods.name: The saints and icons
gods.description: Icons are venerated, not worshipped, and the theology defending them is a theology of the Incarnation: God took matter, therefore matter can carry God. They are treated as windows onto the persons depicted.

eastOrthodox.godsNote: Orthodoxy is monotheistic and Trinitarian, and it rejects the Filioque. The veneration of icons and saints is sharply distinguished, in Orthodox theology, from the worship owed to God alone.

protestant.name: Protestantism
protestant.description: On 31 October 1517, an Augustinian friar and professor of biblical theology named Martin Luther posted ninety-five theses against the sale of indulgences on the door of the castle church at Wittenberg. He was not trying to start a revolution; he was trying to start an argument. What followed split Western Christendom. The complaint about indulgences opened onto something much larger, and it reduces to two claims. The first: justification by faith alone. The sinner is put right with God not by good works, not by sacramental machinery, not by moral effort or purchased merit, but by faith, and even the faith is a gift. Luther described it as a living, daring confidence in God's grace, so certain that a man would stake his life on it a thousand times over. That claim demolished the medieval system of merits, indulgences, and purgatory in a single stroke. The second: the supreme authority of Scripture. The Bible is the only infallible source of doctrine; councils, popes, and traditions are subordinate to it and must be tested by it. Luther never meant that tradition was worthless, since he read the Fathers closely, but he meant that nothing stands above Scripture. The five solas summarize the whole: Scripture alone, faith alone, grace alone, Christ alone, to God alone the glory. Luther was excommunicated by Leo X in 1521 and outlawed at the Diet of Worms the same year, and survived only under the protection of his Saxon prince. Within a few years the church had splintered not into two camps but into dozens, and Protestantism has been splintering ever since.

sources.title: Luther's Battle for Sola Scriptura
sources.container: Southern Equip, Southern Baptist Theological Seminary
sources.mla: "Luther's Battle for Sola Scriptura." Southern Equip, Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, https://equip.sbts.edu/article/luthers-battle-for-sola-scriptura/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Luther's Ninety-five Theses: What You May Not Know and Why They Matter Today
sources.mla: "Luther's Ninety-five Theses: What You May Not Know and Why They Matter Today." The Gospel Coalition, https://www.thegospelcoalition.org/article/luther-ninety-five-theses-what-you-may-not-know-why-they-matter-today/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: God the Father
gods.description: The one God, and in the Reformed strand especially, sovereign to a degree that leaves nothing to human merit at all. Grace is unearned by definition; if it could be earned it would not be grace.

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: Solus Christus, Christ alone. Salvation rests on his obedience, not ours, and he is the only mediator, which is precisely why Protestants dropped the intercession of saints and the priesthood as a mediating caste.

gods.name: The Holy Spirit
gods.description: The one who gives faith, opens Scripture to the reader, and makes the believer holy. In Protestant theology the Spirit works through the Word rather than through a sacramental system.

gods.name: The Bible
gods.description: Not a god, but functionally the axis on which the whole movement turns: the norm that norms all other norms and cannot itself be normed. Luther's German translation put it in the hands of anyone who could read, and that is arguably what did the most damage to Rome.

protestant.godsNote: Protestantism is not a single church but a family of them, Lutheran, Reformed, Anglican, Anabaptist, Methodist, Baptist, Pentecostal, and hundreds more, differing sharply on the sacraments, church government, predestination, and much else. What they share is the rejection of papal authority and the priority of Scripture over tradition.

africanPoly.name:
African
Polytheism
africanPoly.description: African polytheism is a label of convenience covering the indigenous religions of a continent: thousands of distinct traditions in hundreds of languages, and scholars have warned that it is genuinely difficult to generalize about them because of the sheer number of differences. What recurs, though, recurs widely. Most of these traditions recognize a supreme being, usually a creator, and this being is characteristically distant. He made everything, he knows everything, and he is not the one you address about your harvest. Between the supreme being and human life stand the divinities, who function rather like ministers with portfolios, and the ancestors, who are nearer still, and who are in most of these traditions the single most important religious fact of daily life. Some traditions pair a god and goddess, as with Mawu-Lisa in Dahomey, moon and sun together, who apportioned the sky, sea, and earth among their children. Beneath these are spirits of place, of water, of forest, of species. These are lived religions rather than book religions: they are carried in ritual, ceremony, initiation, divination, drumming, dance, and oral transmission, not scripture, and communal ceremonies often work through possession trance induced by rhythmic drumming and song. There is typically no sharp line between the sacred and the ordinary, and no notion of conversion in the missionary sense. They fed directly into the Atlantic diaspora traditions, Vodou, Santeria, and Candomble, which are treated separately here.

sources.title: God: African Supreme Beings
sources.mla: "God: African Supreme Beings." Encyclopedia.com, https://www.encyclopedia.com/environment/encyclopedias-almanacs-transcripts-and-maps/god-african-supreme-beings. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Nyame
gods.description: The supreme deity of the Akan of Ghana: sky god, creator, and titled Nana, Great Ancestor, with all the other ancestors ranked between him and the living as mediators. His counterpart is Asase Yaa, the earth goddess.

gods.name: Nzambi Mpungu
gods.description: The supreme being of the Kongo and neighbouring peoples: self-existent, almighty, identified with the sun, and said to know everything. The Bakongo say he is made by no other and no one is beyond him.

gods.name: Mawu-Lisa
gods.domain: Sun and moon, creation, the divine pair
gods.description: The creator pair of Dahomey, Mawu associated with the moon and Lisa with the sun, together an arch-divinity who apportioned the kingdoms of sky, sea, and earth among their offspring, and made the youngest, Legba, the divine messenger.

gods.name: Amma
gods.description: The creator of the Dogon of Mali, who made the earth and coupled with it, producing the Nommo, twin beings half human and half serpent, of divine essence like himself. The Dogon cosmology built around him is one of the most elaborate ever recorded in Africa.

gods.name: Chukwu
gods.description: The supreme being of the Igbo, the great spirit who created everything, approached through the alusi, the lesser deities, and through Ala, the earth goddess who is the moral centre of Igbo life.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: Not gods, and in most of these traditions more important in daily practice than any god. They remain members of the community, they are fed and consulted, they punish neglect, and they mediate between the living and the supreme being. Ancestor veneration is the one thing that appears in nearly every African indigenous religion.

africanPoly.godsNote: This is an umbrella category, not a religion. It covers thousands of distinct traditions with different pantheons, cosmologies, and practices, and the deities listed here are a sample of widely attested supreme beings from different regions, not a shared pantheon. Several major African traditions, including Yoruba, Bantu, Nilotic, Berber, Egyptian, and San, are treated as their own entries.

bahai.name: Baha'i Faith
bahai.description: The Baha'i Faith rests on three unities: the unity of God, the unity of religion, and the unity of humanity. The second is the striking one. Baha'is hold that religious truth is not absolute but relative, and that God has revealed it progressively, through a series of Manifestations, Adam, Abraham, Krishna, Moses, Zoroaster, the Buddha, Jesus, Muhammad, the Bab, and Baha'u'llah, each bringing a revelation calibrated to the capacity of the age and place in which it appeared. Religions differ, on this view, not because some are false but because humanity was at different stages of maturity, the way a school teaches different things in different grades. Essential spiritual truths do not change; the social provisions, dietary rules, direction of prayer, legal codes, do, and are meant to. And the process does not end: Baha'is expect another Manifestation, though not within a thousand years of Baha'u'llah. The faith began in Iran in 1844, when a young merchant took the title the Bab, the Gate, and preached that a greater messenger was coming. He told his followers that the path of guidance is one of love and compassion, not of force. He was publicly executed for heresy in 1850, and thousands of his followers were killed. In 1863 Mirza Husayn-Ali Nuri, exiled and imprisoned, declared that he was the one foretold, and took the name Baha'u'llah, the Glory of God. He spent forty years in exile, torture, and imprisonment. The teachings that came out of it are relentlessly practical: the oneness of humanity, the elimination of racial and religious prejudice, the equality of men and women, universal education, the harmony of science and religion, a universal auxiliary language, a world federation. There is no priesthood and no ritual worship. Baha'is are still persecuted in Iran.

gods.name: God
gods.domain: Creation, unity, the source of all revelation
gods.description: One God, and in his essence unknowable, which is why the Manifestations exist at all. They are the only channel through which humanity can know anything of him: perfect mirrors reflecting the divine attributes into the physical world.

gods.name: Baha'u'llah
gods.description: The founder, who declared in 1863 that he was the messenger the Bab had promised, and who spent forty years in exile and imprisonment for it. Not the last Manifestation, and the Baha'i writings are explicit that there will never be a last. His major works are the Kitab-i-Aqdas and the Kitab-i-Iqan.

gods.name: The Bab
gods.description: The merchant who declared in 1844 that he bore a new revelation and that a greater messenger was coming. Publicly executed for heresy in 1850. His shrine is on Mount Carmel in Haifa.
gods.imageQuery: Shrine of the Bab Haifa terraces

gods.name: The Manifestations of God
gods.description: Krishna, Abraham, Moses, Zoroaster, the Buddha, Jesus, Muhammad, the Bab, Baha'u'llah, and others named and unnamed. All from the same God, all of the same spiritual nature, and equal: the differences between their teachings reflect the needs of the age, not any difference in their rank.

bahai.godsNote: The Baha'i Faith is strictly monotheistic. The Manifestations are not incarnations of God and are not worshipped; they are mirrors. Baha'is explicitly reject the label 'syncretic', holding Baha'u'llah's revelation to be independent, related to earlier ones as Christianity is related to Judaism.

reformJudaism.name:
Reform
Judaism
reformJudaism.description: Reform Judaism began in Germany in the early nineteenth century, among Jews who were being offered political emancipation and social integration for the first time, and who began to ask which of their distinguishing practices were essential. Its intellectual father was Abraham Geiger, who argued that Judaism had always developed, that revelation to the prophets was the beginning of an evolutionary process, that each generation had given fresh meaning to inherited liturgy and practice, and that new truth becomes available to every age. The unchanging essence, on Geiger's account, was not the law but the morality: ethical monotheism. The Jews were not a nation but a religious community, charged with a mission to be a light to the nations and to spread ethical monotheism through the world, and their dispersion was not a punishment but the mechanism of that mission. Geiger deleted the prayers for a return to Zion from the prayerbook he edited in 1854. In America the movement's classical statement was the Pittsburgh Platform of 1885: Judaism is a progressive religion, ever striving to be in accord with reason, and modern science is not antagonistic to it; ritual laws not adapted to modern civilization, kashrut and dress codes among them, may be rejected as obstructing rather than furthering spiritual elevation; bodily resurrection, heaven, and hell are rejected as ideas not rooted in Judaism; and, most consequentially, the Jews are no longer a nation but a religious community, expecting neither a return to Palestine nor the restoration of a Jewish state. That last plank did not survive the twentieth century. The Columbus Platform of 1937, facing the rise of Nazism, affirmed Jewish peoplehood and support for settlement in Palestine, an almost complete reversal, and evidence that the movement meant what it said about every generation revising what it received.

sources.title: The Pittsburgh Platform: Defining American Reform Judaism (1885)
sources.mla: "The Pittsburgh Platform: Defining American Reform Judaism (1885)." American Jewish Archives, https://www.americanjewisharchives.org/snapshots/the-pittsburgh-platform-defining-american-reform-judaism-1885/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: God
gods.description: The one God, approached in classical Reform chiefly as the ground of the moral law rather than as a lawgiver whose ritual commands bind. The Pittsburgh Platform claims for Judaism the highest conception of the God-idea, and treats the Bible not as divine dictation but as the record of the Jewish people's consecration to its mission.

reformJudaism.godsNote: Reform Judaism is monotheistic, and its distinctive claims are not about the nature of God but about the authority of law, the status of ritual, and whether the Jews are a nation or a religious community. Contemporary Reform has moved a long way from the 1885 platform, and many congregations have restored practices it discarded.

islamSunni.name: Islam
islamSunni.description: Islam means submission, and it rests on tawhid: the absolute oneness of God, who has no partner, no equal, no offspring, and no rival. The confession that carries it is the shahada, that there is no god but God and Muhammad is the messenger of God, spoken in every prayer, said into the ear of a newborn and again over the dying. The Quran, revealed to Muhammad from 610 until his death in 632, is held to be God's uncreated word; the Sunnah, his normative practice as recorded in the hadith, is its constitutional companion. From these, plus scholarly consensus and analogical reasoning, the four Sunni schools of law, Hanafi, Hanbali, Maliki, and Shafi'i, derive sharia. Practice rests on the Five Pillars: the shahada; salah, the five daily prayers, preceded by ablution and oriented toward Mecca; zakat, obligatory almsgiving, usually 2.5% of accumulated wealth, and purification is what the word means, since the payment makes the rest of one's wealth clean; sawm, the fast of Ramadan; and hajj, the pilgrimage. The name Sunni comes from sunnah: those who follow the practice of the Prophet. The split from the Shia is over succession. Sunnis hold that Muhammad appointed no successor and that Abu Bakr was rightly chosen by the community, and they revere the first four caliphs, Abu Bakr, Umar, Uthman, and Ali, as rightly guided. Sunnis make up the large majority of the world's Muslims. Sufism sits inside Sunnism, not outside it, and always has.

sources.title: Five Pillars of Islam
sources.mla: "Five Pillars of Islam." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Five_Pillars_of_Islam. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Pillars of Islam
sources.mla: "Pillars of Islam." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Pillars-of-Islam. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Allah
gods.description: The one God. The word is simply Arabic for 'the God', and Arabic-speaking Jews and Christians use it too. Tawhid means his absolute oneness: no partners, no equals, no offspring, nothing comparable. He is called by ninety-nine names, of which the two most often repeated are the Compassionate and the Merciful.

gods.name: Muhammad
gods.description: Not divine, and Islam is emphatic about this: the seal of the prophets, the last in a line that includes Adam, Abraham, Moses, and Jesus, and the conduit through which the Quran came. His practice, the Sunnah, is the model for how to live, and is why the branch is called Sunni.

gods.name: The angels
gods.description: Created of light, without free will, and belief in them is one of the six articles of faith. Jibril brought the revelation; two recording angels attend every person; Israfil will blow the trumpet.

islamSunni.godsNote: Islam is strictly monotheistic. Muhammad, the prophets, and the angels are not divine, and depicting them is avoided in most of the tradition, which is one reason Islamic sacred art turned so decisively toward calligraphy and geometry.

islamShiite.name:
Islam
(Shiite)
islamShiite.description: The Shia hold that Muhammad did not leave the succession to the community's judgment, that God appointed it, and that God appointed Ali, the Prophet's cousin and son-in-law, at Ghadir Khumm. From that follows the imamate, which is the whole of Shia theology in one word. There is always an Imam of the Age: divinely appointed, the authoritative guide on all matters of faith and law, preserver and interpreter of the sharia and of the esoteric meaning of the Quran, and ma'sum, infallible, free from error and sin. The Imams are not prophets; they carry the Prophet's message. Twelver Shi'ism, about 85% of all Shia, counts twelve, from Ali down to Muhammad al-Mahdi, who was born around 868 and who, on his father's death in 874, entered occultation, concealment by God. For some seventy years he was said to communicate through four successive deputies; then, in 941, the Major Occultation began, and he has been hidden ever since, alive in his physical body, and will return at the end of time as the Mahdi, the rightly guided one, to fill the earth with justice as it is now filled with tyranny. Jesus returns with him. In his absence, believers who are not themselves jurists follow a marja al-taqlid, a living source of emulation. And over everything sits Karbala: in 680 Ali's son Husayn was killed with his household by the forces of the caliph Yazid, and the survivors were marched across the desert. Ashura mourns it every year, and it has made Shi'ism a religion organized around the memory of an injustice.

gods.name: Allah
gods.description: The one God, with justice given a structural place in Twelver theology that it does not have in Sunni creeds: God is just, necessarily, and would not leave humanity without access to divine guidance. Which is the argument for the imamate.

gods.name: Muhammad
gods.description: The final prophet, and in Shia belief infallible along with his household, the ahl al-bayt. The Shia shahada adds a third clause: that Ali is the wali, the custodian, of God.

gods.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
gods.description: Cousin and son-in-law of the Prophet, and in Shia belief his divinely appointed successor. Credited with establishing Islamic theology and with the first philosophical proofs of God's unity. Assassinated by a Kharijite; buried at Najaf, which remains a centre of Shia scholarship.

gods.name: Husayn ibn Ali
gods.description: Killed at Karbala in 680 with his household by Yazid's army; the women and children were then marched to Damascus. Ashura and Arbaeen mourn it, and the event gave Shi'ism its central moral grammar: sacrifice, injustice, and refusal to submit to an illegitimate ruler.

gods.name: Muhammad al-Mahdi
gods.domain: Occultation, eschatology, the restoration of justice
gods.description: The twelfth Imam, born around 868, hidden since 874, alive and guiding the faithful inwardly, and awaited. He will return when God permits, at a time when the earth is filled with tyranny, and Jesus will return with him.

gods.name: Fatimah
gods.description: The Prophet's daughter, Ali's wife, mother of Hasan and Husayn, and in Twelver belief infallible. The line of Imams descends through her.

islamShiite.godsNote: Shia Islam is strictly monotheistic. The Imams are not divine and not prophets; they are infallible human guides. Zaydi Shia reject both infallibility and the occultation; Ismailis follow a different line and a different doctrine of the imamate.

sufism.name: Sufism
sufism.description: Sufism is not a sect; it is the interior dimension of Islam, and it sits inside Sunnism, a fact the classical scholars took for granted. The Sufi seeks direct, personal knowledge of God, and the path, tariqa, begins with repentance and proceeds under a shaykh, through ascetic discipline and meditation, through a sequence of stations that are earned and states that are given. The struggle is against the nafs, the lower self. The goal is fana, the annihilation of the self, and union with God. The central practice is dhikr, the remembrance of God: the repeated recitation of his names or of a devotional formula, sometimes silent, sometimes chanted, sometimes with music and poetry in the sama. From the twelfth century the orders formed, Qadiriyya, Naqshbandiyya, Chishtiyya, Shadhiliyya, Mevleviyya, each claiming a chain of spiritual descent, silsila, back to the Prophet, each with its own disciplines, each initiating members and building lodges where laity as well as adepts could stay. By the mid-twentieth century there were hundreds of orders with millions of members. The great figures are Rumi, who died in 1273 and whose followers founded the Mevlevi order at Konya, and whose whirling, a ritual prayer performed spinning on the right foot, Europeans reduced to dancing dervishes; and Ibn al-Arabi, who died in 1240 and taught the Unity of Being, that all existence is a manifestation of the divine reality. Sober teachers warned constantly against excess. Sufis carried Islam into Africa, India, and Southeast Asia, and modern fundamentalist movements attack them with a venom they reserve for almost nothing else.

gods.name: Allah
gods.description: The same God of Islam, but approached as the Beloved, and sought not through obedience alone but through love, longing, and finally annihilation of the self in him. Ibn al-Arabi's Unity of Being holds that everything that exists is a manifestation of the divine reality.

gods.name: Muhammad
gods.domain: The model of the path, spiritual descent
gods.description: The origin of every silsila. Every Sufi order traces its chain of transmission back to him, usually through Ali. In Sufi metaphysics he is also the Perfect Man, the fullest human mirror of the divine.

gods.name: The awliya
gods.description: The friends of God, saints whose tombs became pilgrimage sites from Morocco to Java. Reformers denounce pilgrimage to Sufi tombs as idolatry and reject the idea that saints can intercede at all, which is the sharpest fault line in modern Islam.

sufism.godsNote: Sufism is Islam and is strictly monotheistic. The saints are venerated, not worshipped, but that veneration is exactly what Salafi and Wahhabi movements attack as shirk, and many Sufi shrines have been destroyed on those grounds.

kabbalah.name: Kabbalah
kabbalah.description: Kabbalah means 'that which is received', the teaching handed down. Its architecture is a single daring idea: God as he is in himself, Ein Sof, Without End, is utterly hidden, beyond thought, beyond language, and beyond any name in the Bible, because the letters of those names were produced only after the emanation. So the God of prayer and Torah is not Ein Sof directly, but the ten Sefirot, the powers through which the Infinite makes itself knowable, arranged as the Tree of Life. The Kabbalists insist constantly that the Sefirot are bound up in Ein Sof and have no existence apart from it, because the alternative is duality, and duality is idolatry. Its foundational texts are the Sefer Yetzirah, the Sefer Bahir, and above all the Zohar, a vast Aramaic commentary on the Torah that appeared in thirteenth-century Spain, attributed to a second-century sage but written, scholarship agrees, by Moses de Leon. That is not fraud; pseudepigraphy is a recognized mystical genre. The Zohar's theory of reading is its engine: the plain stories of scripture are a garment, beneath which lie a body and a soul. Then, in sixteenth-century Safed, Isaac Luria recast the whole thing as a cosmic drama. To make room for a world, the Infinite first withdraws, tzimtzum, a self-contraction. The vessels meant to hold the divine light shatter, scattering sparks through a broken creation. And the repair of that rupture is tikkun, accomplished by human beings, through ritual and righteous living. From which comes the phrase that has travelled furthest of all, into places that have never heard of Luria: tikkun olam, the mending of the world.

gods.name: Ein Sof
gods.domain: The hidden God, beyond all attribute
gods.description: God as he is in himself: infinite, unknowable, unnameable, and beyond every biblical name, since those names came into being only with the emanation. Nothing can be predicated of Ein Sof at all, and the tradition is rigorous about this.

gods.name: The Sefirot
gods.domain: Divine attributes, creation, the knowable God
gods.description: The ten powers, Keter, Chokhmah, Binah, Chesed, Gevurah, Tiferet, Netzach, Hod, Yesod, Malkhut, through which the Infinite becomes the God one can address. They are not ten gods, and the Kabbalists stress this constantly, out of fear that misreading would produce exactly that.
gods.imageQuery: Sefirot Tree of Life Kabbalistic illustration

gods.name: The Shekhinah
gods.description: The tenth Sefirah, God's indwelling presence in the world, grammatically and theologically feminine, and in Kabbalistic thought in exile along with Israel. Reuniting the Shekhinah with the upper Sefirot is what much of Kabbalistic practice is actually for.

kabbalah.godsNote: Kabbalah is a mystical tradition within Judaism, inseparable from Torah and halakha, and rigorously monotheistic. The Sefirot are aspects of the one God, not beings. Hermetic 'Qabalah', the Western esoteric adaptation grafted onto Tarot, astrology, and ceremonial magic from the Renaissance onward, is a different thing wearing the same name.

ayyavazhi.name: Ayyavazhi
ayyavazhi.description: Ayyavazhi, the Path of Ayya, began in South India in 1833, when a man named Muthukutty, after a year of severe illness, went into the sea at Thiruchendur and disappeared. On the third day, according to the Akilathirattu Ammanai, he emerged transformed: Ayya Vaikundar, the son of Narayana, no longer his mother's son. He went to Poovandanthope, now Swamithope, and undertook a six-year penance, two years standing in a pit, two years squatting, two years on a raised platform, and then began to teach. The theology is genuinely its own. Above the Hindu gods sits Ekam, the source of all existence, beyond consciousness; Vaikundar is the incarnation of Ekam in human form, sent to destroy Kaliyan, the sixth fragment of the primordial evil Kroni, who in this age has no body and instead occupies the minds of human beings as illusion. But the reason it spread was social. Vaikundar preached against caste in a Travancore society organized entirely around it, in language that is unambiguous: cast away the eighteen castes, do not discriminate between the powerful and the powerless, and the caste would disappear by itself. He rejected idol worship and ritual sacrifice. He held communal feasts and mass bathing rituals whose whole purpose was to dissolve social boundaries, and most of his followers came from the Nadar caste and other marginalized communities. The colonial state imprisoned him. He died in 1851. Ayyavazhi helped trigger the Upper Cloth agitation and the temple-entry movements, and influenced Narayana Guru and Ayyankali. In the census, most of its followers are still counted as Hindus.

gods.name: Ekam
gods.domain: The ultimate reality beyond all gods
gods.description: The all-pervading divine essence from which everything, including the gods, arises, and which lies beyond consciousness itself. This is the properly monotheistic core of Ayyavazhi, and it is largely unknown to most of the tradition's own followers today.

gods.name: Ayya Vaikundar
gods.domain: Incarnation, social equality, the destruction of Kali
gods.description: The founder: the incarnation of Ekam, and simultaneously the son of Narayana and an ordinary human body. He is not merely a man, not merely Narayana, and not merely the Ultimate Soul, but all three at once. He preached against caste, was imprisoned by the colonial state, and died in 1851.

gods.name: Narayana
gods.description: Inherited from Vaishnavism, and in the Akilam's cosmology the supreme power from the beginning of Kali Yuga, until Vaikundar, at which point the theology moves past him.

gods.name: Kroni
gods.description: The primordial evil, shattered into six fragments across the six yugas. The first five took physical form and Narayana incarnated to destroy each. The sixth, in this age, has no body: it occupies the minds of human beings as illusion. Which is why this incarnation had to work differently.
