ayyavazhi.godsNote: Ayyavazhi's own scripture teaches a strict monotheism centred on Ekam, but in practice many followers treat it as a Hindu sect, include Vaikundar among the avatars of Vishnu, and offer worship to lesser gods, a gap between the Akilam and lived practice that the sources note openly.

sikhism.name: Sikhism
sikhism.description: Sikhism opens with a symbol rather than a sentence: Ik Onkar, the first mark in the Guru Granth Sahib. The numeral 1 is not the word 'one'; it is the figure, chosen deliberately so that it cannot be argued into meaning anything else. There is one Reality. The Mool Mantar unfolds it: one God, true by name, the creator, without fear, without hatred, timeless in form, unborn, self-existent, realized by the Guru's grace. The God of Sikhism is Waheguru, the Wondrous Teacher, shapeless, timeless, beyond birth and death, incomprehensible and invisible, and simultaneously within everything and encompassing everything. Guru Nanak, who lived from 1469 to 1539, taught that the highest worship is loving devotion, and that the essential practice is naam simran, remembrance of the divine name. He travelled as far as Mecca and Baghdad, spoke in temples and mosques alike, and preached against empty ritual, against pilgrimage as a substitute for inner change, against the caste system, against the sacrifice of widows, and against depending on books to learn true religion, a striking thing to have said given what happened next. Nine Gurus followed him. The tenth, Guru Gobind Singh, founded the Khalsa and then, in 1708, closed the human line: the Guru Granth Sahib itself became the eternal Guru. The Word is the Guru now. It is treated as a living one, robed, carried on the head, put to rest at night on its own bed, and it is the only scripture of its kind that contains not only the words of its own founders but the hymns of fourteen Hindu bhakti saints and one Muslim Sufi. The practical heart of the religion is seva, selfless service, and langar, the free community kitchen in every gurdwara, where everyone, of any religion, caste, or status, sits on the floor at the same level and eats the same food.

gods.name: Waheguru
gods.description: The Wondrous Teacher: one, shapeless, timeless, unborn, self-existent, incomprehensible, beyond death, and at the same time within everything and encompassing everything. God has no gender in Sikhism, though the metaphors run masculine for God and feminine for God's creative power. The name is what is remembered, constantly, in naam simran.

gods.name: Guru Nanak
gods.domain: Devotion, equality, the founding of the path
gods.description: The founder, a bhakti saint who started the tradition of kirtan, the singing of the hymns, and the institution of langar. He is revered, and he is not worshipped: a teacher, not a god.

gods.name: The Guru Granth Sahib
gods.description: Not a god, but the eternal Guru, and treated as a living one. Sikhs bow before it on entering a gurdwara. Guru Gobind Singh's last command was that all Sikhs should take the Granth as Guru.
gods.imageQuery: Guru Granth Sahib gurdwara Sikh scripture

sikhism.godsNote: Sikhism is strictly monotheistic, monistic even: nothing exists except God, and no god, goddess, or adversary has reality such as God's. The ten Gurus are teachers, revered but never worshipped, and Sikhism rejects the claim that any single tradition holds a monopoly on absolute truth.

theravada.name:
Theravada
Buddhism
theravada.description: Theravada means the teaching of the elders, and it holds itself to be the closest surviving thing to what the Buddha actually taught. Its canon is the Pali Canon, the earliest complete body of Buddhist scripture, and it does not accept the Mahayana sutras as the word of the Buddha. Its structure is the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path, its analysis is the three marks of existence, impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, and no-self, and its goal is the arhat: one who has seen through to the end, extinguished craving, and will not be reborn. The path is worked, not granted. Insight into the four truths, in the Theravada view, is liberating in itself. The tradition is analytical, and it distrusts metaphysical elaboration; the Buddha is a historical teacher who found the way and pointed at it, not a cosmic being. Monasticism is central: the sangha is the institution that carries the teaching, and the lay relationship to it is one of support and merit. Meditation splits into samatha, calming, and vipassana, insight, and it is vipassana, in the commentarial tradition and in the modern movement that grew out of it, that does the liberating work: watching the arising and passing of phenomena until the mind stops grasping at any of it. Theravada is the dominant form of Buddhism in Sri Lanka, Thailand, Myanmar, Cambodia, and Laos, and it is the tradition from which the Western mindfulness movement was extracted, though the vipassana teachings that reached the West mostly arrived stripped of the doctrines of karma, rebirth, and nirvana that gave them their point.

sources.title: Four Noble Truths
sources.author: Lopez, Donald S.
sources.mla: Lopez, Donald S. "Four Noble Truths." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Four-Noble-Truths. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The Buddha
gods.description: In Theravada, a human teacher who woke up and described how. Honoured, taken refuge in, but not a god and not a saviour. He is gone; the teaching remains, and each person has to walk it themselves.

gods.name: The arhat
gods.description: Not a deity but the goal: one who has fully awakened, ended craving, and escaped samsara. Mahayana would later characterize this ideal as a lesser vehicle that stops short of full Buddhahood, which Theravada disputes.

theravada.godsNote: Theravada is non-theistic. The devas of Indian cosmology exist in its texts but are themselves subject to rebirth and cannot liberate anyone; there is no creator and no saviour. Popular Theravada practice in Southeast Asia does incorporate local spirits and protective deities, which the canonical tradition tolerates without endorsing.

mahayana.name:
Mahayana
Buddhism
mahayana.description: Mahayana means the Great Vehicle, and the name is a claim: that the goal of individual escape is a lesser one, and that the real aim is Buddhahood, for the sake of everything that lives. Its central figure is the bodhisattva, a being who has generated bodhicitta, the mind of awakening, and vowed to attain full enlightenment for the benefit of all sentient beings, and who therefore voluntarily remains in the cycle of rebirth rather than exiting it. The path takes aeons and ten stages. Compassion and wisdom are co-equal, not sequential; you do not get wise and then get kind. Philosophically, Mahayana pushed the doctrine of emptiness, sunyata, far past where earlier Buddhism had left it: not only is there no self, there is no intrinsic essence to anything at all. Everything arises dependently, including emptiness itself, which is why Nagarjuna spends so much time dismantling positions rather than defending one. It also brought an enormous expansion of scripture, the Heart Sutra, the Diamond Sutra, the Lotus Sutra, the Vimalakirti, which the tradition regards as genuine teachings of the Buddha disclosed to audiences prepared to receive them, and which Theravada regards as later inventions. And the Buddha himself changes: from a historical teacher who died into a transcendent principle with three bodies, present in all beings as Buddha-nature. Mahayana went up the Silk Road into China and generated Chan, which became Zen in Japan, along with Pure Land and Tiantai, and spread to Korea and Vietnam. It is now the numerically dominant form of Buddhism in the world.

gods.name: Avalokiteshvara
gods.description: The bodhisattva of compassion, who hears the cries of the world and does not turn away, and who in China gradually became female and became Guanyin, one of the most beloved religious figures on earth. Not a god who created anything, but a being of unlimited compassion who can be appealed to.

gods.name: Amitabha
gods.description: The Buddha who vowed that anyone who called his name in faith would be reborn in Sukhavati, the Pure Land, where awakening becomes easy. Pure Land Buddhism, built on that vow, is the most widely practised form of Buddhism in East Asia, and the closest Buddhism gets to a doctrine of salvation by grace.

gods.name: Manjushri
gods.description: The bodhisattva of wisdom, holding a sword that cuts through ignorance and a book of the Perfection of Wisdom. Wisdom in Mahayana means seeing emptiness, and the sword is for cutting through the concepts one is using to see it.

gods.name: Maitreya
gods.description: The bodhisattva awaiting his time, who will appear when the teaching has been forgotten and turn the wheel again. Often depicted seated in a chair, waiting.

gods.name: Buddha-nature
gods.domain: The potential for awakening in all beings
gods.description: Not a deity but the claim that awakening is not something added from outside but something uncovered, and that it is already present in every sentient being. This is the ground on which Zen builds its whole method.

mahayana.godsNote: Mahayana is non-theistic; there is no creator. But the bodhisattvas and cosmic Buddhas function devotionally in ways that look, from outside, very like gods: they are venerated, petitioned, and depicted everywhere. The tradition's own account is that they are enlightened beings, not creators.

vajrayana.name:
Vajrayana
Buddhism
vajrayana.description: Vajrayana means the Diamond Vehicle, or the Thunderbolt Vehicle, vajra being both, and standing for the indestructible awake nature that is inseparable from emptiness. It emerged in medieval India as the last great development of Indian Buddhism before its collapse, and it is built on Mahayana: same emptiness, same bodhisattva vow, same compassion. What it adds is method. Where the sutra path is said to take aeons, tantra claims to make Buddhahood attainable in a single lifetime, and it does so by working with the passions rather than renouncing them, transformation rather than suppression. Its distinctive literature is the tantras, which deal not with the intellectual problem of reality but with the existential one: what it actually feels like to get there. Its methods are deity yoga, in which one visualizes oneself as an enlightened being, since Buddha-nature is already present and the practice is a way of inhabiting it, along with mandalas, mantras, mudras, and a set of practices transmitted only under initiation from a qualified teacher, which is why so much of it is secret and why the guru relationship is so much more central here than anywhere else in Buddhism. Its imagery is famously startling: wrathful deities wreathed in flame, skulls, and the yab-yum union of a passive female figure signifying wisdom with a dynamic male one signifying compassion, a union that stands for the realization itself, known experientially rather than cognitively. Vajrayana is the Buddhism of Tibet, Bhutan, Nepal, and Mongolia, and it absorbed a great deal of the indigenous Bon tradition on the way in. Japanese Shingon is also a Vajrayana lineage.

sources.title: Buddhism: Vajrayana, Tantric, or Esoteric Buddhism
sources.mla: "Buddhism: Vajrayana, Tantric, or Esoteric Buddhism." Encyclopaedia Britannica, https://www.britannica.com/topic/Buddhism/Vajrayana-Tantric-or-Esoteric-Buddhism. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Vajradhara
gods.domain: The primordial Buddha, the source of tantra
gods.description: The primordial Buddha from whom the tantric transmission descends, dark blue, holding vajra and bell crossed at the heart. Not a creator: the personification of the dharmakaya, the reality-body of enlightenment itself.

gods.name: Padmasambhava
gods.domain: The transmission of tantra to Tibet
gods.description: The eighth-century tantric master credited with bringing Vajrayana to Tibet and subduing the local deities, not destroying them but binding them into service as protectors, which is a fairly accurate description of what Buddhism did to Tibetan religion generally.

gods.name: Tara
gods.description: Born, in the story, from a tear of Avalokiteshvara. Where he weeps, she acts. Green Tara is swift rescue from danger; White Tara is healing and long life. One foot is always stepping down off the lotus, ready to move.

gods.name: Vajrayogini
gods.description: A wrathful female Buddha, red, dancing, holding a skull cup and a curved knife, an image designed to be unsettling. The wrath is not anger; it is the ferocity of wisdom cutting through ego, and the practice is about transmuting the passions rather than avoiding them.

gods.name: Mahakala
gods.description: A wrathful protector, black, many-armed, wreathed in flame and skulls. In Vajrayana the terrifying forms are not demons but enlightened compassion in its fiercest mode, and they guard the teaching and the practitioner.

vajrayana.godsNote: Vajrayana is non-theistic and has no creator. The deities of deity yoga are not beings to be propitiated but enlightened qualities to be visualized and inhabited: the practitioner visualizes themselves as the deity, on the grounds that Buddha-nature is already present. Much of the practice is transmitted only under initiation.

bon.name: Bon
bon.description: Bon presents a problem of definition before it presents anything else. Bonpos hold that it is the original religion of Tibet, taught in the kingdom of Zhangzhung around Mount Kailash by the Buddha Tonpa Shenrab Miwo, Supreme Holy Man, thousands of years before Shakyamuni. Scholars distinguish at least three things called Bon: a prehistoric ritual system of Zhangzhung and Tibet, mostly extinct but surviving in fortune-calling, soul-retrieval, and ransom rituals; Yungdrung Bon, Eternal Bon, a living tradition that took its present form in the tenth and eleventh centuries and closely resembles Nyingma Buddhism; and New Bon, a syncretic movement from the fourteenth century that incorporates Padmasambhava. Tonpa Shenrab's biography runs remarkably parallel to the Buddha's, with royal birth, renunciation, austerity, teaching, and death in old age, and Tibetan Buddhists have never stopped saying so, calling Bon an imitation. Bonpos reply that Shenrab came first and that Buddhism copied them. There are no sources before the tenth century with which to establish his historicity at all. What is not in dispute: Yungdrung Bon has monasteries, ordained monks under vows of celibacy, philosophical debate, a two-part canon, a system of Nine Ways, and a complete Dzogchen tradition of its own. Its emblem is the yungdrung, the left-facing swastika, holding the place the vajra holds in Buddhism: indestructibility and eternity. Bon reformed animal sacrifice out of Tibetan practice by substituting tormas, cakes shaped like animals. The Dalai Lama recognized it in 1978 as the fifth school of Tibet, and has stressed its role as the indigenous source of Tibetan culture.

gods.name: Tonpa Shenrab Miwo
gods.domain: The founding of Bon, teaching, enlightenment
gods.description: The founder, a fully enlightened being who, Bonpos hold, descended from the heavenly realms and was born a prince in Olmo Lungring, renounced his inheritance, and taught for decades. He occupies the position Shakyamuni holds in Buddhism. No source earlier than the tenth century establishes that he existed.

gods.name: Sherab Chamma
gods.description: The great mother of wisdom in Bon, the counterpart to the feminine wisdom principle in Buddhist tantra, and one of the most important figures in Bonpo devotion.

gods.name: Drenpa Namkha
gods.domain: Preservation, the hiding of the teachings
gods.description: The most influential historical figure of the imperial period, and, unusually for this tradition, one whose existence Buddhist sources also attest, so there is little doubt he was real. He ordained himself into Bon while the religion was being suppressed and hid vast numbers of texts as terma, treasures to be rediscovered later.

gods.name: The local spirits
gods.description: The indigenous deities of the Tibetan landscape, of mountains, lakes, springs, and soil, who must be kept in relationship. The oldest layer of Bon is largely about this: calling fortune, retrieving lost souls, and paying ransom to the beings who have taken something.

bon.godsNote: Whether Bon predates Buddhism in Tibet or was substantially shaped by it is one of the genuinely unresolved questions in the field, and both sides have polemical reasons for their positions. Bonpos also reject the label 'shamanism' for Yungdrung Bon, noting that Tonpa Shenrab abolished animal sacrifice and that the word 'shaman' is Siberian and has no Tibetan equivalent.

mormonism.name: Mormonism
mormonism.description: The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints was founded in 1830 in upstate New York by Joseph Smith, who taught that all existing churches had fallen into apostasy after the death of the apostles and that God had chosen him to restore the true church. Its distinctive scripture is the Book of Mormon, which Smith said he translated by divine power from golden plates delivered by an angel, and which presents itself as the sacred record of ancient Israelite peoples who migrated to the Americas and were visited by the risen Christ. Alongside the Bible, Latter-day Saints accept the Book of Mormon, the Doctrine and Covenants, and the Pearl of Great Price as scripture. Two doctrines set Mormonism sharply apart from historic Christianity. The first concerns the nature of God: in Smith's later teaching, especially the King Follett sermon, God the Father was once a man who progressed to godhood, and there may be a plurality of gods, though Latter-day Saints worship only the Father through the Son. The second is exaltation, or eternal progression: human beings are understood as literal spirit children of Heavenly Father who existed before birth and who can, through faith, temple ordinances, and righteous living, progress to become gods themselves, as God did before them. Salvation is by grace after all one can do. The priesthood, restored to Smith by angelic ministration, the temple ordinances including baptism for the dead and celestial marriage, and the traceable line of authority through Smith's successors are all central. Smith was killed by a mob in 1844; most Latter-day Saints then followed Brigham Young west to Utah.

sources.title: From Monotheism to Eternal Progression: The Evolution of the Mormon Doctrine of Exaltation
sources.mla: "From Monotheism to Eternal Progression: The Evolution of the Mormon Doctrine of Exaltation." Institute for Religious Research, https://mit.irr.org/monotheism-eternal-progression-evolution-of-mormon-doctrine-of-exaltation. Accessed 14 July 2026.

sources.title: Mormonism
sources.mla: "Mormonism." ris3n's Apologetics Codex, https://ris3n.com/codex/mormonism/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Heavenly Father
gods.description: The God worshipped by Latter-day Saints, understood in Smith's later teaching as an exalted, embodied being who was once a man and progressed to godhood, and who is the literal father of human spirits. This differs fundamentally from the classical Christian doctrine of an uncreated, eternal, immaterial God.

gods.name: Jesus Christ
gods.description: The Savior, whose atonement makes salvation possible, and who, Latter-day Saints believe, visited the peoples of the Book of Mormon after his resurrection. Distinct in person from the Father, and identified with the Old Testament Jehovah.

gods.name: Heavenly Mother
gods.domain: The divine feminine, the spiritual family
gods.description: A distinctive Latter-day Saint belief: a Mother in Heaven, the divine consort of the Father and co-parent of human spirits. She is acknowledged but rarely discussed in worship, and the doctrine follows directly from the idea of eternal families and human divine potential.

gods.name: Joseph Smith
gods.description: Not divine, but the founding prophet, whom Latter-day Saints revere as the instrument through whom the true church, priesthood, and scripture were restored. He reported visits from God the Father and Christ, from the angel Moroni, and from resurrected apostles who conferred priesthood authority.

mormonism.godsNote: Latter-day Saints consider themselves Christian and worship one God, the Father, through Christ, but Mormon theology diverges sharply from historic Christian orthodoxy: God as an exalted, formerly human being, a plurality of gods, and human exaltation to godhood. Other Christian traditions generally regard these as incompatible with monotheism as they understand it.

scientology.name: Scientology
scientology.description: Scientology was created by the American science-fiction writer L. Ron Hubbard, who introduced its precursor, Dianetics, in 1950 as a form of talk therapy, then reframed and expanded it into a religion, founding the Church of Scientology in 1954. Its central belief is that each person is an immortal spiritual being called a thetan, the true self, which has lived through countless past lives, predates the body, and originally created the physical universe (called MEST, for matter, energy, space, and time) before becoming trapped within it and forgetting its own nature. Human suffering, in this system, comes from engrams: recordings of past painful or traumatic experiences, many from earlier lifetimes, stored in a 'reactive mind' that drives irrational behaviour. The core practice is auditing, a one-on-one counselling process using a device called an E-meter, which measures a small electrical current through the body and is said to help locate engrams so they can be confronted and erased. A member progresses along a structured path called the Bridge to Total Freedom, from Preclear to Clear (free of the reactive mind) and then up the confidential Operating Thetan levels toward full spiritual freedom and recovered innate abilities. The most notorious of these upper-level teachings, revealed only to advanced members, is the story of Xenu, a galactic ruler said to have brought billions of beings to Earth 75 million years ago and killed them near volcanoes, their spirits now clustering on living people as 'body thetans'. Scientology has drawn extensive controversy over its finances, its treatment of members and critics, its aggressive litigation, and its hostility to psychiatry.

sources.title: Scientology Beliefs and Practices
sources.mla: "Scientology Beliefs and Practices." Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientology_beliefs_and_practices. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The thetan
gods.domain: The true self, immortality, past lives
gods.description: Not a god to be worshipped but the core of Scientology's anthropology: each person is a thetan, an immortal spirit that has lived countless lives, created the physical universe, and became trapped in it. The whole practice aims at recovering the thetan's native, near-godlike abilities. Everything in Scientology rests on this concept.

gods.name: The Supreme Being
gods.description: Scientology formally acknowledges a Supreme Being or 'Eighth Dynamic' as the ultimate reality, but deliberately leaves it undefined and prescribes no worship of it, holding that each person approaches infinity in their own way. The religion's practical attention is entirely on the thetan, not on a deity.

gods.name: L. Ron Hubbard
gods.description: Not divine, but revered as the founder and sole source of Scientology's teachings, or 'technology'. The church describes his death in 1986 as his decision to 'drop his body' and continue research in a discarnate state, and preserves his writings and offices for his return.

scientology.godsNote: Scientology acknowledges an undefined Supreme Being but does not worship it; its focus is the thetan, the immortal self. It has been intensely controversial, with its religious status contested in several countries and extensive criticism over its finances, litigation, treatment of members, and opposition to psychiatry. The Xenu material is confidential doctrine disclosed only at advanced levels.

haitianVoodoo.name: Haitian Voodoo
haitianVoodoo.description: Haitian Vodou developed among enslaved West and Central Africans in the French colony of Saint-Domingue, now Haiti, between the sixteenth and nineteenth centuries, through the fusion of the traditional religions of the Fon, Yoruba, Kongo, and other peoples with the Catholicism their captors imposed. Forced to attend Mass and venerate the saints, the enslaved recognized in the chromolithographs of Catholic saints a way to keep their own divinities alive behind an acceptable mask, and out of this synthesis Vodou took shape. It is, in its own self-understanding, a monotheistic religion: above everything is Bondye, the good God, the transcendent creator, who is omnipotent but remote and does not concern himself with the daily affairs of humanity. Ritual life is directed not to Bondye but to the lwa (loa), the spirits Bondye created to mediate between himself and the world, much as Catholics venerate saints under the one God. The lwa derive from African deities and are organized into nanchon, nations, and rites: the Rada lwa, from the Fon-Aja region, are the cool, benevolent, ancestral spirits; the Petwo lwa are hot, fierce, and associated with fire, resistance, and the hard magic forged in slavery; the Gede are the ribald, irreverent spirits of death and the ancestors. Each lwa has its own colours, foods, symbols (the veve drawn on the ground), and corresponding Catholic saint. The central ritual act is possession: during ceremonies led by a priest (oungan) or priestess (manbo), a lwa 'mounts' or 'rides' a devotee, temporarily displacing their consciousness, so the spirit can speak, heal, counsel, and be present in the community. The Bois Caiman ceremony of 1791 helped ignite the Haitian Revolution, the only successful large-scale slave revolt in history.

sources.title: Haitian Vodou
sources.mla: "Haitian Vodou." Open Encyclopedia of Anthropology, https://www.anthroencyclopedia.com/entry/haitian-vodou. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Bondye
gods.description: The supreme creator God, omnipotent but distant, who made the world and the lwa and stands above all. Vodou is understood by its practitioners as monotheistic, with Bondye as the one God; but because he is remote, no shrines are built to him and no offerings made, and ritual attention goes entirely to the lwa he created to act as intermediaries.

gods.name: The Rada lwa
gods.description: The cool, benevolent, largely ancestral spirits, derived from the Fon-Aja region of West Africa. They include Papa Legba, the old man who opens the gate between worlds and must be greeted first; Danbala the serpent and his wife Ayida-Weddo the rainbow, the oldest and most revered; and Erzulie Freda, the lwa of love and beauty. Approached with white and cool offerings.

gods.name: The Petwo lwa
gods.description: The hot, fierce, and forceful spirits, forged in the crucible of slavery and associated with fire, aggression, and the power of resistance, as against the coolness of the Rada. Erzulie Dantor, fierce protective mother, is among the most important. It was a Petwo ceremony that is said to have launched the Haitian Revolution.

gods.name: The Gede
gods.description: The spirits of death and the ancestors, ribald, irreverent, and comic, who guard the cemetery and the crossing into death but also affirm life through raucous humour and sexuality. Baron Samedi, in his top hat and dark glasses, presides over the dead. They are honoured especially around the Catholic feast of the Dead.

haitianVoodoo.godsNote: Vodou understands itself as monotheistic, with the lwa as created intermediaries under the one God Bondye, comparable in structure to Catholic veneration of saints, not as independent gods. It is a decentralized oral tradition with much regional and family variation, and it has been persistently misrepresented and sensationalized ('voodoo') in outside popular culture. Louisiana Voodoo is a related but distinct North American tradition.

hoodoo.name: Hoodoo
hoodoo.description: Hoodoo, also called rootwork or conjure, is not a religion in the way Vodou or Santeria is, but a tradition of African American folk magic that developed among enslaved people in the American South, principally from the practices of Central and West African peoples, above all the Kongo, blended over time with Native American herbal knowledge, European folk magic, and, crucially, the Protestant Christianity that most of its practitioners held. This is the key difference from the Caribbean and Brazilian traditions: hoodoo generally has no pantheon of African deities and no possession by spirits. Most practitioners are Christian, and the God of the Bible, Jesus, and the biblical figures are the spiritual powers invoked; the Book of Psalms in particular is used as a magical text, individual psalms recited for specific purposes, and the practitioner (a rootworker, conjure doctor, or two-headed doctor) works within a broadly Christian frame. What makes it hoodoo rather than ordinary prayer is the underlying African-derived understanding of spiritual power residing in material things, and the practical, results-oriented character of the work: it exists to solve concrete problems, love, money, luck, protection, health, justice, legal cases, and revenge. Its techniques centre on the manipulation of natural and household materials, roots, herbs, minerals, animal parts, graveyard dirt, personal effects, made up into mojo bags (also called hands or tobies), oils, powders (like goofer dust), washes, and candles, applied through rituals of dressing, laying tricks, and cleansing. Ancestor veneration and work at the crossroads, a Kongo-derived sacred site, recur. Hoodoo was carried across the country in the Great Migration and remains a living practice.

gods.name: God
gods.domain: The supreme power, the source of the work
gods.description: Hoodoo is practised largely within a Protestant Christian frame, and its supreme power is the biblical God, invoked directly. The Bible itself is treated as the greatest book of magic, and the work is understood by most practitioners as operating through God's power, not against it.

gods.name: Jesus and the biblical figures
gods.description: Jesus, together with biblical figures such as Moses (a great worker of wonders in the hoodoo imagination) and the saints, are called upon as spiritual powers. The Psalms are recited as spells for particular ends, and scripture is woven directly into the ritual work.

gods.name: The ancestors
gods.description: The dead are honoured and enlisted, an African-derived element that persists strongly. Graveyard dirt is a key material, gathered with proper payment and respect, and the crossroads, a Kongo-derived sacred meeting-place of worlds, is where certain workings and pacts are made.

hoodoo.godsNote: Hoodoo is a system of folk magic, not a religion with its own pantheon; it is practised mostly by Christians within a Christian framework and has no deities of its own and, unlike Vodou or Santeria, generally no spirit possession. It draws chiefly on Kongo and other African spiritual traditions, blended with Native American, European, and biblical elements. It should not be confused with Vodou, though outsiders often conflate them.

inuit.name:
Inuit
Shamanism
inuit.description: Inuit religion is the traditional belief system of the Inuit of the Arctic, from Chukotka and Alaska across northern Canada to Greenland, and it is shaped through and through by life in the harshest inhabited environment on earth. It has no formal organization, no creator enthroned above the world, no pantheon of ruling gods handing down commandments, and no system of divine reward and punishment. What it has instead is a universe saturated with spirit and governed by taboo. Every living thing, and many objects, possesses a soul, the anirniq or 'breath', which is freed at death and can take revenge if wronged. Because human food consists entirely of the souls of animals, the whole moral order rests on treating prey with respect and observing the correct rituals over their flesh and bones; to offend an animal's soul is to risk starvation for the whole community. The great mediating figure is the angakkuq, the shaman, who through trance and helper-spirits travels to the spirit world, flying to the moon or descending to the sea floor, to discover which taboo has been broken when the hunt fails or sickness strikes, and to negotiate its repair. The most important spirit-being is Sedna, the Sea Woman, once a mortal girl thrown into the sea by her father, who cut off her fingers as she clung to the boat; her severed fingers became the seals, walruses, and whales, and she now rules the sea animals from the ocean floor. When people break taboos she withholds the game in her tangled hair, and only a shaman who descends to comb it out can release the animals again. Other powers include Sila, the impersonal spirit of the air and weather, and the Moon Man.

sources.title: Inuit Mythology
sources.mla: "Inuit Mythology." EBSCO Research Starters, https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/religion-and-philosophy/inuit-mythology. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Sedna
gods.domain: The sea, marine animals, the hunt
gods.description: The mistress of the sea and its animals, the closest thing Inuit religion has to a central figure. Once a mortal girl, she was cast into the sea by her father, who cut off her fingers as she clung to the gunwale; the fingers became the first seals, walruses, and whales. She does not rule so much as withhold: when taboos are broken she keeps the game entangled in her hair, and a shaman must descend to comb it out to release the hunt.

gods.name: Sila
gods.domain: The air, weather, the sky, life-breath
gods.description: The impersonal spirit or power of the air, weather, and sky, sometimes described as a kind of world-soul or intellect pervading everything, comparable to a life-force. The anirniit, the breath-souls of individuals, are considered part of Sila and borrowed from it. In some groups Sila or Pinga governs the land animals, especially caribou.

gods.name: The Moon Man
gods.description: A cosmic being associated with the moon, generally benevolent toward humans and their souls, connected with hunting and, in some traditions, with fertility and the welfare of the dead as they travel to the celestial realms.

gods.name: The tuurngait
gods.domain: The spirit world, aid and harm
gods.description: Spirits unconnected to physical bodies, which may be helpers summoned by the angakkuq in times of need, or malevolent beings responsible for bad hunts, broken tools, and sickness, and capable of possessing people. A shaman recruits the good ones and fights or wards off the bad.

gods.name: The angakkuq
gods.description: Not a deity but the essential religious specialist: the shaman who mediates between the community and the spirit world, travelling in trance to Sedna's home or to the moon, diagnosing which taboo has caused misfortune, healing the sick, and negotiating with the spirits. The role has largely disappeared with Christianization.

inuit.godsNote: Inuit religion has no creator god, no ruling pantheon, and no system of divine reward or punishment; the anthropologist's guide Aua famously told Knud Rasmussen 'We do not believe. We fear.' It is an animist and shamanic tradition governed by taboo, with much internal variation across the Arctic. Most Inuit today are Christian, though traditional beliefs persist alongside.

plateau.name:
Plateau
Indians
Shamanism
plateau.description: The shamanic religion of the Plateau peoples, of the high country between the Rockies and the Cascades drained by the Columbia and Fraser rivers, Nez Perce, Yakama, Flathead, Kootenai, Salish, and others, rests on the pursuit of personal spiritual power through the guardian spirit quest. The world was understood to be full of spirits, in living and non-living things alike, some good and some dangerous, and the most important religious event in a person's life was to acquire one as a personal guardian. In adolescence, and sometimes in childhood, a young person went out alone to a remote and often sacred place, marked by cairns, cliffs, or rock art where others had gone before, purified by sweat-bathing and fasting, to keep a vigil until a guardian spirit appeared, usually first in human form and then revealing an animal or other identity. The spirit granted a special power or talent, hunting, fishing, healing, or war, together with a song and often a dance, and a set of taboos the recipient had to observe on pain of losing the power. The power was meant to benefit the whole community, not just the individual. Characteristically, the young person then forgot the encounter, and the spirit returned only years later in maturity, taking possession of the person during the Winter Spirit Dance, the great communal ceremony at which people personified their guardians in song and dance and the medicine people identified the nature of each spirit. Coyote is the central figure of Plateau myth, the transformer who brought the salmon up the rivers each spring and shaped the world and its people. In the nineteenth century, prophets such as Smohalla founded revitalization movements, the Washani or Dreamer religion, teaching a return to traditional ways.

sources.title: The Plateau Indian Vision Quest
sources.mla: "The Plateau Indian Vision Quest." Daily Kos, https://www.dailykos.com/stories/2020/3/19/1928351/community/Indians-101-The-Plateau-Indian-vision-quest/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The guardian spirits
gods.description: The individual spirit-helpers at the heart of Plateau religion, acquired through the solitary vision quest. Each grants a specific power, a song, and a dance, together with taboos to keep the power alive, and returns to possess its human partner during the Winter Spirit Dance. Almost everyone traditionally sought and held one, and the power was to serve the community.
gods.imageQuery: Plateau Columbia River landscape vision quest

gods.name: Coyote
gods.description: The central figure of Plateau mythology, the transformer and trickster who shaped the world and its people into their present form and who, in many tellings, was responsible for bringing the salmon up the rivers each spring, the mainstay of Plateau life. A creator and a fool at once, teaching through both.

gods.name: Nami Piap
gods.description: The Creator recognized in the Plateau prophetic tradition, especially in Smohalla's Washani religion, who made the world, assigned peoples to their lands, and provided sustenance through Mother Earth, and who commanded, through the dreamer-prophets, that the people keep to traditional ways and not divide or disturb the land.

gods.name: Mother Earth
gods.description: The earth as sacred provider, central to Plateau prophetic teaching, which held that the land must not be carved into parcels or torn up by farming, since it was the source of fish and game given by the Creator, a doctrine that put the Dreamer religion directly at odds with U.S. land policy.

plateau.godsNote: Plateau religion is decentralized and varies among its many peoples; it centres on personal guardian-spirit power rather than a fixed pantheon. The nineteenth-century Dreamer or Washani movements, led by prophets like Smohalla, were revitalization responses to colonization and were suppressed by the U.S. government. The Winter Spirit Dance is still practised. Some knowledge is held privately.

plains.name:
Plains
Indians
Shamanism
plains.description: The shamanic religion of the Plains peoples, of which Lakota (Sioux) practice is the best documented, centres on Wakan Tanka, usually rendered the Great Spirit or Great Mystery but better left untranslated, the sacred, animating power that pervades and unifies all of creation. Wakan Tanka is not a single anthropomorphic deity so much as the totality of the sacred, at once one and many, comprising a range of powers and beings, some existing before the creation of the earth and some after. The root wakan, sacred, names the potential of anything to become holy; everything in the world, stones, animals, winds, is a manifestation of this power, and all beings are therefore relatives, bound in a single sacred web, an idea captured in the phrase mitakuye oyasin, 'all my relations'. Two ritual complexes define the tradition. The vision quest, hanblecheyapi or 'crying for a vision', sends an individual alone to a remote sacred place to fast and pray for several days, seeking contact with a guardian spirit and a vision to guide their life, a practice found across the Plains and beyond. The Sun Dance, wiwanyang wacipi, is the great communal ceremony, held at the summer gathering, in which dancers pledge flesh and endurance, some piercing their chests and tethering themselves to the sacred cottonwood tree at the centre of the dance circle, dancing for days without food or water in sacrifice and prayer for the renewal of the people and all life. The sacred pipe, the chanunpa, brought to the Lakota by White Buffalo Calf Woman, is the central holy object; its smoke carries prayers to Wakan Tanka, and it is used in all major rites. The holy people, the wicasa wakan, conduct the ceremonies.

gods.name: Wakan Tanka
gods.domain: The sacred totality, the animating power of all
gods.description: The Great Mystery, the sacred power that pervades and unifies the whole of creation, at once one and many. Not a single anthropomorphic god but the sum of all that is holy, comprising sixteen aspects in some Lakota reckonings, arranged in fours, including the sun, sky, earth, and rock. All things are manifestations of it, and all beings are therefore relatives.

gods.name: White Buffalo Calf Woman
gods.domain: Sacred teaching, the pipe, the rites
gods.description: The sacred woman who appeared to the Lakota in a time of hunger, bringing the chanunpa, the sacred pipe, and teaching the Seven Sacred Rites that form the basis of Lakota spirituality. She promised to return and, as she departed, turned into a white buffalo calf. The most revered figure in the tradition after Wakan Tanka itself.
gods.imageQuery: White Buffalo Calf Woman Lakota sacred

gods.name: The sacred pipe
gods.description: Not a deity but the central sacred object: the pipe whose bowl is the feminine earth and whose stem the masculine sky, whose smoke carries prayers directly to Wakan Tanka. Smoking it is itself prayer, and the same Lakota word means both. It is used to open and seal every important rite and vow.

gods.name: The four winds and the directions
gods.domain: The cardinal directions, the sacred hoop
gods.description: The wind, Tate, and his four sons the directional winds, part of the ordered structure of the wakan powers. The four directions, each with its colour, animal, and teaching, organize the Sacred Hoop, the circle that expresses the cyclical, interconnected nature of all life.

gods.name: Iktomi
gods.description: The trickster and culture-hero, who named all things and taught culture to humanity, but who remains on earth to deceive people for his own benefit. Like tricksters across Native North America, he is both a bringer of knowledge and a cautionary figure whose transgressions teach by negative example.

plains.godsNote: Wakan Tanka is best understood not as a personal creator god but as the sacred, animating power of the universe, simultaneously singular and plural. Lakota classifications of the wakan powers vary, often deriving from individual visions. Plains religion was suppressed for decades under U.S. policy; the Sun Dance was banned and has since been revived. Some ceremonial knowledge is held only by holy people and not shared publicly.

mississippi.name:
Mississippian
Shamanism
mississippi.description: The Mississippian tradition names the culture of the mound-building peoples who dominated the American Southeast and mid-continent from about 800 CE to European contact, and its religion is reconstructed almost entirely from archaeology, since no Mississippian sacred text or unbroken oral tradition survives to name its gods. What the record shows is monumental: great earthen platform mounds arranged around plazas at ceremonial centres, the largest of them Cahokia near modern St. Louis, which at its height was among the largest cities in the world north of Mexico, dominated by a mound covering more ground at its base than the Great Pyramid. Atop the mounds stood temples and the residences of a priestly-chiefly elite, and the whole layout expresses a religion of sacred kingship, in which chiefs mediated between the community and the cosmos. Running through Mississippian art, on engraved shell, embossed copper, and pottery, is a shared body of imagery archaeologists call the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex, and from it, together with the beliefs of the historic-era Southeastern peoples who descend from these cultures, a cosmology can be partly reconstructed: a three-tiered universe of an Upper World of order, a Lower World of chaos and fertility, and This World between them, linked by a central axis. Recurring supernatural figures appear, though their original names are lost: a winged 'Birdman' or falcon-warrior associated with the Upper World, the sun, and sacred rulership; and an Underwater Panther or Great Serpent, a horned, water-dwelling being of the Lower World. The cross-in-circle and the sun were central symbols, and a sun cult and a sacred fire were probably at the heart of the ceremonial life.

gods.name: The Birdman
gods.domain: The Upper World, the sun, sacred rulership, war
gods.description: A recurring supernatural figure in Mississippian art, a human-falcon composite associated with the Upper World, the sun, sky, and the sacred authority of chiefs and warriors. Depicted on engraved shell and embossed copper plates, most famously the Rogan plates from Etowah. The original name is unknown; the identification is inferred from imagery and from historic Southeastern beliefs.

gods.name: The Underwater Panther
gods.domain: The Lower World, water, chaos, fertility
gods.description: A powerful being of the Lower World in the reconstructed Mississippian cosmos, a horned, water-dwelling creature combining serpent, panther, and other features, associated with water, the underworld, chaos, and fertility, and set in opposition to the Upper World's Birdman. Known from historic Southeastern and Great Lakes traditions and read back into the ancient imagery.

gods.name: The Sun
gods.description: The sun and its earthly counterpart, the sacred fire, probably central to Mississippian religion, as they were among historic Southeastern peoples like the Natchez, whose paramount chief was called the Great Sun and was carried so his feet would not touch the ground. The cross-in-circle symbol, widespread in the art, likely represents the sacred fire or the sun and the four directions.
gods.imageQuery: Mississippian cross in circle sun symbol

mississippi.godsNote: Mississippian religion is reconstructed from archaeology and from the beliefs of the historic Southeastern peoples who descend from these cultures; no original names for its supernatural beings survive, and the figures here are modern scholarly labels ('Birdman', 'Underwater Panther') inferred from the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex imagery. The reconstruction is real but partial and debated. Descendant nations include the Muscogee, Chickasaw, Choctaw, Natchez, and others.

pueblo.name:
Pueblo
Shamanism
pueblo.description: Pueblo religion, of the Hopi, Zuni, and the Rio Grande and Acoma-Laguna Keres, Tewa, Tiwa, and Towa peoples of the Southwest, is among the oldest continuously practised religious traditions in North America, descended in part from the Ancestral Puebloans and centred on the maintenance of cosmic balance and, above all, the bringing of rain to a desert land. Its most distinctive feature is the katsina (kachina) system. The katsinam are supernatural beings, spirits of clouds, ancestors, animals, and natural forces, hundreds of them, who once lived among the people and, when they departed, taught a few faithful ones to make their masks and impersonate them. During the ceremonial season, roughly from the winter solstice to midsummer, masked and costumed dancers become the katsinam, embodying them in the plaza and kiva dances, bringing blessings, rain, fertility, and good health, disciplining and instructing children, and carrying the community's prayers. The ceremonies are conducted from the kiva, the subterranean chamber descended from Ancestral Puebloan architecture, entered by ladder, with a sipapu, the small hole symbolizing the place of emergence from the worlds below. Pueblo cosmology holds that the people emerged from earlier worlds beneath this one, led up by spirit beings, and Corn Mothers and the sacred role of maize are central, corn being both sustenance and a sacred substance. Religion is inseparable from the calendar and from the whole life of the community, organized through clans, societies, and ceremonial offices. The Pueblo peoples are deliberately protective of their religious knowledge, much of which is closed to outsiders, a reticence sharpened by centuries of Spanish suppression and the Pueblo Revolt of 1680.

sources.title: Kachina Cult
sources.mla: "Kachina Cult." World History Encyclopedia, https://www.worldhistory.org/Kachina_Cult/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: The katsinam
gods.description: The hundreds of supernatural beings at the centre of Pueblo religion, spirits of clouds, rain, ancestors, animals, and natural forces, who once lived among the people and now visit the villages during the ceremonial season, embodied by masked dancers who become them. They bring rain and fertility, carry prayers, and instruct and discipline the young. The local set of katsinam differs from pueblo to pueblo.

gods.name: The Corn Mothers
gods.description: The maternal creator-figures associated with corn, who in many Pueblo traditions brought the people up from the underworlds and gave them maize, both their food and a sacred substance. Corn and cornmeal are central to nearly every Pueblo rite, and the ear of corn is a profound emblem of life.

gods.name: The spirits of emergence
gods.description: Pueblo cosmology holds that the people emerged from earlier worlds beneath this one, guided upward by spirit beings, entering the present world through a place of emergence, symbolized by the sipapu, the small hole in the kiva floor. The ancestors and the beings who led the emergence remain part of the sacred order.

gods.name: The Sun
gods.domain: The sun, life, the daily order
gods.description: The sun, addressed as father in many Pueblo traditions, an object of daily prayer and cornmeal offering and central to the ceremonial calendar, whose solstices mark the great turning points of the ritual year.

pueblo.godsNote: Pueblo peoples deliberately restrict access to their religious knowledge, and much of it, including the identities and meanings of specific katsinam and the details of ceremonies, is not shared with outsiders. This reticence reflects both the sacred, secret character of the knowledge and centuries of suppression under Spanish rule that provoked the Pueblo Revolt of 1680. The living tradition descends in part from the Ancestral Puebloans (see anasazi).

iroquois.name:
Iroquois
Shamanism
iroquois.description: The religion of the Haudenosaunee, the Six Nations confederacy of the Mohawk, Oneida, Onondaga, Cayuga, Seneca, and later Tuscarora, is a woodland tradition in which the sacred is woven through all of daily life rather than separated from it, and whose fundamental posture toward the world is thanksgiving. Every ceremony opens and closes with the Thanksgiving Address, a long recitation giving thanks in turn to the earth, waters, plants, trees, animals, the Three Sisters, the winds, the thunderers, the sun, moon, and stars, and the spirit-forces above. Central concepts include Orenda, a spiritual power or life-force believed to flow through all things, people, animals, and objects alike, which can be harnessed through respect and right conduct. The cosmos begins with Sky Woman, who fell from the sky world through a hole and was caught by birds and set on the back of a great turtle, which became the earth, Turtle Island. Her descendants include a pair of twins, one who shaped the good things of the world and the sun, and one who made the monstrous and harmful things, a duality of creative and destructive forces. Among the most important spirit-beings are the Three Sisters, the spirits of corn, beans, and squash, the staple crops grown together; the Thunderers who bring rain and fight underwater serpents; and the Great Spirit, a creator and source of good, who guides people indirectly. Prayers are carried upward by the smoke of burning tobacco. Six seasonal festivals mark the agricultural year, and the medicine societies, above all the False Face Society with its carved wooden masks, conduct healing ceremonies. In the late eighteenth century the Seneca prophet Handsome Lake founded a reform movement, the Longhouse Religion, still followed today.

gods.name: The Great Spirit
gods.description: The creator and the source of the good forces in nature, who made human beings, plants, and animals, and who guides the lives of people indirectly rather than by direct intervention. Ordinary humans communicate with the higher powers through the mediating spirits and through tobacco smoke.

gods.name: Sky Woman
gods.domain: The creation of the earth, the sky world
gods.description: The primordial woman who fell from the sky world through a hole left by an uprooted tree, was caught by water birds, and was placed on the back of a great turtle, whose shell, spread with mud from the ocean floor, became the earth, Turtle Island. The ancestress of humanity in Haudenosaunee cosmology.
gods.imageQuery: Sky Woman turtle island Haudenosaunee art

gods.name: The Good Twin and Evil Twin
gods.domain: Creation and its opposite, order and disorder
gods.description: The grandsons of Sky Woman, whose contest shapes the world: the good twin creates the sun from his mother's face, the good plants and animals, and heals and teaches; the evil twin makes the monstrous, noxious, and harmful things. Their duality runs through all of existence, a balance of life-giving and life-diminishing forces.

gods.name: The Three Sisters
gods.description: The spirits of the three staple crops, grown together in the same mound so that the corn supports the beans and the squash shades the ground. Called 'Our Life' or 'Our Supporters', they are honoured in the planting, green corn, and harvest festivals as inseparable sustainers of the people.
gods.imageQuery: Three Sisters corn beans squash agriculture

gods.name: Heno
gods.description: The spirit of thunder, addressed as Grandfather, who brings the rains that nourish the crops and hurls chert at evil spirits and the underwater serpents that threaten humanity. He is said to live in a cave behind Niagara Falls.

gods.name: The False Faces
gods.description: The powerful forest spirits represented by the carved wooden masks of the False Face Society, whose members conduct healing ceremonies. A mask is carved from a living tree after a prayer and tobacco offering, and the twisted features embody the spirit that revealed itself to the carver.
gods.imageQuery: Haudenosaunee False Face mask carved wood

iroquois.godsNote: The Haudenosaunee recognize a Great Spirit or Creator but the tradition is not monotheistic in the Western sense; it centres on a large family of spirit-forces and on Orenda, the life-power flowing through all things. Beliefs vary among the Six Nations. Much of the tradition continues today, including the Longhouse Religion founded by the prophet Handsome Lake around 1799. Out of respect, some ceremonial knowledge is not shared publicly.
imageQueries: Three Sisters agriculture corn beans squash

macumba.name: Macumba
macumba.description: Macumba is a term that has to be handled carefully, because it is used in at least two very different ways. In everyday Brazilian speech it is a loose, and usually pejorative, catch-all for Afro-Brazilian religion in general, especially Umbanda and Candomble, and for their ritual offerings left at crossroads; used this way it carries the stigma and the accusations of 'black magic' that outsiders and hostile Christians have long directed at these religions, and practitioners themselves generally reject it, preferring to name their actual tradition, Candomble or Umbanda. In a narrower and more historical sense, Macumba refers to a specific set of Afro-Brazilian practices that developed in the Rio de Janeiro region in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries, out of Bantu (Angola and Kongo) roots blended with Catholicism, indigenous elements, and Kardecist Spiritism, a tradition that was one of the direct forerunners from which Umbanda crystallized around 1900 and out of which its more African-facing counterpart also drew. In this older Carioca form, Macumba centred, like its relatives, on the invocation and incorporation of spirits, deities of African origin and the spirits of the dead, through mediumship, drumming, dance, offerings, and possession, for purposes of healing, protection, guidance, and the working of the practitioner's will in the world. Because it was never a single unified church and because the label became a slur, Macumba is less well defined than Candomble or Umbanda, and much of what it named has been absorbed into, or continues alongside, those better-organized traditions.

gods.name: The African deities
gods.domain: The forces of nature, divine power
gods.description: The deities of African origin, in the Bantu-rooted Carioca tradition often the inkices of Kongo-Angola religion alongside the Yoruba orixas, governing the forces of nature and invoked through offerings and possession. As in the related traditions, understood under a single remote supreme God.

gods.name: The spirits of the dead
gods.description: The spirits of the dead, incorporated by mediums in the manner shared across Afro-Brazilian religion, who work for healing, protection, guidance, and the practitioner's aims. In the forms ancestral to Umbanda these develop into the familiar lines of pretos velhos, caboclos, and others.

gods.name: Exu
gods.domain: The crossroads, movement, force, the offerings
gods.description: The crossroads spirit and messenger, especially prominent in the Macumba imagination, at whose sites the characteristic offerings, candles, cachaca, farofa, are left. His association with the crossroads and with hard, direct magic made him the figure outsiders most often, and most wrongly, cast as diabolical.

macumba.godsNote: 'Macumba' is primarily a colloquial and pejorative Brazilian label for Afro-Brazilian religion generally, and practitioners reject it as a slur, preferring the names of their actual traditions (Candomble, Umbanda). Historically it also named a specific Bantu-rooted Carioca tradition ancestral to Umbanda. It was never a single organized church, and its content overlaps heavily with, and has largely been absorbed into, Umbanda and Candomble.

candomble.name: Candomble
candomble.description: Candomble is the Afro-Brazilian religion that developed among enslaved Africans in Brazil, above all in Bahia, through the same process of reconstructing African deity-worship under Catholic cover that produced Santeria and Vodou elsewhere in the Americas. Its principal root is Yoruba, but it drew also on Fon (Jeje) and Bantu (Angola) traditions, and it crystallized into distinct 'nations' (nacoes) reflecting these origins. Above all stands Olorun (Olodumare), the supreme, remote creator, source of axe, the sacred vital force. Ritual devotion is directed to the orixas, the deities who govern nature and human destiny, the Brazilian counterparts of the Cuban orichas and, through common Yoruba origin, of the Haitian lwa: Xango of thunder, Iemanja of the sea, Oxum of the rivers, Ogum of iron, Oxala (Obatala) the serene creator of bodies, Exu the trickster of the crossroads honoured first, Iansa (Oya) of wind and storms, and others. Each orixa was matched with a Catholic saint, and each has its colours, foods, sacred rhythms drummed on the atabaques, and dances. Every devotee is understood to be a child of a particular orixa who governs their nature. Candomble is deeply initiatory: through elaborate rites conducted in the terreiro, the temple-community led by a mother-of-saints (mae de santo) or father-of-saints (pai de santo), initiates are consecrated to their orixa and become able to receive them. The central act of worship is spirit possession, in which the orixa 'descends' upon a fully initiated devotee during the drumming and dancing and is present, dancing, among the community. Divination, especially the cowrie-shell system, guides the religious life.

sources.title: The Meeting of Paths: Allan Kardec, Umbanda & Candomble, Santo Daime, and Ayahuasca in Brazil
sources.mla: "The Meeting of Paths: Allan Kardec, Umbanda & Candomble, Santo Daime, and Ayahuasca in Brazil." MaraVilha, https://www.maravilharetreat.com/blog/maravilha-retreat/maravilharetreat/blog-page-url/ayahuasca. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Olorun
gods.description: The supreme God and creator, source of axe, the sacred energy that flows through all existence. Remote and transcendent, Olorun does not receive direct worship; the orixas he set over the world are the focus of devotion, mediating between the creator and humanity.

gods.name: Exu
gods.description: The orixa of the crossroads and of communication, the messenger who carries offerings and prayers between humans and the other orixas, and who must be honoured first so that the way is open. A dynamic, ambivalent trickster-figure, wrongly demonized by outsiders who confused him with the Christian devil.

gods.name: Xango
gods.description: The orixa of thunder, lightning, and fire, and of justice, a divinized king wielding the double axe, commanding and passionate. Among the most important orixas in Brazil, syncretized variously with Saint Jerome or Saint Barbara.

gods.name: Iemanja
gods.description: The great mother orixa of the sea, one of the most beloved figures in Brazil, honoured every New Year and on her feast day when devotees launch flowers and gifts into the ocean. Identified with the Virgin Mary in several forms. Her worship extends far beyond Candomble into Brazilian popular culture.
gods.imageQuery: Iemanja orixa sea goddess Brazil offering

gods.name: Oxum
gods.domain: Rivers, fresh water, love, beauty, wealth
gods.description: The orixa of fresh water, rivers, love, beauty, and wealth, sweet and generous, associated with gold and honey. The Brazilian counterpart of the Cuban Ochun, and among the most cherished female orixas.

gods.name: Oxala
gods.domain: Creation of human bodies, purity, peace
gods.description: The serene, white-clad orixa of creation, purity, and peace, who fashioned human bodies, the eldest and most venerable of the orixas, father-figure of the pantheon. Identified with Jesus, especially as Our Lord of Bonfim in Bahia.

gods.name: Iansa
gods.description: The orixa of wind, storms, and lightning, a fierce warrior woman and companion of Xango, who also commands the spirits of the dead. Dynamic, powerful, and courageous, syncretized with Saint Barbara.
gods.imageQuery: Iansa Oya orixa wind storm Brazil

candomble.godsNote: Candomble is an initiatory Afro-Brazilian religion organized into 'nations' (Ketu/Yoruba, Jeje/Fon, Angola/Bantu) reflecting different African roots. The orixas are understood as intermediaries under the one remote creator, and their old identification with Catholic saints was originally protective. Candomble has faced long persecution in Brazil, both historical and recent, including attacks by some evangelical groups, and its sacred knowledge is reserved to initiates.

santeria.name: Santeria
santeria.description: Santeria, more properly called Regla de Ocha or Lucumi, developed in Cuba among enslaved Yoruba people, who reconstructed the orisha religion of West Africa under the cover of Spanish Catholicism. As in Haiti, the enslaved matched their deities to Catholic saints, giving the tradition its popular name, 'the way of the saints', and allowing worship to continue behind an acceptable facade. At its head is Olodumare (also Olofi, Olorun), the supreme creator, who like the African high god is remote and does not receive direct cult. Ritual life centres on the orishas (orichas), the deities of the Yoruba pantheon who govern the forces of nature and human life and mediate between Olodumare and humanity. Each orisha has a distinct personality, domain, colours, numbers, foods, rhythms, and dances, and each is identified with a Catholic saint: Chango, orisha of thunder, fire, and virility, with Saint Barbara; Yemaya, mother of the sea, with the Virgin of Regla; Ochun, of rivers, love, and sweetness, with the Virgin of Charity, Cuba's patroness; Ogun, of iron and war, with Saint Peter; Eleggua, the trickster who opens and closes the roads and must be honoured first, with the Holy Child of Atocha or Saint Anthony. Devotees develop a personal relationship with the orisha who 'owns their head'. The tradition works through drumming and dance ceremonies in which orishas possess initiates, through animal sacrifice, and through a sophisticated divination system, above all Ifa, read by a babalawo, and the simpler cowrie-shell divination, dilogun. Initiation as a priest, becoming a santero or santera, is a costly, elaborate, and lifelong commitment.

sources.title: How Religious Syncretism Spawned Haitian Vodou
sources.mla: "How Religious Syncretism Spawned Haitian Vodou." Lore of Ancestors, https://www.loreofancestors.com/articles/how-religious-syncretism-spawned-haitian-vodou/. Accessed 14 July 2026.

gods.name: Olodumare
gods.description: The supreme creator God, source of ashe, the divine life-force that animates the cosmos. Like the West African high god, Olodumare is transcendent and remote, delegating the governance of the world and direct relationship with humanity to the orishas, and receiving no direct cult of his own.

gods.name: Eleggua
gods.description: The orisha who opens and closes the roads and the doorways between worlds, master of the crossroads and of chance, honoured first in every ceremony because nothing can proceed without him. Trickster and messenger, identified with the Holy Child of Atocha or Saint Anthony.

gods.name: Chango
gods.domain: Thunder, lightning, fire, virility, justice, drumming
gods.description: The orisha of thunder and lightning, fire, male virility, dance, and justice, a former king of Oyo divinized, passionate and powerful, wielding the double-headed axe. Syncretized with Saint Barbara. One of the most beloved and important orishas across the diaspora.

gods.name: Yemaya
gods.description: The great mother orisha, goddess of the sea and of maternity, source and sustainer of life, deep and nurturing but with the ocean's power. Identified with the Virgin of Regla and honoured in blues and whites.

gods.name: Ochun
gods.description: The orisha of the rivers, of love, beauty, sensuality, fertility, and gold, sweet and generous but formidable when wronged. Identified in Cuba with Our Lady of Charity of El Cobre, the patroness of the island. Honoured in yellow and gold with honey.

gods.name: Ogun
gods.domain: Iron, war, tools, labour, the forge
gods.description: The orisha of iron, war, and all who work with metal, the pathbreaker who clears the way with his machete, patron of blacksmiths, soldiers, and labourers. Fierce and hard-working. Identified with Saint Peter or Saint George.

gods.name: Obatala
gods.domain: Purity, wisdom, creation of human bodies, the head
gods.description: The orisha of purity, peace, and wisdom, the eldest, who by Olodumare's charge fashioned human bodies and is lord of the head and of clear thought. Dressed in white, calm and cool, the father-figure of the orishas. Identified with Our Lady of Mercy.

santeria.godsNote: Santeria (Regla de Ocha / Lucumi) is a reconstruction of Yoruba orisha religion under Cuban Catholic cover; the identification of orishas with Catholic saints was originally protective camouflage, and practitioners' understanding of how deeply the two are fused varies. It is initiatory and largely oral, with sacred knowledge reserved to initiates. Animal sacrifice is a protected religious practice, affirmed for U.S. Santeria by the Supreme Court in 1993.

aztec.name:
Aztec
Polytheism
aztec.description: Aztec religion was a syncretistic system that absorbed the gods and cosmology of earlier Mesoamerica, above all the Maya and Toltec, and organized them around a single overwhelming demand: the sun, and the world, had to be fed with human blood. The Aztecs, or Mexica, believed the present world was the fifth in a series of creations, each previous sun destroyed by catastrophe, and that this one too would end unless the gods were sustained. The gods had sacrificed themselves to set the sun in motion at Teotihuacan; humanity owed the same debt in return. Hence human sacrifice on a scale unmatched in the Americas, offered by extracting the still-beating heart, together with autosacrificial bloodletting by rulers and priests. The pantheon was vast. At its summit stood Huitzilopochtli, the tribal war and sun god of the Mexica, elevated by the fifteenth-century reformer Tlacaelel to equal rank with the older high gods and enshrined at the Templo Mayor in Tenochtitlan; Tlaloc, the ancient rain god, shared the summit of that great pyramid with him, red shrine and blue, war and fertility side by side. Tezcatlipoca, 'Smoking Mirror', god of night, sorcery, and fate, was often reckoned the most powerful of all, the capricious enemy of Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent, patron of priests and the wind and the inventor of the calendar. Behind them all, a remote creative duality, Ometeotl, both male and female, from whom the four world-directional gods issued. Religion was inseparable from the elaborate 260-day and 365-day calendars, which governed a ceaseless round of festivals, each god and temple with its own priesthood.

gods.name: Huitzilopochtli
gods.description: The tribal god of the Mexica, of war and the noonday sun, who required nourishment with human blood to keep rising and hold back the darkness. Originally minor, he was raised by Tlacaelel's reforms to the top rank of the pantheon and enshrined on the red side of the Templo Mayor. He was shown as a hummingbird or eagle and carried the fire-serpent Xiuhcoatl.

gods.name: Tlaloc
gods.description: The ancient rain god, inherited from far earlier Mesoamerica, whose cult was among the most important and widespread in Mexico. He shared the summit of the Templo Mayor with Huitzilopochtli, his blue shrine representing fertility and growth against the war god's red. Children were sacrificed to him, their tears thought to presage rain.

gods.name: Tezcatlipoca
gods.description: Often considered the most powerful god of all: the god of the night sky, of sorcery, destiny, and the north, called 'the Enemy' and 'the Enemy of Both Sides' for his affinity with discord. He was the great adversary of Quetzalcoatl, and a young man impersonated him for a year before being sacrificed at his festival.

gods.name: Quetzalcoatl
gods.domain: Wind, wisdom, priesthood, the calendar, life
gods.description: The Feathered Serpent, of great antiquity in Mesoamerica: patron of priests, inventor of the calendar and books, protector of craftsmen, associated with the planet Venus, and in Aztec myth the re-creator of humanity, who descended to the underworld to retrieve the bones of the dead. As a culture hero he was contrasted with the darker gods, and legend held he would return.

gods.name: Tonatiuh
gods.description: The sun god of the present, fifth age, to whom victims' hearts were commonly offered so that he would continue his journey across the sky. His face is at the centre of the famous Aztec calendar stone.

gods.name: Ometeotl
gods.description: The remote primal duality, both male and female, dwelling in the highest heaven, from whom the four directional creator gods, Tezcatlipoca, Quetzalcoatl, Xipe Totec, and Huitzilopochtli, issued. Rarely worshipped directly, more a philosophical first principle than a god of cult.
gods.imageQuery: Ometeotl Aztec dual creator deity codex

gods.name: Coatlicue
gods.description: The earth mother, mother of Huitzilopochtli and of the stars, depicted with a skirt of serpents and a necklace of hearts, hands, and skulls, powerful and terrible in her fertility. The colossal statue of her is among the most famous survivals of Aztec sculpture.

gods.name: Xipe Totec
gods.domain: Agricultural renewal, spring, the shedding of skin
gods.description: The god of springtime and renewal, whose priests wore the flayed skins of sacrificial victims to symbolize the earth shedding its old husk as the new maize sprouts. One of the four directional creator gods.
gods.imageQuery: Xipe Totec flayed god Aztec sculpture

aztec.godsNote: Aztec religion was explicitly syncretistic, absorbing gods and cosmology from the Maya, Toltec, and other earlier peoples. The scale of human sacrifice is attested by both Spanish sources and archaeology at the Templo Mayor, though the numbers reported by conquistadors are debated.

incan.name:
Incan
Polytheism
incan.description: Inca religion was the highly organized state cult of the largest empire in the pre-Columbian Americas, blending nature worship, ancestor veneration, and a solar theology that placed the emperor at the centre of the cosmos. At its head, in the official cult, was Inti, the sun god, ranking deity of the pantheon, whose warmth ripened the crops and from whom the Sapa Inca, the emperor, claimed direct descent, which made sun-worship the state religion and the ruler a living solar son. Inti's great temple, the Coricancha in Cusco, was sheathed in gold, and his festival, Inti Raymi at the June solstice, is re-enacted in Peru to this day. Above or beside Inti, depending on the source and the region, stood Viracocha, the creator, a deity so sacred he was more often named by his attributes than directly, who made the sun, moon, and stars from Lake Titicaca and shaped humanity before vanishing into the sea with a promise to return. For ordinary farmers, though, the most important powers were closer to the ground: Pachamama, the earth mother, honoured with offerings of chicha and coca to secure the harvest, and the apus, the sacred mountain-lords whose shrines dotted the Andes. Mama Quilla, the moon, was Inti's sister and wife and governed the calendar; Illapa was the god of weather and thunder. The dead were central: the mummified bodies of past emperors were treated as living participants in state affairs, and offerings, including, in extremity, the child sacrifices of the capacocha rite on high mountain summits, were made to sustain the sacred order. The Inca permitted conquered peoples to keep their own gods within this framework.

gods.name: Inti
gods.domain: The sun, kingship, the state, the harvest
gods.description: The sun god and ranking deity of the official Inca pantheon, ancestor of the emperor and therefore the patron of the ruling dynasty and the state itself. Represented as a golden disk with a human face and rays. His temple, the gold-clad Coricancha, was the empire's most sacred site, and his solstice festival is still celebrated.
gods.imageQuery: Inti Inca sun god golden disk

gods.name: Viracocha
gods.domain: Creation, the origin of the world
gods.description: The creator god, so exalted that his true name was considered too sacred to speak, so he was called 'Light', 'Beginning', or 'Instructor'. He made the sun, moon, and stars from Lake Titicaca, fashioned humanity, taught the arts of civilization, and departed across the sea promising to return. More remote from daily affairs than Inti.
gods.imageQuery: Viracocha Inca creator god Tiwanaku gateway

gods.name: Pachamama
gods.description: The earth goddess, and for ordinary Andean people the most immediately important deity of all, since she governed the fertility of the fields. Honoured with libations of chicha and offerings of coca leaves poured onto the ground. Still venerated in the Andes today.

gods.name: Mama Quilla
gods.domain: The moon, the calendar, women, marriage
gods.description: The moon goddess, sister and wife of Inti, mother of the royal line, who governed the calendar and festivals and protected women. Silver was said to be her tears; a lunar eclipse meant a celestial animal was attacking her, and people made noise to drive it off.

gods.name: Illapa
gods.description: The god of weather, thunder, and lightning, of great importance to farmers dependent on the rains. He was imagined as a warrior in the sky who drew water from the celestial river of the Milky Way.

gods.name: The apus
gods.description: The sacred spirits of the mountains, powerful local lords honoured with shrines throughout the Andes and appealed to for protection and fortune. Along with Pachamama, the apus remain central to living Andean religion.

gods.name: The royal mummies
gods.description: Not gods, but the mummified bodies of dead emperors, kept in the Coricancha and their palaces, fed, clothed, consulted, and carried out to festivals as living participants in the affairs of the empire. Ancestor veneration was inseparable from Inca kingship and cosmology.

incan.godsNote: The Inca ran a state cult that tolerated the local gods of conquered peoples within its framework. Much of what survives comes through Spanish chroniclers, whose Catholic lens shaped the record, so the precise relationship between Inti and Viracocha, in particular, varies by source. The capacocha child sacrifices are confirmed by high-altitude archaeology.

umbanda.name: Umbanda
umbanda.description: Umbanda is a Brazilian religion that emerged in the early twentieth century, conventionally traced to the medium Zelio Fernandino de Moraes in the Rio de Janeiro area around 1908, and it is a genuinely Brazilian synthesis, blending the African orixa traditions of Candomble, the Catholicism of the colonizer, the Spiritism of the French writer Allan Kardec (with its doctrines of reincarnation, spiritual evolution, and communication with the dead), and indigenous Brazilian elements. Where Candomble looks toward Africa and reserves its deepest knowledge to initiates, Umbanda is more urban, more improvisational, more accessible, and oriented above all to charity and healing. It recognizes the one supreme God, Olorun or Zambi, identified with the Christian God, and honours the orixas beneath him, often equated with Catholic saints, but its distinctive and central practice concerns a different order of spirits: the spirits of the dead who work through mediums for the benefit of the living. Chief among these are the pretos velhos, the 'old black ones', the wise and compassionate spirits of enslaved African elders, full of patience and folk wisdom and healing; the caboclos, the proud, strong spirits of indigenous Brazilians, masters of the forest and of herbal medicine; and the ebos or crianças, child-spirits of playfulness and innocence.
